title: add mounted-path btrfs exact missing-xattr errno coverage for getxattr and removexattr
description: Survey finding: btrfs fuse_e2e currently proves mounted-path user xattr happy paths plus create/replace mode errors, but it does not freeze the exact public errno contract for missing getxattr and removexattr. ffs-fuse maps both missing-xattr cases to Linux ENODATA on the mounted path. Scope: add btrfs mounted-path probes that assert exact ENODATA for missing getxattr and removexattr, with unchanged file bytes and xattr state. Acceptance: btrfs E2E freezes exact errno and no-side-effect behavior for both missing-xattr operations.
close_reason: done

title: Refine bd-rchk0.5.6.1 with release-gate evidence downgrade proofs
description:
Supplemental refinement for bd-rchk0.5.6.1. Release gates need downgrade proofs as much as upgrade proofs: if evidence expires, becomes noisy, loses host capability, or is contradicted by a new failure, public feature state must move backward predictably. This bead is additive and avoids editing the existing dotted gate bead until bd-suf84 is fixed.

Required proof before close:
1. Unit tests cover feature-state downgrade transitions from validated to experimental, opt-in mutating to detection-only, supported to disabled, and ready to hidden when required evidence is stale, missing, threshold-failing, security-refused, unsafe-repair-refused, or capability-blocked.
2. Policy tests prove a hand-edited README or FEATURE_PARITY claim cannot override gate data, and generated wording changes when the controlling evidence downgrades.
3. E2E/integration smoke evaluates passing, missing-evidence, stale-evidence, threshold-failing, hostile-image, unsafe-repair, noisy-performance, and host-capability-skip bundles, then verifies public status and remediation output.
4. Logs include feature id, previous state, proposed state, final state, transition reason, controlling artifact hash, threshold value, observed value, remediation id, docs wording id, output paths, and reproduction command.
5. Any downgrade without a linked remediation bead or explicit non-goal rationale fails the evaluator so users are never left with a dead-end status.
close_reason: Completed release-gate downgrade proof refinement: lane risk_class policy, downgrade-specific unit coverage, 11/11 release-gate E2E scenarios, workspace check/clippy, UBS. E2E artifact: artifacts/e2e/20260503_085722_ffs_release_gate/result.json

title: Validate legacy verify-goldens wrapper against canonical golden gate docs
description: With no open backlog items and all remote cargo gates/tests passing, validate the remaining script/docs slice that converts scripts/verify-goldens.sh into a compatibility shim for scripts/verify_golden.sh and updates README/tests fixture docs accordingly. Scope: reserve the wrapper/docs files, verify the wrapper behavior and shell syntax, and fix any concrete mismatch discovered.
close_reason: Validated: wrapper shell syntax clean, legacy script forwards correctly, canonical golden gate passes end-to-end, docs references consistent

title: Add ext4 dx_hash kernel-reference conformance coverage
close_reason: Matched ext4 dx_hash behavior to kernel reference and restored htree conformance parity

title: Reject e2compr clusters whose holemap cannot consume decompressed bytes
description: Fresh-eyes follow-up around ext4 e2compr: after checksum verification and codec decode, decompress_e2compr_cluster unpacks the decompressed payload through the cluster holemap but never verifies that every decoded byte was consumed. A structurally inconsistent cluster can mark too many blocks as holes and silently discard checksum-valid payload bytes, returning zero-filled data instead of reporting corruption. Acceptance: factor/guard holemap expansion so leftover decoded bytes are rejected with FfsError::Corruption; add focused valid and inconsistent-holemap tests; update spec/parity docs if wording mentions e2compr integrity; run targeted e2compr tests plus fmt/diff/UBS/workspace check/clippy.
close_reason: Rejected e2compr clusters whose holemap cannot consume every decoded byte, added focused valid/inconsistent-holemap tests, and updated spec/parity docs.

title: Refine hostile-image safety with resource caps and containment proofs
description:
Supplemental refinement for bd-rchk0.5.11. The adversarial threat model lists malformed images and resource-exhaustion corpus cases, but implementation needs a concrete containment proof so hostile images cannot consume unbounded CPU, memory, disk, file descriptors, mount time, or proof-artifact space while still producing a misleading readiness result.

Scope: define hostile-image resource budgets and containment behavior for ext4/btrfs parsing, mount preflight, repair-ledger ingestion, proof-bundle validation, and operator commands. The expected safe behavior should be deterministic: reject, quarantine, cap, downgrade, or require long-campaign handling with an owning bead.

Required proof before close:
1. Unit/schema tests reject threat cases missing resource class, limit value/unit, enforcement point, expected classification, cleanup policy, remediation id, and release-gate effect.
2. Unit tests cover oversized metadata, cyclic metadata references, deeply nested directories, huge xattr payloads, truncated/corrupt repair ledgers, path traversal artifacts, symlinked proof paths, excessive log output, excessive artifact count, timeout, and file-descriptor exhaustion fixtures.
3. E2E/security smoke runs bounded hostile fixtures and proves every case exits with reject/quarantine/cap/unsupported classification rather than success, hang, host mutation, or unstructured panic.
4. Logs include threat scenario id, input hash, resource limits, observed CPU/time/memory/disk counters where available, enforcement point, classification, cleanup status, artifact paths, remediation id, and reproduction command.
5. Release gates and README safety wording cannot mark hostile-image handling as stronger than experimental unless these containment artifacts are fresh and every critical threat class has an explicit pass, cap, quarantine, unsupported, or follow-up result.
close_reason: Implemented hostile-image containment resource controls, observed counters, bounded fixture matrix, negative validation cases, E2E smoke, docs, and release-gate-safe wording.

title: Refine proof overhead budgets with retention and reproduction-preserving compression
description:
Supplemental refinement for bd-rchk0.5.14. Budgeting proof overhead is not just about runtime and artifact size; the project also needs a retention and compression policy that keeps reproduction-critical evidence while preventing proof runs from filling disks, hiding failures, or leaking unnecessary host details.

Scope: define retention tiers for developer smoke, CI required, nightly/stress, permissioned mount, and release-readiness profiles. Specify which logs/artifacts are mandatory, which may be compressed, which may be sampled, which may be redacted, and which must never be dropped before validator and remediation consumers finish.

Required proof before close:
1. Unit/schema tests reject retention policies missing profile, artifact class, retention duration/count, compression setting, redaction policy, mandatory fields, reproduction command preservation, maximum size, and exception expiry.
2. Unit tests cover pass, warn, fail, over-budget, missing mandatory log, compression success, compression corruption, redaction mismatch, stale exception, cleanup failure, and reproduction-command loss cases.
3. E2E smoke generates fixture proof artifacts, applies retention/compression/redaction policy, validates the resulting bundle offline, and proves mandatory reproduction fields and raw failure diagnostics remain accessible.
4. Logs include budget profile, artifact class, original size, compressed size, retention decision, redaction policy version, dropped/sampled field list, exception id, validator result, cleanup status, and reproduction command.
5. Release gates cannot accept a proof bundle whose retention or compression policy removed fields required by remediation, cross-lane correlation, tamper validation, or reproduction.
close_reason: Implemented proof overhead retention/compression/redaction policy with class-specific gates, reproduction-preserving artifact validation, E2E smoke, and docs.

title: Refine repair corpus with ledger chain-of-custody and wrong-image proofs
description:
Supplemental refinement for bd-rchk7.2. Repair and ledger corruption fixtures must prove not only decoder success/failure but also ledger chain-of-custody: symbols, manifests, image hashes, and verification rows must belong to the image under repair. Otherwise a stale or wrong-image ledger can produce convincing but unsafe repair evidence.

Scope: add corpus requirements for ledger identity, symbol generation, image hash binding, wrong-image rejection, stale-ledger rejection, partial-write handling, tamper evidence, and post-repair symbol refresh. The corpus should include positive recovery cases and negative cases where refusal is the correct result.

Required proof before close:
1. Unit/schema tests reject corpus rows missing original image hash, corrupted image hash or corruption manifest hash, ledger id, symbol generation, repair-symbol budget, expected recoverability, verification expectation, and chain-of-custody fields.
2. Unit tests cover valid ledger, wrong-image ledger, stale ledger, truncated ledger, tampered ledger row, duplicate ledger row, insufficient symbols, excessive symbols from unrelated image, partial ledger write, and post-repair symbol refresh mismatch.
3. Harness/E2E smoke runs a bounded subset and emits per-case artifacts with expected outcome, observed outcome, refusal reason, ledger row ids, image hashes, and reproduction command.
4. Logs include corpus id, seed, original/corrupted/repaired image hashes, ledger id, symbol generation, decoder parameters, expected classification, observed classification, cleanup status, artifact paths, and reproduction command.
5. Automatic repair, remediation, and release-gate beads cannot use repair corpus evidence unless wrong-image and stale-ledger refusal cases are present and passing.

title: Publish criterion benchmark baselines with regression detection
description:
## What
Refresh criterion benchmark baselines that are ~2 months stale (latest: 2026-02-19) and ensure
the existing perf-regression CI workflow runs cleanly against current code.

## Why
Benchmark infrastructure is comprehensive — 10 benchmark files exist across 6 crates, a
scripts/benchmark_record.sh script exists, and .github/workflows/perf-regression.yml provides
CI integration. However, baselines haven't been refreshed since February 2026. Two months of
active development (100+ commits) means the baselines are meaningless for regression detection.

## What Already Exists
- 10 benchmark files: arc_cache, ondisk_parse, metadata_parse, extent_resolve, mount_runtime,
  degraded_pressure, wal_throughput, bitmap_ops, batch_alloc, scrub_codec
- scripts/benchmark_record.sh with hyperfine integration
- baselines/ directory with historical results (latest: 2026-02-19)
- benchmarks/baselines/latest.json
- .github/workflows/perf-regression.yml (manual dispatch)

## Remaining Work
1. Run all 10 existing criterion benchmarks on release-perf profile
2. Run scripts/benchmark_record.sh to capture new hyperfine baselines
3. Commit updated baselines to baselines/ and benchmarks/baselines/
4. Verify perf-regression.yml workflow runs cleanly (manual dispatch)
5. If any benchmarks fail to compile (API drift from 2 months of changes), fix them
6. Document current numbers in baselines/baseline-20260406.md

## Test Plan
- All 10 benchmark binaries compile on release-perf profile
- scripts/benchmark_record.sh completes without errors
- perf-regression.yml passes on manual dispatch
- Variance < 5% across 3 consecutive benchmark runs

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All existing benchmarks compile and run
- [ ] Fresh baselines committed (baselines/baseline-20260406.md)
- [ ] benchmarks/baselines/latest.json updated
- [ ] perf-regression.yml verified working
- [ ] Any API-drift compilation failures in benchmarks fixed
close_reason: Baselines refreshed 2026-04-06: baseline-20260406.md with release-perf profile, latest.json updated, perf-regression.yml has --profile release-perf

title: Docs: Align parser error taxonomy (ParseError vs FfsError)
description:
Problem: COMPREHENSIVE_SPEC describes some parser failures as returning FfsError::Format/Corruption, but the current design uses ffs-types::ParseError for pure parsing (ffs-ondisk) and maps to FfsError at the orchestration boundary (ffs-core / ffs-ext4 / ffs-btrfs).

Goal: document the layering explicitly:
- ffs-ondisk is pure and returns ParseError
- ffs-core converts ParseError to FfsError for user-facing surfaces (CLI/FUSE)
- checksum verification errors choose a stable representation (ParseError::InvalidField vs a dedicated variant)

Acceptance:
- Spec sections that mention parser error returns match the actual crate boundaries.
- No doc requires ffs-ondisk to depend on ffs-error (unless we intentionally change architecture and implement it).
close_reason: done

title: Add performance regression test with baseline tracking
description:
# Add performance regression test with baseline tracking

## GOAL
Implement automated performance regression detection so no commit silently degrades performance.

## BACKGROUND
From AGENTS.md:
- Benchmark loop is mandatory
- Baseline first, then optimize
- Re-measure and record deltas

We need automated CI integration.

## TECHNICAL REQUIREMENTS

### 1. Baseline Storage
```
benchmarks/
├── baselines/
│   ├── latest.json           # Current baseline
│   └── history/
│       ├── 2026-02-12.json
│       └── ...
├── results/
│   └── <commit-sha>.json
└── thresholds.toml           # Regression thresholds
```

### 2. Benchmark Suite
```rust
// In ffs-harness
criterion_group!(
    perf_regression,
    bench_read_throughput,
    bench_write_throughput,
    bench_lookup_latency,
    bench_readdir_latency,
    bench_mvcc_snapshot,
    bench_mvcc_commit,
    bench_arc_hit,
    bench_arc_miss,
);
```

### 3. Regression Detection
```toml
# thresholds.toml
[thresholds]
read_throughput = { min_ratio = 0.9 }  # max 10% regression
write_throughput = { min_ratio = 0.85 }
lookup_latency = { max_ratio = 1.1 }   # max 10% regression
```

### 4. CI Integration
```yaml
# .github/workflows/perf.yml
- name: Run benchmarks
  run: cargo bench -p ffs-harness -- --save-baseline current

- name: Check regression
  run: scripts/check_perf_regression.sh current baselines/latest.json
```

### 5. Reporting
- JSON output with all metrics
- Human-readable summary
- PR comment with delta table

## TESTS
1. Unit: Threshold checking logic
2. Integration: Benchmark suite runs to completion
3. Integration: Regression detected when introduced

## LOGGING
- Benchmark results (info): metric, value, baseline, ratio
- Regression (warn): metric, threshold exceeded
- Pass (info): all within threshold

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. CI fails on performance regression beyond threshold
2. Baselines updated on main branch
3. History preserved for trend analysis
4. Clear reporting of which metric regressed
close_reason: Completed: added TOML-driven perf thresholds, benchmark baseline latest/history exports, CI artifact wiring, and threshold classification unit tests

title: EPIC: EBR for Block Version GC (Graveyard Entry 14.10)
description:
# EPIC: EBR for Block Version GC (Graveyard Entry 14.10)

## PURPOSE
Implement Epoch-Based Reclamation (EBR) for block version chain garbage collection. Without GC, MVCC version chains grow unbounded — every committed write creates a new version that is never freed. EBR provides safe, lock-free reclamation of old versions once no active transaction can observe them.

## BACKGROUND
From the Alien CS Graveyard entry 14.10:
- EBR: threads announce entry into epochs; old block versions freed after all threads advance past the epoch in which versions were retired
- Critical for MVCC scalability — without GC, memory usage is O(total_writes) instead of O(active_versions)
- crossbeam-epoch provides production-quality EBR in Rust

## DESIGN

### Epoch Lifecycle
1. Each MVCC reader pins the current epoch on transaction begin
2. Writers create new versions and retire old versions (mark for deferred free)
3. Background GC thread advances the global epoch periodically
4. Retired versions are freed when all threads have advanced past the retire epoch

### Version Chain Integration
- Version chain: Block -> V3 -> V2 -> V1 (newest first)
- Active snapshot with commit_seq=5 may still need V2
- V1 can be freed only when no active snapshot has commit_seq <= V1.commit_seq
- EBR epoch pins ensure no version is freed while observable

### crossbeam-epoch Integration
- Use crossbeam::epoch::{pin, Guard, Atomic, Owned}
- Version chain nodes wrapped in Atomic<VersionNode>
- On retire: guard.defer_destroy(old_version)
- GC happens automatically when guard is dropped and epoch advances

### Memory Bounds
- Version chain length bound: configurable max versions per block (e.g., 64)
- If chain exceeds bound: force-advance oldest snapshot or abort oldest transaction
- This prevents unbounded memory growth even with long-running snapshots

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] Old block versions are freed after all observing transactions complete
- [ ] No use-after-free (verified with Miri or AddressSanitizer)
- [ ] Memory usage bounded: version chain length enforced
- [ ] crossbeam-epoch correctly integrated
- [ ] Benchmark: memory usage stable under continuous write load
- [ ] Unit tests: version freed after last observer drops
- [ ] Unit tests: version NOT freed while observer holds pin

## Success Criteria
1. All children closed
2. E2E GC test (bd-14jb.4) passes
3. No memory leaks under continuous write workload
4. Miri clean (no undefined behavior)
5. Version chain length bounded
close_reason: All child beads closed with evidence for EBR integration, bounded reclamation, benchmark + E2E + unit/Miri validation, and cross-crate flush/GC coordination.

title: Integrate crossbeam-epoch for block version GC
description:
# Integrate crossbeam-epoch for block version GC

## GOAL
Wire crossbeam-epoch into the MVCC version chain so that old block versions are automatically reclaimed when no active transaction can observe them.

## DESIGN

### crossbeam-epoch API Usage
- On transaction begin: let guard = crossbeam::epoch::pin()
- Version chain nodes: Atomic<VersionNode> for lock-free traversal
- On version retire: guard.defer_destroy(old_node)
- Guard dropped on transaction commit/abort -> epoch advances

### Key Changes
- ffs-mvcc/src/version_chain.rs: replace Box<VersionNode> with crossbeam Atomic
- ffs-mvcc/src/txn.rs: pin epoch on begin, drop guard on commit/abort
- Add crossbeam-epoch dependency to ffs-mvcc/Cargo.toml

### Safety
- All version chain access must go through pinned guard
- No raw pointer dereference without guard
- Miri-compatible (crossbeam-epoch works with Miri)

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] crossbeam-epoch integrated into version chain
- [ ] Old versions freed after epoch advances
- [ ] No memory leaks under continuous write workload (track with allocator stats)
- [ ] Passes Miri (no undefined behavior)
- [ ] Unit test: create versions, advance epoch, verify old versions freed
close_reason: Implemented crossbeam-epoch-backed deferred reclamation for retired logical block versions in ffs-mvcc (EbrVersionReclaimer/EbrVersionStats + ebr_collect/ebr_stats wiring), with retirement integrated into chain-cap and watermark pruning paths plus unit coverage. Package gates passed: cargo fmt/check/clippy/test -p ffs-mvcc. Workspace-wide gates currently blocked by unrelated pre-existing ffs-alloc borrow-check error at crates/ffs-alloc/src/lib.rs:1461. Targeted Miri test executed; plain Miri reports crossbeam-epoch stacked-borrows/provenance incompatibility, while test passes under MIRIFLAGS -Zmiri-disable-stacked-borrows -Zmiri-permissive-provenance -Zmiri-ignore-leaks.

title: Implement version chain length bounds
description:
# Implement version chain length bounds

## GOAL
Enforce a configurable maximum version chain length per block to prevent unbounded memory growth, even when long-running snapshots pin old versions.

## DESIGN
- Max chain length: configurable, default 64 versions per block
- When chain exceeds max: emit warning, force-advance oldest snapshot epoch
- If force-advance insufficient: abort oldest read-only transaction holding the pin
- Last resort: refuse new writes to that block until GC catches up (backpressure)
- Track chain lengths in BlockVersionStats for monitoring

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] Chain length bounded at configured maximum
- [ ] Long-running snapshots warned and force-advanced when blocking GC
- [ ] Backpressure applied when chain length critical
- [ ] Unit test: write 100 versions, verify chain length stays <= max
close_reason: Implemented chain-length bounds and pressure controls in ffs-mvcc: default capped chains (CompressionPolicy), chain-pressure detection at critical length, oldest-snapshot force-advance, and explicit write rejection via CommitError::ChainBackpressure when pressure persists. Added BlockVersionStats monitoring API and GC pressure logging (debug/warn/info/error events). Added tests: chain_length_bounded_after_many_writes and chain_backpressure_triggers_and_force_advances_oldest_snapshot, plus prior chain-cap tests. Gates passed for ffs-mvcc: cargo fmt --check -p ffs-mvcc; cargo check -p ffs-mvcc --all-targets; cargo clippy -p ffs-mvcc --all-targets -- -D warnings; cargo test -p ffs-mvcc (122 tests). Targeted Miri (crossbeam-compatible flags) passed for chain-related tests.

title: Benchmark: memory usage under concurrent writers with EBR GC
description:
# Benchmark: memory usage under concurrent writers with EBR GC

## GOAL
Prove that EBR-based garbage collection keeps memory usage bounded under continuous concurrent write workloads. Without this benchmark, we cannot verify that the GC reclaims versions fast enough to prevent unbounded memory growth.

## BACKGROUND
From Alien CS Graveyard entry 14.10: EBR (Epoch-Based Reclamation) defers freeing old block versions until all observing transactions complete. If GC lags behind writers, memory grows without bound. This benchmark measures the steady-state memory profile under realistic concurrent write loads.

## BENCHMARK SCENARIOS

### Scenario 1: Single Writer Steady State
- 1 writer thread, continuous block writes (100K transactions)
- Measure: peak RSS, version chain lengths, GC reclaim rate
- Expected: RSS stabilizes after initial ramp-up (bounded growth)

### Scenario 2: Multi-Writer Scaling (2, 4, 8, 16 threads)
- Each thread writes to random blocks, 10K transactions each
- Measure: peak RSS per thread count, GC throughput, epoch advance rate
- Expected: RSS scales sub-linearly with thread count (shared versions reclaimed)

### Scenario 3: Long-Running Reader Pinning
- 1 long-running read transaction (holds epoch pin for 30 seconds)
- 8 concurrent writers producing new versions
- Measure: RSS growth during pinned period, RSS drop after reader completes
- Expected: RSS grows during pin (versions retained), drops after unpin (GC catches up)

### Scenario 4: Hot-Key Contention
- 16 threads all writing to same 100 blocks (maximum version chain churn)
- Measure: peak chain length, GC latency, abort rate
- Expected: chain length bounded by configured max (bd-14jb.2), GC keeps up

### Scenario 5: Bursty Write Pattern
- Alternating 5s burst (max writes) and 5s idle, repeated 6 times
- Measure: RSS during burst vs idle, GC catch-up time
- Expected: RSS drops to near-baseline during idle periods

## METRICS
- Peak RSS (resident set size) via /proc/self/statm
- Version chain length histogram (p50/p95/p99/max)
- GC reclaim rate (versions freed per second)
- Epoch advance frequency
- Time to steady-state RSS

## REPORTING
- JSON output with all metrics per scenario
- Comparison table: with-EBR vs without-GC (leak baseline)
- Store results as CI artifacts for regression tracking

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] All 5 scenarios execute and produce metrics
- [ ] Single-writer RSS stabilizes (no unbounded growth)
- [ ] Multi-writer RSS scales sub-linearly with thread count
- [ ] Long-running reader: RSS recovers after reader drops pin
- [ ] Hot-key: chain length stays within configured bounds
- [ ] Bursty: RSS returns to near-baseline during idle periods
- [ ] Results stored as JSON CI artifacts
- [ ] Benchmark harness is deterministic (seeded PRNG for block selection)
- [ ] Logging: tracing spans for each scenario with memory snapshots at 1s intervals
close_reason: Implemented deterministic EBR memory benchmark/report scenarios in crates/ffs-mvcc/benches/wal_throughput.rs and JSON artifact emission to artifacts/benchmarks/ebr_memory_usage.json. Added scenario coverage for: single-writer no-GC baseline, single-writer steady state with GC, multi-writer scaling (2/4/8/16), long-running reader pinning, hot-key contention, and bursty writes. Report includes RSS start/peak/end, commits/failures, chain stats, retired/reclaimed/pending versions, reclaim rate, epoch-advance estimate, and pin/release recovery counters (version_count_during_pin vs version_count_after_release). Verified artifact generation via cargo bench -p ffs-mvcc --bench wal_throughput mvcc_ebr_report_cached. ffs-mvcc gates pass: cargo fmt --check -p ffs-mvcc; cargo check -p ffs-mvcc --all-targets; cargo clippy -p ffs-mvcc --all-targets -- -D warnings; cargo test -p ffs-mvcc (127 tests).

title: E2E test: EBR GC correctness under concurrent MVCC transactions
description:
# E2E test: EBR GC correctness under concurrent MVCC transactions

## GOAL
Prove that EBR-based garbage collection correctly reclaims old block versions without ever freeing a version that an active transaction still observes. This is the safety test for the entire EBR epic.

## TEST SCENARIOS

### Scenario 1: Basic Reclamation Correctness
1. Start transaction T1 (snapshot at commit_seq=5)
2. Writer W1 creates versions V6, V7, V8 for the same block
3. V5 (observed by T1) must NOT be freed
4. T1 commits -> V5 becomes eligible for GC
5. Advance epoch -> V5 freed
6. Verify: T1 always saw V5 during its lifetime, V5 freed after T1 ended

### Scenario 2: Multi-Reader Multi-Writer
1. 4 reader transactions at different snapshots (commit_seq 10, 20, 30, 40)
2. 4 writer threads producing new versions continuously
3. Each reader reads its snapshot blocks, verifies content matches expected
4. Readers commit in reverse order (40 first, 10 last)
5. After each reader commit: verify newly eligible versions freed
6. After all readers commit: verify all obsolete versions freed

### Scenario 3: No Use-After-Free
1. Spawn reader that pins epoch, reads version V_old, sleeps 500ms, reads again
2. Writer creates new version V_new for same block
3. GC thread attempts to free V_old
4. Verify: V_old NOT freed while reader holds pin (epoch prevents reclamation)
5. Reader drops pin -> V_old freed on next epoch advance
6. Run under AddressSanitizer / Miri for UAF detection

### Scenario 4: Chain Length Enforcement
1. Create block with max_chain_length=16
2. Write 32 versions without any reader advancing
3. Verify: chain length stays at 16 (oldest versions force-freed or writer blocked)
4. Verify: no data corruption from forced chain truncation

### Scenario 5: Crash Recovery of GC State
1. Build up version chains, partially GC
2. Simulate crash (kill process)
3. Remount: version chains consistent, no dangling pointers
4. GC resumes correctly from recovered state

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] No use-after-free detected (AddressSanitizer or Miri clean)
- [ ] Readers always see consistent snapshots (no torn reads)
- [ ] Versions freed promptly after last observer completes
- [ ] Chain length bounds enforced under all scenarios
- [ ] Crash recovery preserves GC invariants
- [ ] All scenarios deterministic with seeded PRNG
- [ ] Logging: tracing spans for epoch advances, version frees, pin/unpin events
close_reason: Implemented EBR E2E correctness scenarios in crates/ffs-mvcc/src/lib.rs: e2e_ebr_basic_reclamation_correctness, e2e_ebr_multi_reader_multi_writer_release_order, e2e_ebr_reader_pin_blocks_reclamation_until_release, e2e_ebr_chain_length_enforced_under_concurrent_writers, and e2e_ebr_restart_preserves_latest_visible_value. These cover snapshot-stable visibility under concurrent writes, release-ordered reclamation progress, pin/unpin safety, chain bound enforcement under contention, and restart visibility consistency via PersistentMvccStore WAL replay. ffs-mvcc gates pass: cargo fmt/check/clippy/test -p ffs-mvcc (138 tests). Miri coverage executed with crossbeam-compatible flags: e2e_ebr_basic_reclamation_correctness PASS, e2e_ebr_reader_pin_blocks_reclamation_until_release PASS, e2e_ebr_restart_preserves_latest_visible_value PASS (requires -Zmiri-disable-isolation for tempfile-backed file I/O).

title: Cross-crate integration: EBR GC coordinates with write-back cache eviction
description:
# Cross-crate integration: EBR GC coordinates with write-back cache eviction

## GOAL
Wire EBR-based version garbage collection to coordinate with the write-back cache eviction policy. When EBR GC reclaims old block versions, it must ensure no dirty cache entries reference those versions and that the cache eviction path respects epoch guards.

## BACKGROUND
EBR (bd-14jb) reclaims old MVCC versions. Write-back cache (bd-5s0r) holds dirty blocks. If GC reclaims a version that has dirty cache entries pending flush, data loss occurs. The cache eviction path must pin epochs during flush.

## DELIVERABLES
1. Cache eviction acquires epoch guard before flushing dirty blocks
2. GC skips versions with outstanding dirty cache references
3. Dirty flush completes before epoch can advance past that version
4. Integration test proving no use-after-free under concurrent GC + flush

## TESTS (REQUIRED)
1. Unit: epoch guard held during flush prevents GC of referenced version
2. Unit: GC correctly skips pinned versions
3. Stress: concurrent writers + GC + flush -- no data corruption (Miri-safe)
4. Integration: version reclaimed only after all dirty blocks flushed

## LOGGING REQUIREMENTS
- GC skips pinned version (debug): version_id, pinned_by, epoch
- Flush acquires epoch guard (trace): flush_batch_id, epoch_id
- Flush releases epoch guard (trace): flush_batch_id, blocks_flushed
- GC reclaims version (info): version_id, freed_blocks, epoch_advanced_to

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. No use-after-free under concurrent GC + cache flush (Miri clean)
2. GC does not stall indefinitely (bounded epoch lag)
3. Dirty blocks always flushed before version reclaimed

title: Add unit tests for EBR epoch advancement and version reclamation
description:
# Add unit tests for EBR epoch advancement and version reclamation

## GOAL
Provide unit test coverage for the EBR for Block Version GC epic (bd-14jb). Currently has E2E test (bd-14jb.4) and benchmark (bd-14jb.3) but NO unit tests for epoch advancement, guard pinning, or version reclamation correctness.

## TEST PLAN

### Epoch Tests
1. Epoch advances when all guards dropped
2. Epoch does NOT advance while guard is held
3. Multiple concurrent guards: epoch waits for all
4. Guard drop order does not matter

### Reclamation Tests
5. Old version reclaimed after epoch advances past it
6. Version NOT reclaimed while any guard pins it
7. Reclamation batch processes multiple versions
8. Empty reclamation queue is no-op

### Version Chain Tests
9. Version chain length decreases after GC
10. Bounded chain length enforced (bd-14jb.2 integration)
11. Latest committed version never reclaimed
12. Aborted version reclaimed immediately

### Safety Tests
13. No use-after-free (Miri clean)
14. No memory leak under continuous write/GC cycle
15. Concurrent pin/unpin/reclaim does not corrupt state

## TESTS (REQUIRED)
All 15 tests above. Tests 13-14 require Miri.

## LOGGING REQUIREMENTS
- Test failures log epoch state, guard count, reclamation queue

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. All 15 tests pass (including Miri)
2. No memory leaks verified via allocator stats
3. Deterministic

Combined with existing EBR tests, this validates no-op collect behavior, snapshot pin gating, multi-block retirement accounting, chain trimming correctness, and post-collect pending=0 invariants. Gates passed: cargo fmt/check/clippy/test -p ffs-mvcc (118 tests). Miri executed for all EBR tests with crossbeam-compatible flags (MIRIFLAGS=-Zmiri-disable-stacked-borrows -Zmiri-permissive-provenance -Zmiri-ignore-leaks): 6/6 EBR tests pass.

title: ffs-types: Add missing canonical newtypes (BlockSize, ByteOffset, GroupNumber, DeviceId, Generation, ...)
description:
Goal: make ffs-types the single canonical home for cross-crate identifiers and basic arithmetic invariants.

Why: without strong newtypes we will silently mix units (bytes vs blocks, logical vs physical, ext4 group vs btrfs objectid) and ship corruption bugs.

Deliverables (minimum set):
- BlockSize(u32): must be power-of-two; ext4 v1 supports 1024/2048/4096 only.
- ByteOffset(u64): byte offsets on ByteDevice; checked add/sub/mul helpers.
- GroupNumber(u32): ext4 block group index.
- DeviceId(u64) or u128: stable id for multi-device future; for now single-device only.
- Generation(u64): btrfs generation / ext4 generation boundary type.
- LogicalBlock(BlockNumber) vs PhysicalBlock(BlockNumber) wrappers if we decide they are distinct.

Acceptance:
- All added newtypes have documented invariants + constructors that enforce them.
- Existing code in ffs-ondisk/ffs-block/ffs-core is updated to use these types where it improves correctness.
close_reason: done

title: EPIC: Self-Healing Durability (RaptorQ)
description:
# EPIC: Self-Healing Durability (RaptorQ)

## PURPOSE
Implement FrankenFS’s second key innovation: automatic corruption recovery via fountain-coded repair symbols.

## CURRENT REALITY (CODE)
A meaningful portion of the foundation already exists:
- Codec core:
  - `crates/ffs-repair/src/codec.rs` implements deterministic group encode/decode via asupersync RaptorQ (SystematicEncoder + InactivationDecoder) with unit tests.
- Symbol layout:
  - `crates/ffs-repair/src/symbol.rs` defines a checksummed on-disk symbol header and group descriptor extension types.
- Scrub engine:
  - `crates/ffs-repair/src/scrub.rs` provides a generic block scrub pipeline that can surface corruption findings.

## WHAT’S STILL MISSING (THE REAL WORK)
1. Durable symbol storage I/O + reserved-space rules (so symbols exist after restart and are not allocated as user data).
2. Recovery orchestration (detect -> load symbols -> decode -> writeback -> verify) + evidence ledger.
3. End-to-end, reproducible corruption injection and recovery proof scripts.

## DESIGN REQUIREMENTS (NON-NEGOTIABLE)
Per AGENTS.md “alien artifact” bar for risky logic:
- Explicit invariants for recovery decisions.
- Evidence ledger (structured records) for every recovery attempt.
- No silent partial repair; fail loudly if redundancy insufficient.

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA (EPIC)
1. Repair symbols are generated and durably stored per block group (configurable overhead).
2. Controlled corruption triggers recovery attempts.
3. Successful recovery restores exact bytes and is verified.
4. Failures are graceful and explainable (clear error + evidence).
5. E2E script demonstrates bounded corruption + recovery deterministically with logs/artifacts.

## RELATED SPEC SECTIONS
- COMPREHENSIVE_SPEC_FOR_FRANKENFS_V1.md §4 (Self-Healing)
- `crates/ffs-repair/src/symbol.rs` (current storage model)

## Success Criteria
`scripts/e2e/ffs_repair_recovery_smoke.sh` passes and produces:
- corruption plan
- before/after checksums
- recovery evidence ledger
- clear PASS/FAIL summary
close_reason: All child tasks complete; self-healing durability epic criteria met.

description:
# Integrate asupersync RaptorQ codec into ffs-repair

## GOAL
Provide a deterministic, well-tested RaptorQ encode/decode core that FrankenFS can build storage + recovery workflows on top of.

## CURRENT REALITY (CODE)
This bead is **already implemented** in:
- `crates/ffs-repair/src/codec.rs`
  - `encode_group()` (SystematicEncoder)
  - `decode_group()` (InactivationDecoder)
  - `EncodedGroup` / `DecodeOutcome` types
- `crates/ffs-repair/src/symbol.rs`
  - deterministic seed derivation (`repair_seed`)

The unit test suite in `crates/ffs-repair/src/codec.rs` covers:
- determinism (same inputs/seed -> same symbols)
- successful recovery of 1+ corrupt blocks with sufficient repair symbols
- failure modes with insufficient symbols
- non-zero `first_block` offsets
- progressive fault injection

## OUT OF SCOPE (HANDLED BY OTHER BEADS)
- Persistent storage of repair symbols on disk (`bd-15c.2`).
- Recovery orchestration + evidence ledger (`bd-15c.3`).
- End-to-end corruption injection scripts (`bd-15c.4`).

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. `cargo test -p ffs-repair` passes.
2. Codec unit tests provide actionable failures (seed/K/block_size printed or derivable).
3. Public APIs are stable enough for storage/recovery layers to build on.

title: Implement eager vs lazy refresh protocol for RaptorQ symbols
description:
# Implement eager vs lazy refresh protocol for RaptorQ symbols

## GOAL
Define and implement the protocol for when RaptorQ repair symbols are regenerated after data blocks change. Two strategies: eager (regenerate on every write) and lazy (regenerate on next scrub or background pass). The system must choose correctly based on workload and corruption risk.

## BACKGROUND
When a data block in a block group is modified, the existing repair symbols become stale — they encode the old data. If corruption occurs before symbols are refreshed, repair will restore the OLD data, not the new data. This is a correctness hazard.

## DESIGN

### Eager Refresh
- Regenerate all repair symbols for a block group whenever any block in the group is modified
- PRO: symbols always current, repair always correct
- CON: write amplification — every data write triggers O(overhead_ratio * group_size) symbol writes
- USE WHEN: low write rate, high corruption risk, metadata groups

### Lazy Refresh
- Mark block group as dirty when any block is modified
- Regenerate symbols during next background scrub pass or explicit refresh
- PRO: amortizes symbol regeneration cost across multiple writes
- CON: window of vulnerability — stale symbols during dirty period
- USE WHEN: high write rate, low corruption risk, data groups

### Hybrid Policy
- Default: lazy for data groups, eager for metadata groups (superblock, GDT, inode tables)
- Configurable: per-group override based on DurabilityAutopilot risk assessment
- If corruption posterior exceeds threshold (e.g., P > 0.01), switch group to eager
- Track dirty duration — if group stays dirty > timeout (e.g., 30s), force refresh

### Dirty Tracking
- Per-block-group dirty bitmap in memory
- Dirty timestamp for staleness tracking
- Integration with write path: set dirty bit on block write
- Integration with scrub daemon: clear dirty bit after refresh

## IMPLEMENTATION

### Data Structures
```rust
enum RefreshPolicy {
    Eager,
    Lazy { max_staleness: Duration },
    Adaptive { risk_threshold: f64 },
}

struct GroupRefreshState {
    dirty: bool,
    dirty_since: Option<Instant>,
    policy: RefreshPolicy,
    last_refresh: Instant,
}
```

### Integration Points
- Write path (ffs-block or ffs-ext4/ffs-btrfs): set dirty bit after block write
- Scrub daemon: refresh stale groups during background pass
- DurabilityAutopilot: adjust per-group policy based on risk
- bd-820 (RaptorQ symbol refresh on dirty group flush): this bead generalizes that concept

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] Eager refresh correctly regenerates symbols on every write to affected group
- [ ] Lazy refresh marks groups dirty and refreshes during scrub
- [ ] Hybrid policy applies eager to metadata, lazy to data by default
- [ ] Adaptive policy switches eager/lazy based on corruption posterior
- [ ] Dirty duration timeout forces refresh after configurable period
- [ ] Evidence records logged for every refresh (eager or lazy)
- [ ] Unit tests: eager refresh correctness after single write
- [ ] Unit tests: lazy refresh correctness after multiple writes then scrub
- [ ] Integration test: verify repair works correctly with both policies
notes:
## LOGGING REQUIREMENTS
- TRACE target=ffs::repair::refresh: fields group, policy, dirty, dirty_since_ms, last_refresh_ms on policy evaluation.
- DEBUG target=ffs::repair::refresh: fields group, policy, max_staleness_ms, risk_threshold, posterior_mean when adaptive/eager/lazy decisions are computed.
- INFO target=ffs::repair::refresh: fields group, refresh_mode, symbols_generated, generation_before, generation_after when a refresh is executed.
- WARN target=ffs::repair::refresh: fields group, dirty_age_ms, max_staleness_ms when stale lazy groups are force-refreshed.
- ERROR target=ffs::repair::refresh: fields group, refresh_mode, error on refresh failure or evidence-write failure.
close_reason: Completed daemon queued refresh integration and regression coverage.

title: E2E test: survive 5% random block corruption with automatic repair
description:
# E2E test: survive 5% random block corruption with automatic repair

## GOAL
Prove that FrankenFS can survive 5% random block corruption with fully automatic repair, zero data loss, and a complete evidence ledger. This is THE acceptance test for the self-healing durability story.

## TEST SCENARIO

### Setup
1. Create a fresh FrankenFS image (e.g., 256MB ext4 with RaptorQ enabled)
2. Mount via FUSE, write a known dataset (1000 files with deterministic content)
3. Compute BLAKE3 checksums for all files
4. Unmount cleanly (symbols fully refreshed)

### Corruption Injection
5. Randomly corrupt 5% of all data blocks (flip random bits)
   - Use deterministic seed for reproducibility
   - Corruption must span multiple block groups
   - Must corrupt both data and some non-critical metadata blocks

### Repair
6. Remount the filesystem
7. Scrub daemon detects corruption and triggers repair
8. Wait for repair to complete (bounded timeout: 60s for 256MB)

### Verification
9. Read all 1000 files, verify BLAKE3 checksums match original
10. Zero files should be corrupted after repair
11. Evidence ledger must contain:
    - CorruptionDetected records for all corrupted blocks
    - RepairAttempted records for all repair operations
    - RepairSucceeded records for all successful repairs
    - ScrubCycleComplete summary

### Edge Cases to Test
- Corruption concentrated in one block group (stress-tests single group recovery)
- Corruption spread evenly across all groups
- Corruption of repair symbols themselves (secondary corruption)
- Corruption exceeding single-group repair capacity (graceful failure)

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] 5% random block corruption: all files recovered, zero data loss
- [ ] Evidence ledger is complete and machine-parseable
- [ ] Test completes in < 120s for 256MB image
- [ ] Test is deterministic (same seed = same result)
- [ ] Test passes in CI (no hardware dependencies)
- [ ] Graceful failure when corruption exceeds repair capacity (> symbols available)
close_reason: Validated by QuietKnoll: 5% corruption e2e + graceful-failure test pass; artifacts/ledger verified

title: E2E test: repair evidence ledger completeness and correctness
description:
# E2E test: repair evidence ledger completeness and correctness

## GOAL
Verify that the evidence ledger is complete, correct, and auditable after any repair operation. Every corruption event must have a corresponding detection record, every repair attempt must be logged, and the ledger must be self-consistent.

## TEST SCENARIOS

### Scenario 1: Single Block Corruption
1. Write known data, corrupt exactly 1 block, trigger scrub
2. Verify ledger contains exactly: 1 CorruptionDetected, 1 RepairAttempted, 1 RepairSucceeded
3. Verify all fields are populated (block_group, block_range, checksums, timing)

### Scenario 2: Multi-Group Corruption
1. Corrupt blocks across 5 different block groups
2. Verify ledger contains records for each group independently
3. Verify ScrubCycleComplete summary matches individual records

### Scenario 3: Repair Failure
1. Corrupt more blocks than repair symbols can handle in one group
2. Verify ledger contains RepairFailed with InsufficientRedundancy outcome
3. Verify no false RepairSucceeded records

### Scenario 4: Policy Decision Logging
1. Run multiple scrub cycles with varying corruption rates
2. Verify PolicyDecision records are emitted when autopilot adjusts overhead
3. Verify posterior parameters in records match expected Bayesian updates

### Ledger Self-Consistency Checks
- All timestamps are monotonically increasing
- Every RepairAttempted has a corresponding RepairSucceeded or RepairFailed
- Every CorruptionDetected has a corresponding RepairAttempted (unless deferred)
- ScrubCycleComplete.blocks_checked >= sum of individual corruption records
- No duplicate records (same block_group + block_range + timestamp)

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] All 4 scenarios pass
- [ ] Ledger self-consistency checks pass for every scenario
- [ ] Records are valid JSONL (one JSON object per line)
- [ ] Records can be parsed by standard JSON tools (jq)
- [ ] Test is deterministic and CI-executable
close_reason: Implemented ledger completeness/self-consistency assertions across success/failure/policy scenarios; ffs-repair gates/tests green

title: Implement on-image repair symbol storage (per block group)
description:
# Implement on-image repair symbol storage (per block group)

## GOAL
Persist RaptorQ repair symbols (and any required validation metadata) in a **durable, checksummed on-image format** so recovery can run after restart.

## CURRENT REALITY (CODE)
- `crates/ffs-repair/src/symbol.rs` already defines:
  - `RepairBlockHeader` (per repair block, CRC32C checked)
  - `RepairGroupDescExt` (OTI + repair region metadata, CRC32C checked)
  - constants and deterministic seed helpers
- There is **no** implemented I/O layer that:
  - reserves blocks for repair data
  - writes/reads symbol blocks
  - enforces atomic generation updates

## STORAGE STRATEGY (V1)
Use an **in-image reserved range at the end of each block group**, consistent with the layout described in `crates/ffs-repair/src/symbol.rs`.

Key requirement for ext4 images:
- Reserved repair blocks must be marked **allocated** in the ext4 block bitmap (and reflected in free-block counters), so the kernel allocator will never hand them out.

## SCOPE
1. Finalize the exact per-group layout:
   - how many blocks are reserved for:
     - validation digests (optional, but strongly recommended)
     - repair symbols
     - group metadata (`RepairGroupDescExt`) if stored in-band
   - which exact block(s) store `RepairGroupDescExt` and how it is located deterministically.
2. Implement the storage I/O layer (likely new module under `crates/ffs-repair/src/`):
   - `read_group_desc_ext(group) -> RepairGroupDescExt`
   - `write_group_desc_ext(group, new_ext) -> ()` (atomic update rules)
   - `read_repair_symbols(group) -> Vec<(esi, bytes)>`
   - `write_repair_symbols(group, symbols, generation) -> ()`
3. Implement crash-safety for updates:
   - write all new symbol blocks first
   - only then advance the durable generation counter (so torn writes are detectable)
   - on open: choose the latest fully-valid generation
4. Integrate with ext4 metadata accounting (phase-gated but must be planned here):
   - ensure reserved blocks are excluded from allocation (`bd-huh.2`)
   - ensure group/superblock counters remain consistent

## TESTS (REQUIRED)
1. Unit tests for serialization/parsing (already partially present):
   - roundtrip `RepairBlockHeader` and `RepairGroupDescExt`
   - checksum mismatch detection
2. Integration tests using an in-memory `BlockDevice`:
   - write a full group’s symbol set
   - read it back and verify byte-equality
   - simulate torn write (only half the blocks written) and assert the reader rejects the incomplete generation
3. Logging requirements:
   - tests must log group number, generation, and which block offsets were written/read on failure

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. Repair symbol blocks can be written and read back deterministically.
2. Torn/partial updates are detected and do not silently “half-apply”.
3. The storage layer is usable by the recovery pipeline (`bd-15c.3`).

title: Implement corruption recovery pipeline
description:
# Implement corruption recovery pipeline

## GOAL
Wire together:
- corruption detection signals
- symbol retrieval (bd-15c.2)
- RaptorQ decode (`ffs-repair` codec)
- block repair writeback
- post-repair verification

…into an end-to-end, auditable recovery flow.

## CURRENT REALITY (CODE)
- `crates/ffs-repair/src/codec.rs` provides deterministic `encode_group` / `decode_group`.
- `crates/ffs-repair/src/symbol.rs` defines a checksummed symbol format.
- `crates/ffs-repair/src/scrub.rs` provides a generic scrub engine that can surface corruption findings.

What’s missing is the actual **orchestration** and the integration point where reads/scrubs trigger recovery.

## SCOPE
1. Implement a recovery orchestrator (likely new module under `crates/ffs-repair/src/`):
   - determine block group boundaries
   - identify corrupt block indices within the group
   - load repair symbols for the group
   - run `decode_group()` and obtain recovered blocks
   - write recovered bytes back to the device
   - re-validate repaired blocks
2. Define a minimal corruption signal model for V1:
   - Phase A (required for tests): explicit corrupt index list (test-driven)
   - Phase B (recommended): integrate with validation metadata (if bd-15c.2 stores digests) or format-specific validators
3. Evidence ledger (non-negotiable per AGENTS.md):
   - every recovery attempt must emit a structured record with:
     - group, generation
     - corrupt_count
     - symbols_available / symbols_used
     - decoder stats (`DecodeStats`)
     - outcome (recovered / partial / failed + reason)

## INTEGRATION POINTS (CHOOSE ONE; PREFER MINIMAL)
- Option A (preferred): a `RecoveringBlockDevice` wrapper in `ffs-block` that:
  - delegates to an inner `BlockDevice`
  - on read, runs a validator and triggers recovery on mismatch
- Option B: a CLI-driven repair path (`ffs scrub --repair` or similar) that:
  - scrubs blocks
  - repairs findings
  - re-scrubs to prove clean

The bead must pick one and make it testable.

## TESTS (REQUIRED)
1. End-to-end in-memory test:
   - create deterministic group blocks
   - encode + store repair symbols
   - corrupt N blocks (overwrite bytes)
   - run recovery
   - assert the original bytes are restored for all corrupted blocks
2. Failure-mode test:
   - corrupt more blocks than redundancy supports
   - assert recovery fails gracefully (no partial silent writes)
3. Evidence ledger test:
   - assert the ledger record contains all required fields and is parseable JSON

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. Recovery succeeds when redundancy is sufficient and restores exact bytes.
2. Recovery fails loudly and safely when redundancy is insufficient.
3. Evidence ledger is produced for every attempt and is actionable.
4. No panics on malformed symbol data; errors are explicit.

title: Add E2E corruption-injection + recovery verification scripts
description:
# Add E2E corruption-injection + recovery verification scripts

## GOAL
Add an end-to-end script that:
1. Produces (or copies) a working ext4 fixture image
2. Generates/stores repair symbols
3. Injects controlled corruption into a bounded set of blocks
4. Runs a read/scrub path that triggers recovery
5. Verifies the recovered bytes match the pre-corruption baseline
6. Captures an evidence ledger + detailed logs

## CONTEXT
Unit tests can validate encode/decode and parsing, but recovery is an end-to-end behavior across:
- block I/O
- checksum validation
- symbol store
- RaptorQ decode
- repair writeback

This bead makes recovery auditable and reproducible.

## DELIVERABLES
1. `scripts/e2e/ffs_repair_recovery_smoke.sh`
2. Uses `scripts/e2e/lib.sh` logging conventions.
3. Produces artifacts under `artifacts/e2e/<timestamp>/repair/`:
   - `before_checksums.txt`
   - `after_checksums.txt`
   - `corruption_plan.json`
   - `recovery_evidence.jsonl`

## CORRUPTION INJECTION RULES
- Inject corruption deterministically using a fixed seed.
- Corrupt a small percentage (for example 1-3%) of blocks in a single group.
- Record exactly which blocks were modified and how.
- Corruption method must be reversible/controlled (bit flips, zeroing, truncation is not acceptable unless explicitly tested).

## VERIFICATION
- Before corruption, compute `sha256sum` of a bounded set of files read through FUSE (or read via CLI read-path if available).
- After recovery, recompute and verify identical.
- Additionally verify block-level checksums where available.

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. Script is deterministic and idempotent.
2. Logs include enough detail to reproduce a failure.
3. Evidence ledger exists and is easy to parse (JSONL).
4. Script skips gracefully (exit 0) if repair pipeline is not available yet, but prints a clear reason.

## NOTES
- This bead does not decide the recovery policy; it validates the implementation of bd-15c.3.

title: Implement background scrub daemon with Region-based scheduling
description:
# Implement background scrub daemon with Region-based scheduling

## GOAL
Launch an asupersync Region that performs continuous background scrub of all block groups, detecting corruption and triggering automatic RaptorQ recovery.

## BACKGROUND
From README: "Corruption detected by checksums triggers automatic recovery. No separate fsck pass. No downtime."

The existing pieces:
- `ffs-repair::scrub::scrub_group()` - scans one group ✅
- `ffs-repair::recovery::GroupRecoveryOrchestrator` - recovers one group ✅
- `ffs-repair::storage::RepairGroupStorage` - reads/writes symbols ✅

What's missing: the daemon loop that:
1. Iterates groups in round-robin
2. Calls scrub_group() on each
3. On corruption: triggers GroupRecoveryOrchestrator
4. Respects backpressure (yield under I/O pressure)
5. Produces structured evidence log

## DESIGN
```rust
pub struct ScrubDaemon {
    region: asupersync::Region,
    interval: Duration,
    state: Arc<ScrubState>, // tracks last-scanned group, stats
    metrics: ScrubMetrics,
}

impl ScrubDaemon {
    pub fn spawn(cx: &Cx, engine: Arc<FrankenFsEngine>) -> Self {
        // Launch Region that:
        // 1. Iterates groups 0..N
        // 2. scrub_group() each
        // 3. On corruption: attempt_recovery()
        // 4. Sleep(interval) between rounds
        // 5. Yield if cx.is_cancelled() or backpressure
    }
}
```

## DELIVERABLES
1. `ScrubDaemon` struct in ffs-repair or ffs-core
2. Region-based async scheduling via asupersync
3. Automatic recovery trigger on corruption detection
4. Structured JSON evidence log for all scrub/recovery events
5. Configurable scan interval and backpressure sensitivity

## TESTS (REQUIRED)
1. Unit: Daemon starts and scans at least one group
2. Unit: Daemon detects injected corruption and triggers recovery
3. Unit: Daemon respects cancellation (stops promptly)
4. Unit: Daemon yields under backpressure (doesn't block reads)
5. Integration: Evidence log captures all events
6. Integration: Full filesystem scrub completes without error
7. Stress: Scrub under concurrent I/O load

## LOGGING REQUIREMENTS
- Daemon start (info): total_groups, interval_secs, config
- Round start (debug): round_number, starting_group
- Group scan start (trace): group_id
- Group scan complete (trace): group_id, duration_ms, blocks_scanned, errors_found
- Corruption detected (warn): group_id, block_numbers, checksum_type, expected_vs_actual
- Recovery started (info): group_id, corrupt_block_count, available_symbols
- Recovery success (info): group_id, blocks_recovered, duration_ms
- Recovery failure (error): group_id, reason, remaining_corrupt
- Backpressure yield (debug): current_group, pressure_source, yield_duration_ms
- Round complete (info): round_number, duration_secs, groups_scanned, total_corrupt, total_recovered
- Daemon stop (info): reason, final_stats
- Evidence entry (JSON): structured event for each scrub/recovery action

## METRICS (for TUI dashboard)
- blocks_scanned_total: Counter
- blocks_corrupt_found: Counter
- blocks_recovered: Counter
- blocks_unrecoverable: Counter
- scrub_rounds_completed: Counter
- current_group: Gauge
- scrub_rate_blocks_per_sec: Gauge

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. Background scrub runs continuously during mount
2. Corruption is detected and recovered automatically
3. No noticeable performance impact on foreground I/O (<5% throughput reduction)
4. Clean shutdown on unmount (in-flight group completes)
5. All events logged in structured format

Design this with Bayesian evidence accumulation: track per-group corruption rates over time, adjust scan frequency based on observed failure rates (groups with more corruption get scanned more often). This is the principled alternative to fixed-interval scanning.

title: Wire automatic corruption recovery into scrub detection pipeline
description:
# Wire automatic corruption recovery into scrub detection pipeline

## GOAL
When scrub_group() detects a checksum mismatch, automatically trigger GroupRecoveryOrchestrator to recover the corrupted block using RaptorQ repair symbols.

## BACKGROUND
Currently scrub detects corruption (returns mismatch list) but the caller has to manually invoke recovery. This bead connects the two so corruption → recovery is automatic.

## DELIVERABLES
1. `ScrubWithRecovery` struct that wraps scrub + recovery
2. On mismatch: load repair symbols, attempt decode, write back corrected block
3. On successful recovery: refresh repair symbols (re-encode group)
4. On failed recovery: log evidence, mark block as unrecoverable
5. Return RecoveryReport with per-block outcomes

## DESIGN
```rust
pub struct ScrubWithRecovery {
    scrubber: GroupScrubber,
    recovery: GroupRecoveryOrchestrator,
    evidence_log: EvidenceLog,
}

impl ScrubWithRecovery {
    pub async fn scrub_and_recover(
        &mut self, 
        group: GroupNumber, 
        cx: &Cx
    ) -> Result<RecoveryReport> {
        // 1. Scrub group
        let findings = self.scrubber.scrub_group(group, cx).await?;
        
        // 2. If corruption found, attempt recovery
        if !findings.corrupt_blocks.is_empty() {
            let recovery_result = self.recovery
                .recover_group(group, &findings.corrupt_blocks, cx)
                .await;
            
            // 3. Log evidence regardless of outcome
            self.evidence_log.record(RecoveryEvidence {
                timestamp: Utc::now(),
                group,
                corrupt_blocks: findings.corrupt_blocks.clone(),
                recovery_result: recovery_result.clone(),
            });
            
            // 4. If recovery succeeded, refresh symbols
            if recovery_result.is_ok() {
                self.refresh_repair_symbols(group, cx).await?;
            }
        }
        
        Ok(RecoveryReport { ... })
    }
}
```

## TESTS (REQUIRED)
1. Unit: Single corrupted block → automatic recovery → verified correct
2. Unit: Multiple corrupted blocks in same group → all recovered
3. Unit: Too many corrupted blocks (beyond RaptorQ capacity) → graceful failure report
4. Unit: Recovery of stale block (written after symbol gen) → expected failure with clear error
5. Integration: Full group corruption and recovery cycle
6. Integration: Evidence log contains all required fields
7. Stress: 100 groups with random corruption patterns

## LOGGING REQUIREMENTS
### Scrub phase
- scrub_start (debug): group
- mismatch_found (warn): group, block, expected_csum, actual_csum, csum_type

### Recovery phase
- recovery_start (info): group, corrupt_count, available_symbols
- symbol_load (trace): group, symbol_count, load_time_ms
- decode_attempt (debug): group, input_symbols, corrupt_positions
- decode_success (info): group, blocks_recovered, decode_time_ms
- decode_failure (error): group, reason, missing_symbols_needed
- block_writeback (trace): block, csum_after
- post_verify (debug): group, verify_result

### Symbol refresh
- refresh_start (debug): group, trigger (recovery/write)
- refresh_complete (info): group, symbols_generated, duration_ms

### Evidence logging (JSON structured)
```json
{
  "event": "recovery_attempt",
  "timestamp": "2026-02-13T04:00:00Z",
  "group": 42,
  "corrupt_blocks": [1024, 1025],
  "repair_symbols_available": 164,
  "decode_success": true,
  "blocks_recovered": 2,
  "blocks_unrecoverable": 0,
  "post_verify_ok": true,
  "total_duration_ms": 250
}
```

### Error logging
- io_error (error): operation, block, errno
- symbol_storage_error (error): group, slot, error
- checksum_mismatch_after_recovery (error): block, expected, actual (critical bug indicator)

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. Zero manual intervention for recoverable corruption
2. Evidence ledger captures every decision
3. Unrecoverable corruption clearly reported (not silent)
4. Post-recovery verification catches any recovery bugs
5. Symbol refresh prevents cascade failures

This enables audit trails and Bayesian parameter updates for the durability policy model.

title: Implement evidence persistence for repair actions (JSONL)
description:
# Implement evidence persistence for repair actions (JSONL)

## GOAL
Every repair action, corruption detection, policy decision, and scrub cycle must produce a durable, auditable evidence record persisted as append-only JSONL (JSON Lines).

## BACKGROUND
The alien artifact methodology requires an auditable evidence ledger for every decision the system makes. This is non-negotiable for FrankenFS's self-healing story. Without durable evidence, repairs are invisible black boxes — unacceptable for a filesystem.

## DESIGN

### Record Schema
Each JSONL line is a self-contained JSON object with:
- timestamp_ns: u64 — monotonic nanosecond timestamp
- event_type: enum { CorruptionDetected, RepairAttempted, RepairSucceeded, RepairFailed, ScrubCycleComplete, PolicyDecision, SymbolRefresh }
- block_group: u32 — affected block group number
- block_range: (u64, u64) — start/end block numbers
- corruption_details: { blocks_affected: u32, checksum_expected: [u8;32], checksum_found: [u8;32] }
- repair_details: { symbols_used: u32, symbols_available: u32, decode_time_us: u64, verify_pass: bool }
- policy_context: { corruption_posterior: f64, overhead_ratio: f64, risk_bound: f64 }
- outcome: enum { Success, InsufficientRedundancy, IoError, Timeout }

### Persistence Strategy
- Append-only JSONL file per filesystem instance
- File location: reserved metadata area or configurable path
- fsync after each critical record (repair success/failure)
- Batch fsync for scrub cycle records (performance)
- Rotation: new file per mount session, old files archived

### Integration Points
- ffs-repair/src/scrub.rs — scrub daemon emits ScrubCycleComplete records
- ffs-repair/src/codec.rs — repair pipeline emits RepairAttempted/Succeeded/Failed
- Bayesian autopilot — emits PolicyDecision records when adjusting overhead
- Mount/unmount — session boundary markers

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] Every corruption detection produces a JSONL evidence record
- [ ] Every repair attempt (success or failure) produces a JSONL evidence record
- [ ] Every scrub cycle produces a summary JSONL record
- [ ] Records survive crash (fsync on critical events)
- [ ] ffs-cli evidence show can pretty-print the ledger
- [ ] Records are machine-parseable (valid JSON per line)
- [ ] Unit tests: serialize/deserialize round-trip for all event types

title: Implement adaptive overhead ratio via Bayesian expected-loss
description:
# Implement adaptive overhead ratio via Bayesian expected-loss

## GOAL
Replace ad-hoc threshold selection for RaptorQ overhead ratio with principled Bayesian decision theory. The system automatically adjusts repair symbol overhead (3-10%) based on observed corruption rate, using a formal expected-loss minimization framework.

## BACKGROUND
Current state: fixed overhead ratio. This is suboptimal — low-corruption drives waste space on repair symbols, while high-corruption drives may have insufficient redundancy. The alien artifact methodology demands principled, auditable policy decisions.

## MATHEMATICAL FRAMEWORK

### Beta Posterior over Corruption Probability
- Prior: Beta(alpha_0, beta_0) — weakly informative, e.g., Beta(1, 100)
- Observation model: each scrub cycle observes k corrupted blocks out of n checked
- Posterior: Beta(alpha_0 + k, beta_0 + n - k)
- Posterior mean: (alpha_0 + k) / (alpha_0 + beta_0 + n)

### Chernoff-Style Unrecoverable Risk Bound
- P(unrecoverable | overhead_ratio) = P(corrupted_blocks > decodable_symbols)
- For RaptorQ with overhead_ratio r and block group size B:
  - decodable_symbols = floor(r * B)
  - P(unrecoverable) = P(Binomial(B, p_corruption) > decodable_symbols)
- Compute via Beta-Binomial tail probability

### Expected-Loss Policy
- E[loss(overhead_ratio)] = P(unrecoverable) * data_loss_cost + overhead_ratio * storage_cost
- Minimize over overhead_ratio in [0.03, 0.10]
- data_loss_cost: configurable, default 1e6 (in arbitrary units)
- storage_cost: configurable, default 1.0 per unit overhead

### Metadata Elevation
- Superblock and GDT block groups get 2x overhead multiplier
- Rationale: metadata loss is catastrophic (entire FS unrecoverable vs single file loss)

### Update Schedule
- Recompute optimal overhead after each scrub cycle
- Apply lazily: next symbol refresh uses new ratio
- Log PolicyDecision evidence record with full posterior parameters

## IMPLEMENTATION

### Data Structures
```rust
struct DurabilityAutopilot {
    alpha: f64,
    beta: f64,
    data_loss_cost: f64,
    storage_cost: f64,
    min_overhead: f64,  // 0.03
    max_overhead: f64,  // 0.10
    metadata_multiplier: f64,  // 2.0
}
```

### Key Functions
- update_posterior(corrupted: u64, checked: u64) — Bayesian update
- optimal_overhead() -> f64 — minimize expected loss
- optimal_overhead_metadata() -> f64 — with elevation multiplier
- risk_bound(overhead: f64) -> f64 — P(unrecoverable)
- expected_loss(overhead: f64) -> f64 — full loss computation

### Location
- New module: ffs-repair/src/autopilot.rs
- Integration: scrub daemon calls update_posterior after each cycle
- Integration: symbol refresh reads optimal_overhead before encoding

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] Beta posterior correctly updated after each scrub cycle
- [ ] Expected-loss minimization selects overhead in [3%, 10%] range
- [ ] Metadata groups get 2x overhead multiplier
- [ ] PolicyDecision evidence records logged for every adjustment
- [ ] Unit tests: known corruption rates produce expected overhead selections
- [ ] Unit tests: edge cases (zero corruption, 100% corruption, single block group)
- [ ] Property test: optimal_overhead monotonically increases with corruption rate
notes:
## LOGGING REQUIREMENTS
- TRACE target=ffs::repair::policy: fields checked_blocks, corrupted_blocks, groups_total on scrub-cycle observation intake.
- DEBUG target=ffs::repair::policy: fields posterior_alpha, posterior_beta, posterior_mean after posterior updates.
- INFO target=ffs::repair::policy: fields group, metadata_group, selected_overhead, symbol_count, risk_bound, expected_loss for each policy decision.
- WARN target=ffs::repair::policy: fields group, selected_symbols, layout_capacity, effective_overhead when clamping to reserved repair-block capacity.
- ERROR target=ffs::repair::policy: fields group, error when policy evidence append fails.
close_reason: Implemented adaptive Bayesian overhead autopilot in ffs-repair with tests and full gate pass.

title: Add stress test suite for concurrent MVCC operations
description:
# Add stress test suite for concurrent MVCC operations

## GOAL
Create stress tests that validate MVCC correctness under heavy concurrent load.

## BACKGROUND
MVCC is the heart of FrankenFS. Bugs here cause silent data corruption.
Stress tests must exercise:
- Concurrent readers with writers
- Multiple concurrent writers
- FCW conflict detection
- SSI write-skew detection
- GC under load
- Version chain growth/pruning

## TECHNICAL REQUIREMENTS

### 1. Test Harness Structure
```rust
#[cfg(test)]
mod stress_tests {
    use asupersync::lab::Lab;
    
    #[test]
    fn stress_concurrent_rw() {
        let lab = Lab::new(SEED);
        lab.run(|| async {
            let store = MvccStore::new();
            
            // Spawn readers
            for _ in 0..100 {
                lab.spawn(reader_task(&store));
            }
            
            // Spawn writers
            for _ in 0..10 {
                lab.spawn(writer_task(&store));
            }
            
            lab.run_for(Duration::from_secs(60));
            
            verify_invariants(&store);
        });
    }
}
```

### 2. Invariants to Verify
1. **No lost writes**: Every committed write visible in correct snapshot
2. **Snapshot isolation**: Reader sees consistent state
3. **FCW correctness**: Conflicts detected, no silent overwrite
4. **SSI correctness**: Write-skew impossible
5. **GC safety**: No premature version pruning
6. **Memory bounded**: Version chains do not grow unbounded

### 3. Workload Patterns
- **Random**: Random blocks, random operations
- **Hotspot**: 90% ops on 10% of blocks
- **Sequential**: Sequential block access
- **Adversarial**: Maximize conflicts

### 4. Deterministic Replay
- Use Lab runtime for reproducible scheduling
- Seed-based RNG for deterministic operations
- Log operations for replay on failure

## TESTS
1. stress_concurrent_rw (1000 ops, 10 threads)
2. stress_fcw_conflicts (100% conflict rate)
3. stress_ssi_write_skew (classic scenarios)
4. stress_gc_under_load (continuous GC)
5. stress_version_chain_growth (max versions)

## LOGGING
- Operation log (trace): op, block, txn_id, result
- Invariant check (debug): what checked, result
- Failure (error): full state dump

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. No invariant violations in 1-hour run
2. Deterministic replay reproduces any failure
3. Memory usage bounded
4. Performance degrades gracefully under load

title: RCU/QSBR for lock-free metadata reads (Graveyard Entry 14.8)
description:
# RCU/QSBR for lock-free metadata reads (Graveyard Entry 14.8)

## GOAL
Implement Read-Copy-Update (RCU) with Quiescent-State-Based Reclamation (QSBR) for lock-free metadata reads. Metadata-heavy operations (readdir, stat, lookup) should never block on writers.

## BACKGROUND
From Alien CS Graveyard 14.8: RCU allows readers to access shared data without locks. Writers create new versions and publish them atomically. Old versions are freed after all readers finish (quiescent state detection via QSBR).

## DESIGN
- Protect: inode cache, directory entry cache, extent index lookups
- Read path: no locks, no atomic increments, just dereference current pointer
- Write path: copy-on-write, publish new version via atomic pointer swap
- Reclamation: QSBR — each thread periodically announces quiescent state
- crossbeam-epoch provides QSBR in Rust (same infrastructure as EBR for MVCC)

## APPLICABILITY
- Complements MVCC (RCU for metadata, MVCC for data blocks)
- High-value for workloads with many concurrent readers (web servers, build systems)

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] Metadata reads (stat, readdir) never block on concurrent writes
- [ ] No reader-side locks or atomic increments
- [ ] Old metadata versions freed after QSBR quiescent period
- [ ] Benchmark: concurrent stat() throughput vs locked approach
acceptance_criteria: Metadata reads (stat, readdir) never block on concurrent writes. No reader-side locks or atomic increments on hot path. Old metadata versions freed after QSBR quiescent period via crossbeam-epoch. Benchmark: concurrent stat() throughput vs rwlock-protected approach at 1,2,4,8,16 threads. Unit tests: concurrent read+write with no reader blocking. Unit tests: reclamation after quiescent period. Unit tests: no use-after-free under Miri. Integration test: readdir under concurrent file creation does not block or return stale data.

title: Implement btrfs COW B-tree mutation (insert/delete/split/merge)

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: BTRFS COW SEMANTICS: btrfs B-tree mutation is inherently COW—every modification creates a new node and updates the parent's pointer. This propagates up to the root, which gets a new generation number. This is fundamentally different from ext4 extent trees (in-place mutation + journal). The COW chain must be atomic: either all nodes in the chain are updated, or none are.

title: Implement degradation level display in TUI dashboard
description:
# Implement degradation level display in TUI dashboard

## GOAL
Add degradation level and pressure indicators to the TUI dashboard so operators can monitor system health.

## BACKGROUND
The graceful degradation system (bd-3tz) produces state that must be visible:
- Current degradation level (0-4)
- CPU/memory/IO pressure gauges
- Recent degradation events

## TECHNICAL REQUIREMENTS

### 1. Extended DashboardSnapshot
```rust
pub struct DashboardSnapshot {
    // ... existing fields ...
    
    // Degradation state
    pub degradation_level: DegradationLevel,
    pub cpu_pressure: f64,      // 0.0 - 1.0
    pub memory_pressure: f64,   // 0.0 - 1.0
    pub io_queue_depth: usize,
    pub degradation_events: Vec<DegradationEvent>,
}
```

### 2. New Panel: System Health
```
┌─ System Health ─────────────────┐
│ Degradation: NORMAL (Level 0)   │
│                                 │
│ CPU:    [████████░░] 78%        │
│ Memory: [██████░░░░] 62%        │
│ I/O:    [███░░░░░░░] 34%        │
│                                 │
│ Recent events:                  │
│   12:34:05 Level 0 → 1 (CPU)    │
│   12:35:22 Level 1 → 0          │
└─────────────────────────────────┘
```

### 3. Color Coding
- Level 0 (Normal): Green
- Level 1 (Background paused): Yellow
- Level 2 (Cache reduced): Orange
- Level 3 (Writes throttled): Red
- Level 4 (Read-only): Blinking Red

### 4. Gauge Widgets
```rust
fn render_pressure_gauge(value: f64, width: u16) -> String {
    let filled = (value * width as f64) as u16;
    let bar = "█".repeat(filled as usize) + &"░".repeat((width - filled) as usize);
    format!("[{}] {:>3}%", bar, (value * 100.0) as u8)
}
```

## TESTS
1. Unit: Gauge rendering at various values
2. Unit: Color coding for each level
3. Integration: Dashboard updates on level change
4. Visual: Screenshot test for layout

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. Degradation level always visible
2. Pressure gauges update in real-time
3. Event history shows last 10 events
4. Colors distinguish severity

title: EPIC: S3-FIFO for ARC Buffer/Cache Eviction (Graveyard Entry 15.1)
description:
# EPIC: S3-FIFO for ARC Buffer/Cache Eviction (Graveyard Entry 15.1)

## PURPOSE
Replace the current ARC (Adaptive Replacement Cache) buffer cache eviction policy with S3-FIFO, a simpler and often superior eviction algorithm. S3-FIFO uses three FIFO queues — small (admission filter), main, and ghost — with posterior-guided adaptive queue splitting.

## BACKGROUND
From the Alien CS Graveyard entry 15.1:
- S3-FIFO (Yang et al., 2023): three-queue FIFO eviction with admission filter
- Often matches or beats ARC on filesystem workloads with simpler implementation
- No per-access metadata update (unlike ARC which adjusts T1/T2 split on every access)
- Ghost queue provides scan resistance without LRU overhead

## DESIGN

### Three Queues
1. Small Queue (S): admission filter, ~10% of total cache
   - New blocks enter here
   - Evicted from S on first access = not promoted (scan filter)
   - Evicted from S with re-access = promoted to Main
2. Main Queue (M): ~90% of total cache
   - Blocks promoted from S
   - FIFO eviction (no reordering on access)
   - Frequency counter: blocks with count > 0 get second chance
3. Ghost Queue (G): metadata-only (no data), tracks recently evicted
   - Stores block IDs of recently evicted blocks
   - Hit in ghost = block is popular, immediate promotion to Main on re-admission

### Posterior-Guided Adaptive Split
- Bayesian posterior over optimal S/M split ratio
- Update based on ghost hit rate: high ghost hits = S too small
- This extends vanilla S3-FIFO with principled adaptation (alien artifact methodology)

### Drop-In Replacement
- Same BlockCache trait interface as current ARC
- get(block_id) -> Option<BlockData>
- insert(block_id, data) -> Option<(block_id, data)> // evicted entry
- Configurability: total_capacity, small_ratio (default 0.1)

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] S3-FIFO implements BlockCache trait
- [ ] Drop-in replacement for ARC (feature flag to select)
- [ ] Benchmark: hit rate comparison vs ARC on filesystem trace
- [ ] Ghost queue provides scan resistance
- [ ] Adaptive split improves over fixed 10/90 split
- [ ] Unit tests: basic FIFO eviction, promotion, ghost hits

## Success Criteria
1. All children closed
2. E2E cache test (bd-1c4y.3) passes
3. S3-FIFO resists sequential scan pollution
4. Hit rate improvement over plain ARC on mixed workloads
5. No unbounded memory growth in ghost queue
close_reason: All children completed

title: Implement S3-FIFO eviction for ffs-block ARC cache
description:
# Implement S3-FIFO eviction for ffs-block ARC cache

## GOAL
Implement the S3-FIFO eviction algorithm as a drop-in replacement for the current ARC cache in the ffs-block crate.

## DESIGN

### Data Structures
```rust
struct S3FifoCache<K, V> {
    small: VecDeque<(K, V, u8)>,      // (key, value, access_count)
    main: VecDeque<(K, V, u8)>,       // (key, value, access_count)
    ghost: VecDeque<K>,                // key only, no data
    index: HashMap<K, QueueLocation>, // O(1) lookup
    small_capacity: usize,            // ~10% of total
    main_capacity: usize,             // ~90% of total
    ghost_capacity: usize,            // same as total
}
```

### Operations
- get(key): lookup in index, increment access_count, return data
- insert(key, value):
  1. If key in ghost: insert directly to Main (popular block returning)
  2. Else: insert to Small
  3. If Small full: evict from Small
     - If evicted had access_count > 0: promote to Main
     - Else: move to Ghost, discard data
  4. If Main full: evict from Main
     - If evicted had access_count > 0: re-insert at tail (second chance), decrement count
     - Else: discard

### Integration
- Feature flag: --features s3fifo to select S3-FIFO over ARC
- Same BlockCache trait interface
- Location: ffs-block/src/cache/s3fifo.rs

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] Implements BlockCache trait identically to ARC
- [ ] Small queue filters one-hit-wonder scans
- [ ] Main queue provides frequency-based retention
- [ ] Ghost queue enables fast re-admission of popular evicted blocks
- [ ] Feature flag selects S3-FIFO vs ARC at compile time
- [ ] Unit tests: insert, get, eviction order, ghost hit promotion
- [ ] Property tests: cache never exceeds capacity
