title: Create ext4 golden fixture images
description:
# Create ext4 golden fixture images

## GOAL
Expand the ext4 "real image" corpus used by conformance + E2E, with a documented, reproducible generator workflow.

This repo already has:
- Sparse ext4 fixtures in `conformance/fixtures/*.json`.
- One kernel-derived golden reference JSON in `conformance/golden/ext4_8mb_reference.json` (validated by `crates/ffs-harness/tests/kernel_reference.rs`).

This bead extends that foundation with additional ext4 variants so read-path features (allocator/orphans/journal integration) have stronger regression coverage.

## CANONICAL PATHS (USE THESE EVERYWHERE)
- Sparse fixtures: `conformance/fixtures/*.json` + `conformance/fixtures/checksums.sha256`
- Kernel reference goldens: `conformance/golden/*.json` + `conformance/golden/checksums.sha256`
- Generated binary images (not checked in by default): `conformance/golden/*.ext4` (see `conformance/golden/.gitignore`)

## DELIVERABLES
1. Add at least 2 additional ext4 kernel-reference goldens, each with:
   - A deterministic generation recipe (mkfs parameters + debugfs population commands).
   - A checked-in golden JSON file in `conformance/golden/` using the existing `ffs-harness::GoldenReference` schema.
   - A checksum entry in `conformance/golden/checksums.sha256`.
2. Extend harness tests so these goldens are meaningful regression gates:
   - Either extend `crates/ffs-harness/tests/kernel_reference.rs` or add a new ext4-variants test.
   - For each variant: generate the image at test time, parse it with `ffs_ondisk::Ext4ImageReader`, and compare against the golden JSON.
   - On mismatch, log field-level diffs (path/field/expected/actual) so failures are actionable.
3. Add a script to (re)generate all ext4 golden JSONs with detailed logging:
   - Preferred: extend `scripts/capture_ext4_reference.sh` to support variants.
   - Alternative: add `scripts/capture_ext4_references.sh` that produces all ext4 golden JSONs into `conformance/golden/`.
4. Ensure `scripts/verify_golden.sh --update` continues to work with the new JSON files.

## IMAGE VARIANTS (EXAMPLES, PICK 2+)
- A larger geometry image (e.g., 64MiB, 4KiB blocks) to vary group counts.
- `dir_index` enabled with a directory containing enough entries to force htree behavior.
- `sparse_super` enabled.
- A "nearly-full" image to stress free-block accounting.

## LOGGING REQUIREMENTS
- Scripts print tool versions, timestamps, and every command executed.
- Tests print the exact mkfs/debugfs parameters used.

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. New ext4 golden JSON files are checked in and covered by `conformance/golden/checksums.sha256`.
2. Harness tests compare generated images to those goldens and fail on drift.
3. `scripts/verify_golden.sh` passes in a clean tree.
4. A developer can intentionally regenerate goldens using the documented script(s).
close_reason: Added multiple ext4 golden variants, variant conformance tests, and capture script; all gates + verify_golden passed

title: Capture baseline p50/p95/p99 for read/write/fsync/mount
description:
# Capture baseline p50/p95/p99 for read/write/fsync/mount

## GOAL
Establish performance baselines for all critical filesystem operations so that future changes can be measured against a known reference point. Without baselines, we cannot detect performance regressions.

## OPERATIONS TO BENCHMARK

### Read Path
- Sequential read: 4KB blocks, 100MB file, measure IOPS and throughput
- Random read: 4KB blocks, uniform random offset, measure p50/p95/p99 latency
- Metadata read: stat() 10000 files, readdir() 1000 directories

### Write Path (once available)
- Sequential write: 4KB blocks, 100MB file
- Random write: 4KB blocks, uniform random offset
- Small file create: 10000 files, 4KB each
- mkdir: 1000 directories

### fsync
- fsync after single write
- fsync after 100 writes (batch)
- fdatasync vs fsync comparison

### Mount
- Cold mount: time from mount command to first FUSE response
- Warm mount: with existing cache/journal
- Recovery mount: after unclean shutdown

## OUTPUT FORMAT
- JSON report with all metrics
- Compare against previous baselines (stored in repo)
- CI integration: fail if p99 regresses > 20%

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] Baselines captured for all listed operations
- [ ] Results stored as JSON artifacts in repo
- [ ] Benchmark harness is deterministic and reproducible
- [ ] CI gate: alerts on regression > 20% from baseline
- [ ] Documentation: how to run benchmarks locally
notes: IMPLEMENTATION GUIDANCE: Use criterion.rs for benchmark harness. Each benchmark should produce JSON output with schema: {operation, metric, p50_us, p95_us, p99_us, throughput_ops_sec, throughput_mb_sec}. Store baseline results in artifacts/baselines/perf_baseline.json. CI regression check compares current run against stored baseline. For mount latency, measure wall-clock time from process start to first successful FUSE stat() response. For read/write benchmarks, use 256MB test image with known file layout (1000 files, deterministic content). Random read benchmark must use uniform random with fixed seed for reproducibility.
close_reason: Completed: baseline artifacts now include measured read/write/fsync/mount metrics with canonical JSON updates and verified golden gate

title: CI artifact gates for build and test outputs
description:
# CI artifact gates for build and test outputs

## GOAL
Implement CI gates that enforce artifact quality standards: binary size limits, test coverage thresholds, benchmark regression detection, and golden output verification.

## GATES

### Binary Size Gate
- Track release binary size per commit
- Fail if binary grows > 10% from baseline without explicit override
- Report size breakdown by crate (cargo bloat integration)

### Test Coverage Gate
- Minimum line coverage threshold: 60% (aspirational, increase over time)
- Per-crate coverage tracking
- Fail if coverage drops > 5% from previous commit

### Benchmark Regression Gate
- Run performance benchmarks on every PR
- Compare against stored baselines
- Fail if p99 latency regresses > 20%

### Golden Output Gate
- Conformance test outputs compared against golden checksums
- Fail if any golden output changes without explicit update

### Artifact Archive
- Store benchmark results, coverage reports, binary sizes as CI artifacts
- Historical tracking for trend analysis

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] All 4 gates implemented and running in CI
- [ ] Gates produce clear pass/fail with actionable error messages
- [ ] Override mechanism for intentional gate violations (explicit annotation)
- [ ] Historical artifacts accessible for trend analysis
notes: IMPLEMENTATION GUIDANCE: Binary size gate should use cargo bloat for per-crate breakdown. Store baseline binary sizes in artifacts/baselines/binary_size.json. Test coverage gate should use cargo-llvm-cov with JSON output. Benchmark regression gate should integrate with criterion.rs baseline comparison. Golden output gate should compute BLAKE3 checksums of conformance test outputs and compare against artifacts/goldens/checksums.json. Override mechanism: add GATE_OVERRIDE=binary_size to PR description to bypass specific gates, with mandatory reviewer approval.

title: Decision Contract for repair policy (clean/minor/severe/io_stall states)
description:
# Decision Contract for repair policy (clean/minor/severe/io_stall states)

## GOAL
Define a formal decision contract for the self-healing repair policy. The contract specifies: states the system can be in, actions available in each state, loss function for each action, and calibration requirements.

## DECISION CONTRACT

### States S
- clean: no corruption detected, all checksums pass
- minor_corruption: < 1% blocks corrupted, repair symbols sufficient
- severe_corruption: 1-5% blocks corrupted, repair may be insufficient
- io_stall: I/O errors prevent reading some blocks (hardware degradation)

### Actions A
- repair_local: repair using local RaptorQ symbols (low cost, bounded capability)
- repair_global: full block group re-encode (higher cost, resets redundancy)
- defer_repair: schedule repair for next scrub cycle (risk of further degradation)
- degrade_readonly: switch to read-only mode (preserve data, lose writability)
- emergency_backup: trigger backup to external storage (highest cost, highest safety)

### Loss Function L(state, action)
- L(clean, repair_local) = overhead_only (unnecessary work)
- L(minor, repair_local) = small (expected case, low risk)
- L(minor, defer) = medium (corruption may spread)
- L(severe, repair_local) = medium (may not suffice)
- L(severe, defer) = high (data loss risk)
- L(io_stall, any_repair) = depends on I/O availability
- L(any, degrade_readonly) = writability_cost (acceptable for safety)

### Calibration
- Conformal coverage on corruption-risk estimates
- Posterior predictive check: does the Beta posterior accurately predict next-cycle corruption?
- If calibration fails: fall back to conservative policy (eager repair, higher overhead)

### Safety Envelope
- If corruption posterior crosses hard safety threshold (P > 0.05): force immediate repair
- If repair symbols insufficient: switch to read-only + alert
- Never silently continue with known unrecoverable corruption

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] Decision contract formally specified in code (enum states, actions, loss)
- [ ] Policy engine selects optimal action given current state and loss function
- [ ] Conformal calibration check implemented
- [ ] Safety envelope enforced (hard thresholds)
- [ ] Evidence record for every policy decision
- [ ] Unit tests: each state/action combination produces expected behavior

title: E2E test: full conformance gate pass (all golden checksums, all parsers, all fixtures)
description:
# E2E test: full conformance gate pass

## GOAL
E2E test for the Conformance and Quality Infrastructure epic (bd-2jk). Runs all conformance checks in a single pass: golden checksum verification, parser roundtrip, fixture validation, and feature parity check.

## TEST SCENARIO
1. Load all ext4 and btrfs golden fixture images
2. Parse every fixture with all on-disk parsers
3. Verify parsed output matches golden checksums (bd-2jk.5)
4. Run proptest corpus (bd-2jk.4) if available
5. Run fuzz corpus (bd-2jk.7) if available
6. Generate ParityReport (bd-2jk.9) and verify no regressions
7. Exit 0 if all gates pass, exit 1 with detailed failure report

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. Single command runs all conformance checks
2. Failure report identifies exact failing gate
3. Runs in CI in < 60 seconds
4. Deterministic
close_reason: Implemented full_conformance_gate_pass test in conformance.rs: validates all 12 fixtures, both checksum manifests, all 5 golden references, fuzz corpus (>=50 samples), ParityReport 100% coverage with consistency, deterministic execution, and <60s time bound. All 12 conformance tests pass.

title: Support ext4 non-contiguous journal extents for recovery benchmarkability
description:
## Goal
Enable FrankenFS to open journal-enabled ext4 images produced by default mkfs.ext4 so mount recovery paths can be benchmarked and exercised.

## Blocking Evidence
- In bd-2jk.10 benchmarking work, journal-enabled ext4 images (16/32/64/128 MiB) created with mkfs.ext4 -F -O extent,filetype all fail open/inspect with:
  unsupported feature: non-contiguous ext4 journal extents are not supported
- This blocks mount_recovery benchmark automation independently of FUSE host policy.

## Scope
1. Extend ext4 journal extent handling to support non-contiguous journal extents used by standard mkfs layouts.
2. Add parser/unit tests with fixtures covering both contiguous and non-contiguous journal layouts.
3. Add conformance check proving ffs-cli inspect succeeds on default journal-enabled mkfs ext4 images.
4. Update benchmark docs/notes once mount_recovery can be measured.

## Acceptance Criteria
- target/release/ffs-cli inspect <journal-enabled-ext4> --json succeeds for at least one default mkfs image.
- Recovery metadata/journal replay probe path is executable for benchmark automation.
- Tests cover failure and success cases for journal extent layout parsing.
close_reason: Completed

- crates/ffs-core/src/lib.rs: maybe_replay_ext4_journal now maps all journal inode extents into ordered JournalSegment list and calls replay_jbd2_segments instead of rejecting non-contiguous physical layout.
- Added regression tests:

  - ffs-journal: replay_jbd2_segments_rejects_empty_or_zero_length_segments

title: Create btrfs golden fixture images
description:
# Create btrfs golden fixture images

## GOAL
Build a btrfs conformance corpus that is grounded in *real* btrfs on-disk bytes, without forcing us to check large binary images into git.

The repo already has some btrfs fixtures in `conformance/fixtures/` (superblock + sys_chunk mapping + a leaf node). This bead expands that into the minimum set needed to support:
- btrfs read-only mount work (bd-375)
- deterministic regression coverage for tree-walk, inode lookup, dir enumeration, and extent resolution

## CANONICAL PATHS (USE THESE EVERYWHERE)
- Sparse fixtures: `conformance/fixtures/*.json` + `conformance/fixtures/checksums.sha256`
- Any checked-in "golden" JSON artifacts (if added): `conformance/golden/*.json` + `conformance/golden/checksums.sha256`
- Large binary images (NOT checked in): generate into `artifacts/` or `conformance/golden/` and keep them ignored

## STRATEGY (PREFERRED)
1. Generate or obtain a single-device btrfs image *locally* (not committed).
2. Extract only the required byte ranges (superblock, tree blocks, leaves) into small JSON fixtures.
3. Add harness tests that parse those fixtures and assert invariants + key field values.

This matches the "sparse fixture" workflow described in `bd-3q4`.

## DELIVERABLES
1. A documented recipe to create the reference btrfs image (local-only):
   - `scripts/fixtures/make_btrfs_reference_image.sh`
   - Must state prerequisites (`btrfs-progs`) and whether `sudo mount -o loop` is required.
   - Must print detailed logs (tool versions, commands, timestamps).
2. New sparse fixtures extracted from that image (checked in):
   - At least 1 additional root-tree / chunk-tree related fixture beyond what exists today.
   - At least 1 fs-tree leaf fixture that includes:
     - an inode item
     - at least one dir item (so directory enumeration is testable)
     - at least one extent/data reference sufficient to drive read-path plumbing later
3. Update `conformance/fixtures/checksums.sha256` to include the new fixture JSON files.
4. Harness coverage:
   - Extend `crates/ffs-harness/tests/conformance.rs` and/or add targeted tests so each new fixture is parsed and validated.
   - Tests should assert ordering invariants (sorted keys), bounds checks, and "no panic" behavior.

## V1 SCOPE RULES (MUST BE ENFORCED BY FIXTURES)
- Single-device images only.
- No RAID/multi-device.
- Compression may exist on disk, but read-path support must be explicitly gated by V1 scope; fixtures should avoid requiring excluded features.

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. New btrfs fixtures are checked in, checksummed, and validated by harness tests.
2. Fixtures collectively cover the bytes needed to implement bd-375's read-only mount (tree-walk + dir listing + file read plumbing).
3. All validation failures are actionable (clear errors with offsets / keys), not opaque panics.

title: Establish hyperfine performance baselines
description:
# Establish hyperfine performance baselines

## GOAL
Record baseline performance measurements for key user workflows (CLI + harness) using hyperfine, and document regression thresholds.

## CONTEXT
This repo already has a benchmark entrypoint:
- `scripts/benchmark.sh`

It currently runs hyperfine for:
- `ffs-cli inspect` / `ffs-cli scrub` (if an ext4 reference image is present)
- `ffs-cli parity`
- `ffs-harness parity` / `ffs-harness check-fixtures`

This bead turns that into a reproducible baseline protocol with stored artifacts and clear comparison rules.

## DELIVERABLES
1. Baseline artifacts (checked in):
   - A dated baseline report under `baselines/` (Markdown), including:
     - commit SHA
     - hardware info
     - tool versions
     - the exact commands used
     - summary tables (mean/stddev/p95) per command
2. Machine-readable hyperfine exports (checked in):
   - Store JSON under `baselines/hyperfine/<YYYYMMDD>/...json`
   - Capture at least:
     - `ffs-cli inspect` on an ext4 reference image
     - `ffs-cli scrub` on an ext4 reference image
     - `ffs-cli parity`
     - `ffs-harness parity`
     - `ffs-harness check-fixtures`
3. Update `scripts/benchmark.sh` (or add `scripts/benchmark_record.sh`) to:
   - build release binaries once
   - run hyperfine with `--export-json` into the baseline directory
   - print a concise summary + paths to exported files

## FIXTURE INPUT
Benchmarks should use a stable ext4 reference image:
- Preferred: generate it via `scripts/capture_ext4_reference.sh conformance/golden` and point hyperfine at `conformance/golden/ext4_8mb_reference.ext4`.
- If the image is missing, the script should SKIP the inspect/scrub benchmarks with a clear message (but still benchmark parity commands).

## REGRESSION THRESHOLDS
Document (and implement in the benchmark script as an optional "compare" mode):
- Warn if p95 regresses >10% vs the last committed baseline.
- Fail (for optional CI / pre-merge runs) if p95 regresses >25%.

Note: hard CI gating should be opt-in unless running on a stable runner, because GitHub-hosted runners vary.

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. New baseline artifacts exist under `baselines/` and are reproducible.
2. Hyperfine JSON exports are stored and easy to diff.
3. The baseline protocol explicitly ties to conformance verification:
   - before/after any optimization run: `./scripts/verify_golden.sh` must pass.

title: Add proptest property-based tests for parsing
description:
# Add proptest property-based tests for parsing

## GOAL
Add property-based tests (proptest) for `ffs-ondisk` so we continuously enforce:
- "no panics on adversarial bytes"
- core structural invariants when parsing succeeds

This is the cheapest way to harden on-disk parsing against malformed/corrupted images.

## SCOPE
Target these parser entrypoints (real APIs in this repo):
- ext4:
  - `Ext4Superblock::parse_superblock_region`
  - `Ext4Superblock::parse_from_image`
  - extent tree parsing helpers (e.g. `parse_extent_tree`, `parse_inode_extent_tree`) where feasible
  - directory block parsing (`parse_dir_block`, `iter_dir_block`)
- btrfs:
  - `BtrfsSuperblock::parse_superblock_region`
  - `BtrfsSuperblock::parse_from_image`
  - `parse_sys_chunk_array`
  - `parse_leaf_items`, `parse_internal_items`
  - `BtrfsHeader::parse_from_block` + `BtrfsHeader::validate`

## DELIVERABLES
1. Add `proptest` as a dev-dependency:
   - `crates/ffs-ondisk/Cargo.toml` `[dev-dependencies]`
2. Add a dedicated proptest module in each file (no new file proliferation required):
   - `crates/ffs-ondisk/src/ext4.rs` (extend `#[cfg(test)] mod tests { ... }` or add a sibling `mod proptests`)
   - `crates/ffs-ondisk/src/btrfs.rs`
3. Ensure failures are reproducible:
   - print (or rely on proptest’s output) for seed + minimized case
   - document how to re-run a failing case (env vars like `PROPTEST_SEED`)

## TEST DESIGN
### 1. "No panic" fuzz
- Feed bounded random byte vectors into parse entrypoints.
- The test passes if the parser returns `Ok(_)` or `Err(_)`, and fails if it panics.

### 2. Structured generators for "success" paths
Random bytes rarely parse; to exercise invariants, use structured generators:
- ext4 superblock:
  - start from a minimal valid 1024-byte buffer (similar to `make_valid_sb()` in existing unit tests)
  - fuzz non-critical fields and edge boundaries
  - assert invariants on `Ok(sb)`:
    - `sb.block_size` is one of {1024, 2048, 4096}
    - `sb.blocks_per_group > 0`, `sb.inodes_per_group > 0`
    - if `sb.is_64bit()` then `sb.group_desc_size() >= 64`
- btrfs superblock:
  - create a minimally valid region with correct magic and power-of-two sizes
  - fuzz sys_chunk_array length within bounds
  - assert invariants on `Ok(sb)`:
    - `sb.sectorsize.is_power_of_two()` and `<= 256KiB`
    - `sb.nodesize.is_power_of_two()` and `<= 256KiB`

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. At least 10 proptests for ext4 parsing invariants.
2. At least 10 proptests for btrfs parsing invariants.
3. Tests run in a reasonable time (use bounded sizes; avoid unbounded vectors).
4. Any discovered panic is fixed at the parser layer (do not suppress).
close_reason: Completed: added 12 ext4 + 10 btrfs proptests, no-panic + structured invariants, and passed rch gates

title: Implement CI gate for golden checksum verification
description:
# Implement CI gate for golden checksum verification

## GOAL
Make CI enforce the canonical FrankenFS correctness gates on every push/PR, so behavior regressions cannot land silently.

## CANONICAL LOCAL GATE (SOURCE OF TRUTH)
This repo's canonical verification entrypoint is:
- `scripts/verify_golden.sh`

It verifies:
- `conformance/fixtures/checksums.sha256` integrity
- `conformance/golden/checksums.sha256` integrity
- `ParityReport` matches `FEATURE_PARITY.md`
- harness conformance tests
- golden JSON structural validity

## CURRENT STATE (NEEDS FIXING)
A workflow exists at `.github/workflows/ci.yml`, but it is not fully aligned with the canonical conformance paths (`conformance/*`) and canonical script (`scripts/verify_golden.sh`).

This bead is to **correct** CI wiring and make it match the documented gate.

## DELIVERABLES
1. Update `.github/workflows/ci.yml` to run, at minimum:
   - `cargo fmt --check`
   - `cargo check --all-targets`
   - `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings`
   - `cargo test --workspace`
   - `./scripts/verify_golden.sh`
2. Optional but strongly recommended CI step:
   - `SKIP_MOUNT=1 ./scripts/e2e/ffs_smoke.sh`
   - This provides a black-box "build + CLI sanity" check even when `/dev/fuse` is not available.
3. Fix any workflow steps that reference legacy fixture locations (for example `tests/fixtures/...`). The canonical fixture locations are under `conformance/`.
4. Add/extend a small harness test that ensures the golden manifest stays complete:
   - every `conformance/golden/*.json` is listed in `conformance/golden/checksums.sha256`
   - every listed file exists and is non-empty

## CI LOGGING REQUIREMENTS
Every CI job MUST output detailed diagnostic information:

### Environment Logging (at job start)
```yaml
- name: Environment
  run: |
    echo "::group::System Info"
    uname -a
    cat /etc/os-release 2>/dev/null || true
    echo "::endgroup::"
    echo "::group::Rust Toolchain"
    rustc -Vv
    cargo -V
    echo "::endgroup::"
    echo "::group::Available Resources"
    nproc
    free -h 2>/dev/null || true
    df -h .
    echo "::endgroup::"
```

### Test Output Formatting
```yaml
env:
  RUST_BACKTRACE: 1
  CARGO_TERM_COLOR: always
  RUST_LOG: ffs=debug
```

### Failure Artifacts
On failure, upload:
- `target/debug/deps/ffs-*.d` (dependency info)
- `conformance/golden/*.json` (current state)
- `conformance/fixtures/checksums.sha256` (expected)
- Test output log

### Step Annotations
Use `::error::` and `::warning::` annotations for clear PR feedback:
```bash
if ! sha256sum -c conformance/fixtures/checksums.sha256; then
  echo "::error file=conformance/fixtures/checksums.sha256::Checksum mismatch - fixtures changed without updating checksums"
  exit 1
fi
```

## NON-GOALS
- CI does not regenerate goldens automatically.
- CI does not require `/dev/fuse`.

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. A PR with a checksum mismatch fails in CI with a clear error.
2. A PR that changes fixture/golden JSON without updating checksums fails.
3. A PR that changes conformance behavior fails via harness tests.
4. CI runtime remains reasonable (no huge image generation by default).
5. CI output is parseable and provides actionable failure messages.
close_reason: Wired CI to canonical golden gate and added golden checksum manifest coverage; local verify_golden/smoke still fail due existing behavioral baseline issues in current dirty workspace

title: Add E2E smoke tests (CLI + ext4 RO mount) with detailed logging
description:
# Add E2E smoke tests (CLI + ext4 RO mount) with detailed logging

## GOAL
Add reproducible end-to-end smoke scripts that exercise the user-facing workflows:
- `ffs inspect`
- `ffs scrub`
- `ffs parity`
- `ffs mount` (ext4 read-only) + basic file operations through the mount

The scripts must produce high-quality logs so failures are actionable.

## CONTEXT
We have strong harness/unit tests, but we are missing a single-command black-box validation that:
1. Builds the workspace
2. Runs the CLI on a real ext4 image
3. Mounts via FUSE and validates basic behavior
4. Captures structured logs + environment info

## DELIVERABLES
1. `scripts/e2e/ffs_smoke.sh`
2. `scripts/e2e/lib.sh` (shared helpers: logging, cleanup, assertions)
3. `scripts/e2e/README.md` (how to run locally + troubleshooting)

## FIXTURE INPUT (NO CHECKED-IN IMAGES REQUIRED)
To avoid committing binary images, the script should generate a fresh ext4 image in a temp dir using kernel tools (same approach as `crates/ffs-harness/tests/kernel_reference.rs`):
- Requires: `mkfs.ext4`, `debugfs`
- Create a small image (e.g., 8-32MiB)
- Populate with a known structure:
  - `/readme.txt`
  - `/testdir/hello.txt`

If tools are missing, the script must SKIP with a clear message and exit 0.

## SCRIPT REQUIREMENTS (NON-NEGOTIABLE)
- `set -euo pipefail`
- `IFS=$'\n\t'`
- `trap`-based cleanup that always unmounts and removes temp dirs
- Log directory: `artifacts/e2e/<timestamp>/`
- One combined log file: `artifacts/e2e/<timestamp>/run.log`
- Every major step prints:
  - Start/end timestamps
  - Command line
  - Exit code
  - Duration
- Always print environment header:
  - `uname -a`
  - `id`
  - `rustc -Vv`, `cargo -V`
  - `ls -l /dev/fuse` (if present)
  - `fusermount3 --version` (or `fusermount --version`) if available
- Always run with verbose Rust logs:
  - `RUST_LOG=trace`
  - `RUST_BACKTRACE=1`

## TEST MATRIX
### CLI
1. `cargo build --workspace`
2. `cargo run -p ffs-cli -- inspect <tmp>/e2e.ext4 --json`
3. `cargo run -p ffs-cli -- scrub <tmp>/e2e.ext4 --json`
4. `cargo run -p ffs-cli -- parity --json`

### FUSE ext4 read-only mount
1. If `/dev/fuse` is missing/unusable: SKIP mount section and log why.
2. Mount: `cargo run -p ffs-cli -- mount <tmp>/e2e.ext4 <mnt>`
3. Validate (bounded):
   - `ls -la <mnt>`
   - `find <mnt> -maxdepth 2 -type f -print`
   - `cat <mnt>/readme.txt`
4. Unmount:
   - Prefer `fusermount3 -u <mnt>`
   - Fallback `fusermount -u <mnt>`

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. Script is deterministic and idempotent.
2. On success, it exits 0 and prints a clear PASS summary.
3. On failure, it exits non-zero and prints:
   - failing command
   - path to log directory
   - last ~200 lines of the log
4. Mount cleanup works even on failure (no leaked mounts).
5. Runtime <5 minutes on a typical dev laptop.

title: Add adversarial fuzz corpus for all on-disk parsers
description:
# Add adversarial fuzz corpus for all on-disk parsers

## GOAL
Generate and maintain a corpus of adversarial byte sequences that exercise edge cases, boundary conditions, and malformed inputs for every parser in ffs-ondisk (ext4 + btrfs).

## BACKGROUND
From AGENTS.md: "Conformance-first, proof-first for risky logic." Parsers are the attack surface where malformed disk images could cause panics, infinite loops, or incorrect state. proptest (bd-2jk.4) gives property-based coverage, but we also need targeted adversarial inputs:
- Truncated structures (header says N items but data is shorter)
- Magic bytes correct but all other fields maximized/zeroed
- Circular extent tree references
- Overlapping directory entries
- Checksums that validate but data is semantically invalid

## DELIVERABLES
1. `tests/fuzz_corpus/` directory with adversarial .bin files
2. Rust test harness that feeds each .bin through every parser entry point
3. Verify: no panics, all errors returned cleanly via Result
4. Coverage report showing which parser code paths are hit

## TESTS (REQUIRED)
- Each .bin file produces a clean error (not a panic)
- 100% of ParseError variants exercised
- Zero panics on any input under 64KB

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. Corpus of ≥50 adversarial inputs
2. All parsers handle every input gracefully
3. Test suite runs in <5s
4. Integrated into `cargo test --workspace`
close_reason: Added 55-sample adversarial fuzz corpus and parser panic-safe harness in ffs-ondisk; fmt/check/clippy/test passed via rch

title: Implement E2E test framework with fixture lifecycle and detailed logging
description:
# Implement E2E test framework with fixture lifecycle and detailed logging

## GOAL
Build a reusable E2E test framework that all E2E test suites (ext4 RW, btrfs RW, FUSE, corruption recovery, degradation) can use. This avoids duplicating test infrastructure across beads.

## BACKGROUND
Multiple beads require E2E tests:
- bd-huh.5: ext4 write path E2E
- bd-2ju: btrfs RW E2E
- bd-3en1: FUSE runtime E2E
- bd-15c.4: corruption recovery E2E
- bd-lqwc: degradation E2E
- bd-375.3: btrfs RO mount E2E

All need:
1. Fixture image creation (ext4/btrfs with known content)
2. Mount/unmount lifecycle management
3. File operation execution and verification
4. Detailed structured logging (JSON)
5. Cleanup guarantees (unmount even on test failure)
6. Artifact collection (logs, evidence, flamegraphs)

## DELIVERABLES
1. `E2eTestContext` struct in ffs-harness:
   - `new(name, image_type, options)` → creates temp image
   - `mount(&self) -> MountHandle` → mounts via FUSE
   - `verify_file(&self, path, expected_content)` → read-and-compare
   - `inject_corruption(&self, block, bytes)` → flip bits
   - `collect_artifacts(&self, dir)` → save logs + evidence
2. `MountHandle`:
   - RAII: unmounts on drop
   - `exec_in_mount(|path| { ... })` → run closures inside mount
3. Structured JSON logging (see LOGGING REQUIREMENTS below)
4. `scripts/run_e2e.sh`: Shell wrapper that runs all E2E suites with cleanup

## TESTS
1. Framework can create ext4 + btrfs fixture images
2. Mount + unmount lifecycle works without leaks
3. Corruption injection produces detectable corruption
4. Artifact collection captures all logs
5. Cleanup runs even on panic (catch_unwind)

## LOGGING REQUIREMENTS
Every E2E test step MUST emit structured JSON logs:
```json
{"ts":"2026-02-13T04:00:00Z","test":"test_ext4_write","step":"create_fixture","input":{"fs":"ext4","size_mb":64},"output":{"path":"/tmp/ffs-e2e-abc123/image.ext4"},"duration_us":12345,"status":"ok"}
{"ts":"2026-02-13T04:00:01Z","test":"test_ext4_write","step":"mount","input":{"image":"/tmp/ffs-e2e-abc123/image.ext4","mountpoint":"/tmp/ffs-e2e-abc123/mnt"},"output":{"pid":1234},"duration_us":5678,"status":"ok"}
{"ts":"2026-02-13T04:00:02Z","test":"test_ext4_write","step":"write_file","input":{"path":"test.txt","size":1024},"output":{"bytes_written":1024},"duration_us":890,"status":"ok"}
{"ts":"2026-02-13T04:00:03Z","test":"test_ext4_write","step":"verify_file","input":{"path":"test.txt","expected_sha256":"abc..."},"output":{"actual_sha256":"abc...","match":true},"duration_us":456,"status":"ok"}
{"ts":"2026-02-13T04:00:04Z","test":"test_ext4_write","step":"unmount","input":{"mountpoint":"/tmp/ffs-e2e-abc123/mnt"},"output":{},"duration_us":2345,"status":"ok"}
```

Required log fields:
- `ts`: ISO8601 timestamp
- `test`: test name
- `step`: step within test
- `input`: step input (JSON object)
- `output`: step output (JSON object)
- `duration_us`: microseconds
- `status`: "ok" | "error" | "skip"
- `error`: (if status=error) error message

On test failure, dump full log to: `artifacts/{test_name}/e2e_log.json`

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. All E2E test beads can use this framework
2. No test fixture leaks (mount points always cleaned up)
3. Structured logs enable post-mortem debugging
4. Works in CI (no interactive prompts, no sudo)
5. Logs parseable with `jq`
close_reason: done

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: SHARED INFRASTRUCTURE: This E2E framework is used by 6+ downstream beads. Design for reuse: MountHandle RAII, structured JSON logs, artifact collection. Must work in CI (no sudo, no interactive prompts). Consider using temp directories under /tmp/ffs-e2e-* with cleanup guarantees.

title: Add comprehensive E2E test suite for btrfs RW path
description:
# Add comprehensive E2E test suite for btrfs RW path

## GOAL
Create E2E tests for btrfs write path similar to bd-huh.5 for ext4.

## BACKGROUND
The ext4 RW smoke test (bd-huh.5) validates:
- File create/write/read roundtrip
- mkdir/rmdir
- rename
- unlink
- Persistence after unmount/remount

btrfs needs equivalent coverage plus COW-specific validation.

## DELIVERABLES

### 1. Script: scripts/e2e/ffs_btrfs_rw_smoke.sh
```bash
#\!/usr/bin/env bash
# btrfs RW smoke test for FrankenFS
set -euo pipefail
source "$(dirname "$0")/lib.sh"

# Generate fresh btrfs image
mkfs.btrfs -f "$WORKDIR/test.btrfs"

# Mount RW
ffs mount --rw "$WORKDIR/test.btrfs" "$WORKDIR/mnt"

# Test operations...
```

### 2. Test Operations (all required)
1. **File operations**:
   - Create file with echo
   - Write content (small: 10 bytes, medium: 4KB, large: 1MB)
   - Read and verify md5sum
   - Overwrite and verify
   - Truncate (extend and shrink)
   - Append

2. **Directory operations**:
   - mkdir single level
   - mkdir -p nested
   - rmdir (empty)
   - rmdir non-empty fails with correct errno

3. **Name operations**:
   - rename within directory
   - rename across directories
   - rename over existing file (atomic replace)
   - unlink regular file
   - unlink symlink

4. **Link operations**:
   - Create symlink
   - Read symlink (readlink)
   - Create hardlink
   - Verify hardlink shares data

5. **COW-specific tests**:
   - Write same file 10 times -> check generation advances
   - Verify btrfs tree root changed after writes (via btrfs inspect)

6. **Persistence tests**:
   - Unmount cleanly
   - Remount read-only
   - Verify all data intact
   - Verify metadata (size, mode, mtime) preserved

### 3. Logging Specification (in test script)
```bash
# Environment
export RUST_LOG=ffs=trace,fuser=debug
export RUST_BACKTRACE=1

# Per-test logging function
log_test() {
    local name="$1"
    local status="$2"
    local duration="$3"
    echo "[$(date -Iseconds)] TEST: $name STATUS: $status DURATION: ${duration}ms" >> "$LOG_FILE"
}

# Before/after state capture
capture_state() {
    local label="$1"
    echo "=== State: $label ===" >> "$LOG_FILE"
    ls -laR "$WORKDIR/mnt" 2>/dev/null >> "$LOG_FILE" || true
    btrfs inspect-internal dump-tree "$WORKDIR/test.btrfs" 2>/dev/null | head -100 >> "$LOG_FILE" || true
}
```

### 4. Fixture Management
- Generate fresh 256MB btrfs image in temp dir
- Pre-populate with test files if needed
- Clean up on exit (trap ERR and EXIT)
- Log cleanup actions

### 5. CI Integration
- Skip with exit 0 if mkfs.btrfs not available
- Skip if /dev/fuse not available (CI environment)
- Produce JUnit XML report for CI parsing

## FAILURE HANDLING
- On any test failure: capture full state
- Include `dmesg | tail -50` for kernel messages
- Include `journalctl -n 50` if available
- Preserve temp directory for debugging (print path)

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. All test operations pass on clean btrfs image
2. All operations logged with timing and result
3. COW semantics verified (generation increments)
4. Persistence verified across unmount/remount
5. Clean exit code (0 success, 1 failure, 0 skip)
6. Script runs in under 60 seconds
7. No left-behind mounts on exit (trap verified)
close_reason: Hardened btrfs RW E2E suite with rch offload, binary mount path, stronger COW checks, and CI-safe skip/JUnit behavior

title: Implement zero-copy I/O paths where possible
description:
# Implement zero-copy I/O paths where possible

## GOAL
Minimize data copies on I/O paths, applying mechanical sympathy principles.

## BACKGROUND
FUSE fundamentally requires kernel/userspace copies. But we can minimize userspace copies:
1. Direct buffer handoff between cache and FUSE
2. Vectored I/O for multi-block operations
3. Page-aligned allocations for OS optimization

## TECHNICAL REQUIREMENTS

### 1. BlockBuf Refcounting
```rust
pub struct BlockBuf {
    data: Arc<AlignedVec>,  // Shared, aligned buffer
}

impl BlockBuf {
    pub fn as_slice(&self) -> &[u8];
    pub fn clone_ref(&self) -> BlockBuf;  // Arc::clone
    pub fn make_mut(&mut self) -> &mut [u8]; // COW if shared
}
```

### 2. Aligned Allocation
```rust
pub struct AlignedVec {
    ptr: NonNull<u8>,
    len: usize,
    cap: usize,
}

impl AlignedVec {
    pub fn new(size: usize, align: usize) -> Self;
    // Align to page size (4096) for direct I/O compatibility
}
```

### 3. Vectored I/O
```rust
pub trait VectoredBlockDevice: BlockDevice {
    fn read_vectored(
        &self,
        blocks: &[BlockNumber],
        bufs: &mut [BlockBuf],
        cx: &Cx,
    ) -> Result<()>;
    
    fn write_vectored(
        &self,
        blocks: &[BlockNumber],
        bufs: &[BlockBuf],
        cx: &Cx,
    ) -> Result<()>;
}
```

### 4. FUSE Buffer Handoff
- Use fuser reply buffer directly where possible
- Avoid intermediate Vec allocations
- Consider mmap for large reads (if FUSE supports)

## TESTS
1. Unit: BlockBuf refcounting semantics
2. Unit: Aligned allocation alignment
3. Integration: Vectored I/O correctness
4. Benchmark: Copy count measurement

## LOGGING
- Buffer allocation (trace): size, alignment
- Copy detected (trace): source, dest, size
- Vectored I/O (trace): block count

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. No unnecessary copies in hot paths
2. Memory usage not increased
3. Performance improvement measurable
4. Correctness preserved

title: Add ext4 crash-consistency matrix (8 scenarios)
description:
## What
Add 8 ext4 crash-consistency test scenarios mirroring the existing btrfs crash matrix
(FEATURE_PARITY.md lines 93-100, contract IDs e2e::btrfs_rw_crash_matrix_01 through _08).

## Why
btrfs has a comprehensive 8-scenario crash matrix testing create/write/fsync/rename/unlink
boundaries. ext4 has NO equivalent — the ext4 write path lacks crash-consistency coverage.
This is a significant proof gap for the experimental --rw feature.

## What Already Exists
- btrfs crash matrix: 8 unit tests in ffs-core testing crash points through OpenFs methods
- Writeback-cache epoch barrier: 12 crash scenarios unit-tested (different from this — those
  test the epoch state machine, these test actual data recovery)
- ext4 journal replay: replay_jbd2() in ffs-journal (recovery infrastructure exists)
- Crash injection pattern: TestDevice supports clone() for snapshot-before-crash

## Implementation Plan
Add to crates/ffs-core/src/lib.rs test module, following the btrfs pattern:

fn ext4_crash_matrix_01_create_no_fsync()
  - build_writable_ext4() → create file → don't fsync → drop OpenFs (crash)
  - Reopen → verify file absent or present (either valid per ext4 semantics)
  - Emit CRASH_MATRIX_EVENT evidence

fn ext4_crash_matrix_02_write_no_fsync()
  - Create + write data → no fsync → crash → reopen
  - Verify: data NOT guaranteed to persist

fn ext4_crash_matrix_03_write_fsync()
  - Create + write + fsync → crash → reopen
  - Verify: data MUST persist (fsync durability contract)

fn ext4_crash_matrix_04_rename_no_fsync()
  - Create file A → rename A→B → no fsync → crash → reopen
  - Verify: either old name or new name exists (envelope check)

fn ext4_crash_matrix_05_fsync_rename_parent()
  - Rename + fsync parent dir → crash → reopen
  - Verify: new name persists

fn ext4_crash_matrix_06_unlink_no_fsync()
  - Create + unlink → no fsync → crash → reopen
  - Verify: file may still exist (unlink not durable without fsync)

fn ext4_crash_matrix_07_truncate_fsync()
  - Write 8KB → truncate to 4KB → fsync → crash → reopen
  - Verify: file is 4KB

fn ext4_crash_matrix_08_multi_file_interleaved_fsync()
  - Create A, create B, fsync A only → crash → reopen
  - Verify: A persists, B may not

### Crash Injection Strategy
1. Create writable OpenFs with in-memory TestDevice (clone-capable)
2. Perform operations
3. For "crash" — clone device bytes, drop OpenFs without shutdown
4. For "reopen" — create new OpenFs from cloned device bytes
5. Verify recovery invariants on reopened fs

### Evidence Logging
Each test emits structured CRASH_MATRIX_EVENT via evidence ledger:
  { event_type: "crash_matrix", scenario_id: "ext4_crash_01_create_no_fsync",
    crash_point: "before_fsync", outcome: "recovered_correctly" }

## Considerations
- Need build_writable_ext4() helper (may already exist, check for open_writable_ext4)
- ext4 crash recovery involves journal replay — tests should exercise this path
- "Envelope check" pattern: don't assert exact state, assert valid-state-set
  (e.g., after rename without fsync, either old or new name is acceptable)
- Contract IDs should follow pattern: e2e::ext4_rw_crash_matrix_01 through _08
- Update FEATURE_PARITY.md with new ext4 crash-consistency contract rows

## Test Plan
- All 8 tests pass in cargo test -p ffs-core --lib -- ext4_crash_matrix
- Evidence events parseable via parse_json_logs
- Tests deterministic across 3 consecutive runs
- No interference with other tests (no shared state)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] 8 ext4 crash-consistency scenarios implemented and passing
- [ ] Each scenario emits CRASH_MATRIX_EVENT evidence
- [ ] Recovery invariants verified post-crash
- [ ] FEATURE_PARITY.md updated with ext4 crash matrix contract
- [ ] Tests deterministic (3 consecutive runs, 0 flakes)

title: ffs-error: Decide mount-validation error variants (UnsupportedFeature/UnsupportedBlockSize/etc)
description:
Context: mount validation can fail because of unsupported feature bits, block sizes, or incompatible on-disk geometry.

Question: do we represent these as:
- ParseError::InvalidField (and map to FfsError::Format), OR
- dedicated FfsError variants (UnsupportedFeature, UnsupportedBlockSize, InvalidGeometry), OR
- an intermediate MountValidationError used by ffs-core.

Deliverables:
- Chosen approach documented.
- Error mapping yields correct errno (EINVAL vs EOPNOTSUPP) for FUSE and CLI.

Acceptance:
- ext4 validate_v1() failures are surfaced with stable errors and good UX.
- Docs/spec do not list non-existent FfsError variants.

title: EPIC: Parallel WAL — Silo/Aether Style (Graveyard Entry 15.4)
description:
# EPIC: Parallel WAL — Silo/Aether Style (Graveyard Entry 15.4)

## PURPOSE
Scale WAL (Write-Ahead Log) throughput for concurrent MVCC writers by using per-core WAL buffers with epoch-based ordering. Current single WAL is a serialization bottleneck for concurrent transactions.

## BACKGROUND
From the Alien CS Graveyard entry 15.4:
- Silo (Tu et al., SOSP 2013): per-core log buffers, epoch-based commit ordering
- Aether (Johnson et al., VLDB 2010): decoupled log buffer management, early lock release
- Key insight: WAL serialization is unnecessary if epoch ordering provides equivalent guarantees

## DESIGN

### Per-Core WAL Buffers
- Each CPU core has a dedicated WAL buffer (thread-local or core-affinity)
- Transactions write to their core's buffer without contention
- No central lock on WAL append

### Epoch-Based Ordering
- Global epoch counter advanced periodically (e.g., every 10ms or every N commits)
- Each WAL entry stamped with current epoch
- Durability guarantee: all entries in epoch E are durable before epoch E+1 begins
- Recovery: replay all entries in epoch order (entries within same epoch are commutative)

### Group Commit
- All WAL buffers for epoch E flushed together (group commit)
- Single fsync for all cores' buffers at epoch boundary
- Reduces fsync overhead from O(transactions) to O(epochs)

### Recovery
- On crash: find last complete epoch across all per-core logs
- Replay all entries up to last complete epoch
- Discard entries from incomplete epoch (they never committed)

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] Per-core WAL buffers eliminate contention
- [ ] Epoch-based ordering provides equivalent durability guarantees
- [ ] Group commit reduces fsync frequency
- [ ] Recovery correctly handles incomplete epochs
- [ ] Benchmark: WAL throughput scales with core count

## Success Criteria
1. All children closed
2. E2E WAL test (bd-2oah.4) passes
3. Parallel WAL scales linearly to 4+ cores
4. Group commit reduces fsync count by >= 50pct
5. Crash recovery replays all committed transactions

title: Design per-core WAL buffer for concurrent MVCC writes
description:
# Design per-core WAL buffer for concurrent MVCC writes

## GOAL
Design and implement per-core WAL buffers that eliminate contention on the write-ahead log for concurrent MVCC transactions.

## DESIGN

### Buffer Structure
- Thread-local WAL buffer: Vec<WalEntry> per core
- Core affinity: use thread_local! or core_affinity crate
- Buffer capacity: configurable (default 4MB per core)
- When buffer full: flush to disk, reset

### WAL Entry Format
```rust
struct WalEntry {
    epoch: u64,
    txn_id: TxnId,
    entry_type: WalEntryType,
    payload: Vec<u8>,
    crc32c: u32,
}
```

### Concurrency
- No locks on per-core buffers (thread-local access)
- Only contention point: epoch counter increment (atomic CAS)
- Flush can happen concurrently across cores

### Integration
- Replace single WAL in ffs-mvcc with per-core buffer pool
- TxnManager routes writes to current core's buffer
- Epoch manager coordinates global epoch advancement

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] Per-core buffers avoid contention (no mutex on hot path)
- [ ] Buffer management handles full/flush correctly
- [ ] Core affinity works on Linux (sched_setaffinity or equivalent)
- [ ] Unit tests: concurrent writes to separate buffers
- [ ] Benchmark: throughput vs single WAL baseline

title: Implement epoch-based WAL ordering
description:
# Implement epoch-based WAL ordering

## GOAL
Implement the epoch mechanism that provides ordering guarantees across per-core WAL buffers without requiring a global serialization point.

## DESIGN

### Epoch Manager
```rust
struct EpochManager {
    current_epoch: AtomicU64,
    epoch_interval: Duration,     // e.g., 10ms
    commit_threshold: u64,        // advance after N commits
}
```

### Epoch Advancement
- Advance epoch when: interval expires OR commit_threshold reached (whichever first)
- On advance: atomically increment current_epoch
- All new WAL entries stamped with new epoch
- Old epoch entries become eligible for group commit

### Durability Contract
- Transaction T in epoch E is durable after epoch E is fully flushed
- Transaction commit returns only after its epoch is flushed
- Callers waiting for durability block on epoch flush (not individual entry flush)

### Recovery Ordering
- Epochs are globally ordered
- Within an epoch, entries are commutative (no ordering required)
- Recovery: for each epoch in order, apply all entries

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] Epoch advances correctly (time-based and count-based triggers)
- [ ] WAL entries correctly stamped with epoch
- [ ] Durability contract holds: committed entries survive crash
- [ ] Recovery respects epoch ordering
- [ ] Unit tests: epoch advance timing, entry stamping

title: Implement group commit for parallel WAL
description:
# Implement group commit for parallel WAL

## GOAL
Implement group commit that batches fsync calls across all per-core WAL buffers at epoch boundaries, reducing I/O overhead from O(transactions) to O(epochs).

## DESIGN

### Group Commit Protocol
1. Epoch E completes (all transactions in E have written their WAL entries)
2. Group commit coordinator collects all per-core buffers for epoch E
3. Write all buffers to disk (sequential or parallel I/O)
4. Single fsync() call covers all writes
5. Notify all waiting transactions that epoch E is durable
6. Release epoch E buffers for reuse

### Waiting Transactions
- Transactions that committed in epoch E call await_durability(E)
- Block on condvar/futex until group commit for E completes
- Amortized wait: all transactions in same epoch wake together

### Error Handling
- If fsync fails: all transactions in epoch E marked as failed
- Retry logic: configurable retry count before giving up
- Evidence record: log group commit success/failure

### Performance
- Target: 1 fsync per epoch (not per transaction)
- Epoch interval tuning: shorter = lower latency, longer = higher throughput
- Benchmark: compare fsync count vs single-WAL approach

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] Group commit batches all per-core buffers into single fsync
- [ ] Waiting transactions correctly notified on epoch flush
- [ ] fsync failure correctly propagated to all transactions in epoch
- [ ] Benchmark: fsync count reduction vs single WAL
- [ ] Unit tests: group commit with multiple concurrent writers

title: E2E test: parallel WAL throughput scaling and crash recovery
description:
# E2E test: parallel WAL throughput scaling and crash recovery

## GOAL
Prove that the parallel WAL (Silo/Aether-style per-core buffers with epoch-based ordering) correctly scales write throughput with core count and recovers correctly from crashes at any point in the epoch lifecycle.

## TEST SCENARIOS

### Scenario 1: Throughput Scaling
1. Run write workload with 1, 2, 4, 8 threads
2. Each thread: 10K transactions, each writing 4 WAL entries
3. Measure: WAL entries/sec, fsync count, epoch count
4. Verify: throughput increases with thread count (near-linear ideal)
5. Verify: fsync count = epoch count (group commit working)
6. Compare against single-WAL baseline

### Scenario 2: Epoch-Boundary Crash Recovery
1. 4 threads writing concurrently
2. Crash at epoch boundary (between epoch E flush and epoch E+1 start)
3. Recover: replay log
4. Verify: all transactions in epochs <= E are committed
5. Verify: no transactions from epoch E+1 visible (incomplete epoch discarded)

### Scenario 3: Mid-Epoch Crash Recovery
1. 4 threads writing concurrently
2. Crash mid-epoch (some per-core buffers partially flushed)
3. Recover: find last complete epoch across all cores
4. Verify: only complete epochs replayed, partial epoch discarded
5. Verify: no partial transactions visible

### Scenario 4: Group Commit Correctness
1. 8 threads, each committing 1 transaction per epoch
2. Verify: all 8 transactions in same epoch share one fsync
3. Verify: await_durability returns only after group fsync complete
4. Verify: no transaction sees committed data before its epoch is durable

### Scenario 5: Epoch Ordering Under High Contention
1. 16 threads, rapid transaction commits (saturate epoch capacity)
2. Verify: epoch ordering is strict (no epoch E+1 entries before epoch E fully flushed)
3. Verify: cross-epoch transactions correctly ordered
4. Verify: recovery produces state equivalent to some serial execution

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] Throughput scales >= 3x from 1 to 8 threads
- [ ] fsync count per second is O(epochs), not O(transactions)
- [ ] Epoch-boundary crash: complete epochs recovered, partial discarded
- [ ] Mid-epoch crash: same recovery guarantee
- [ ] No data corruption under any crash scenario
- [ ] All scenarios deterministic and CI-executable
- [ ] Logging: tracing spans for epoch advances, group commits, recovery actions

description:
# Add unit tests for parallel WAL buffer and epoch ordering

## GOAL
Provide unit test coverage for the Parallel WAL epic (bd-2oah). Currently has E2E test (bd-2oah.4) but NO unit tests for per-core WAL buffer, epoch ordering, or group commit logic.

## TEST PLAN

### Per-Core Buffer Tests
1. Each core gets independent WAL buffer
2. Buffer append is lock-free within same core
3. Buffer flush serializes entries correctly
4. Buffer overflow triggers early flush

### Epoch Ordering Tests
5. Entries within same epoch maintain order
6. Cross-core entries ordered by epoch, then core-local sequence
7. Epoch boundary is consistent across all cores
8. Recovery replays entries in epoch order

### Group Commit Tests
9. Multiple transactions in same group commit
10. Group commit waits for all participants
11. Timeout triggers partial group commit
12. Failed transaction excluded from group commit

## TESTS (REQUIRED)
All 12 tests above. Deterministic, no disk I/O (mock device).

## LOGGING REQUIREMENTS
- Test failures log WAL buffer state, epoch counters

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. All 12 tests pass
2. Epoch ordering invariant verified under concurrent load
3. No data loss in group commit edge cases

title: LRC for distributed mode repair (Graveyard Entry 1.4)
description:
# LRC for distributed mode repair (Graveyard Entry 1.4)

## GOAL
Implement Local Reconstruction Codes (LRC) as an alternative to RaptorQ for distributed/multi-node FrankenFS deployments where repair locality matters.

## BACKGROUND
From Alien CS Graveyard 1.4: LRC (Azure LRC, Huang et al. 2012) provides local repair groups within the global erasure code. When a block fails, repair requires only reading from the local group (3-5 blocks) rather than the full erasure group (14+ blocks). This reduces repair I/O by 5-10x for single-block failures.

## DESIGN
- Local groups: partition each block group into 3-4 local repair groups
- Local parity: 1 local parity block per local group
- Global parity: additional global parity blocks for multi-failure
- Repair: single failure reads only local group; multi-failure falls back to global

## APPLICABILITY
- Future feature: only relevant when FrankenFS supports distributed/replicated storage
- Current RaptorQ is superior for single-node (fountain codes handle arbitrary corruption patterns)
- LRC adds value when I/O cost of repair matters (networked storage, slow disks)

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] LRC codec implemented (local + global parity)
- [ ] Single-failure repair reads only local group
- [ ] Multi-failure falls back to global reconstruction
- [ ] Benchmark: repair I/O compared to RaptorQ for single vs multi failure
acceptance_criteria: LRC codec implemented with local + global parity generation. Single-failure repair reads only local group (3-5 blocks, not full 14+ block erasure group). Multi-failure falls back to global reconstruction correctly. Local group boundaries align with block group structure. Evidence records logged for all local vs global repair decisions. Benchmark: repair I/O bytes read for single-failure LRC vs RaptorQ. Unit tests: local parity encode/decode roundtrip. Unit tests: global parity encode/decode roundtrip. Unit tests: single-failure local repair correctness. Unit tests: multi-failure global repair correctness. Property test: any single-block failure repaired by local group alone.

title: Implement adaptive backpressure system with budget propagation
description:
# Implement adaptive backpressure system with budget propagation

## GOAL
Implement Cx-based budget system that propagates backpressure through the stack, preventing cascade failures under load.

## BACKGROUND (Alien-Artifact Quality)
Traditional systems fail abruptly under load. FrankenFS must degrade gracefully using information-theoretic principles:

1. **Budget = scarce resource allocation**
   - deadline: wall-clock timeout
   - poll_quota: async poll iterations allowed
   - cost_quota: abstract "work units" allowed
   - priority: relative importance

2. **Budget combination**: min for limits, max for priority

3. **Backpressure = signal to reduce demand**
   When budget exhausted, callers must either:
   - Retry with fresh budget (if transient)
   - Propagate failure (if permanent)
   - Shed load (if graceful degradation enabled)

## TECHNICAL REQUIREMENTS

### 1. Budget Types
```rust
pub struct Budget {
    pub deadline: Option<Instant>,
    pub poll_quota: Option<u64>,
    pub cost_quota: Option<u64>,
    pub priority: Priority,
}

impl Budget {
    pub fn combine(&self, other: &Budget) -> Budget {
        Budget {
            deadline: min_option(self.deadline, other.deadline),
            poll_quota: min_option(self.poll_quota, other.poll_quota),
            cost_quota: min_option(self.cost_quota, other.cost_quota),
            priority: max(self.priority, other.priority),
        }
    }

    pub fn is_exhausted(&self) -> bool {
        self.deadline.map_or(false, |d| Instant::now() > d)
            || self.poll_quota == Some(0)
            || self.cost_quota == Some(0)
    }
}
```

### 2. Integration Points
- **FUSE dispatch**: Per-request budget from config
- **ARC cache**: Eviction budget (prevent eviction storms)
- **MVCC commit**: Transaction budget
- **Scrub/GC**: Background budget (yields to foreground)
- **RaptorQ decode**: Symbol budget

### 3. Pressure Detection
```rust
pub struct PressureMonitor {
    pub fn sample_cpu_load() -> f64;
    pub fn sample_memory_pressure() -> f64;
    pub fn sample_io_depth() -> usize;
    pub fn compute_degradation_level() -> DegradationLevel;
}
```

### 4. Graceful Degradation FSM
```
Normal -> Pause Background -> Reduce Cache -> Throttle Writes -> Read-Only
   ^                                                              |
   +--------------------(pressure relieved)----------------------+
```

## TESTS
1. Unit: Budget combination semantics
2. Unit: Pressure detection thresholds
3. Integration: Simulated CPU load triggers degradation
4. Integration: Recovery when pressure relieved

## LOGGING
- Budget exhaustion (warn)
- Degradation level change (info)
- Pressure samples (trace)

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. Operations respect budget limits
2. Degradation level reflects actual pressure
3. Recovery is automatic and stable
4. No infinite loops or deadlocks under pressure

title: EPIC: Production FUSE Runtime
description:
# EPIC: Production FUSE Runtime

## PURPOSE
Transform the FUSE interface from scaffold to production-ready mount runtime, as required by FEATURE_PARITY.md blocking gap #3.

## BACKGROUND
From spec §14 (FUSE Integration):
- Multi-threaded dispatch required
- All state must be Send + Sync
- Never block FUSE dispatch thread on raw I/O
- Proper lifecycle management (mount/unmount/signal handling)

Current state: "FUSE mount runtime: ❌ Interface scaffold only"

## CRITICAL REQUIREMENTS

### 1. Mount Lifecycle
- Clean mount with explicit options (ro/rw, cache_size, etc.)
- Graceful unmount with flush and cleanup
- Signal handling (SIGTERM, SIGINT -> clean unmount)
- Crash recovery on restart

### 2. Multi-threaded Dispatch
- Use fuser multi-threaded mode
- Thread pool sizing based on CPU count
- Per-thread ARC cache access (Send + Sync)
- Connection pooling for concurrent requests

### 3. Error Handling
- Every FUSE op maps to correct errno
- Timeout handling for hung operations
- Partial failure recovery

### 4. Performance
- Request batching where applicable
- Readahead for sequential access
- Writeback coalescing
- Attribute caching with configurable TTL

### 5. Observability
- Per-op latency metrics
- Request queue depth
- Error rate tracking

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. `ffs mount --rw /path/to/image.ext4 /mnt/ffs` works in production
2. Concurrent readers/writers do not deadlock
3. `umount /mnt/ffs` is clean (no data loss)
4. SIGTERM results in clean shutdown
5. All FUSE ops have correct errno mapping

## FILES
- crates/ffs-fuse/src/lib.rs (main implementation)
- crates/ffs-fuse/src/mount.rs (lifecycle)
- crates/ffs-fuse/src/dispatch.rs (multi-thread)
- crates/ffs-cli/src/main.rs (mount command)

## Success Criteria
1. All children closed
2. E2E suite (bd-3en1) passes
3. ffs mount --rw works on ext4 and btrfs images
4. SIGTERM unmount is clean (no data loss)
5. All FUSE ops have correct errno mapping

title: Implement FUSE write operations dispatch (create/mkdir/write/unlink/rename)
description:
# Implement FUSE write operations dispatch

## GOAL
Add write operation handlers to FrankenFuse so that mounted filesystems support mutation operations.

## BACKGROUND
Currently ffs-fuse only handles read operations (getattr, lookup, readdir, read, readlink). Write operations need:
1. FUSE protocol handlers in FrankenFuse
2. Delegation to FsOps trait methods
3. Transaction wrapping (begin → operation → commit)
4. Error mapping for write-specific errors (ENOSPC, EROFS, EEXIST, etc.)

## DELIVERABLES
### Phase 1: File creation and writing
1. `create(parent, name, mode, umask, flags)` → create file + open
2. `write(ino, fh, offset, data, flags)` → write data to file
3. `setattr(ino, mode, uid, gid, size, atime, mtime)` → chmod/chown/truncate

### Phase 2: Directory operations  
4. `mkdir(parent, name, mode, umask)` → create directory
5. `unlink(parent, name)` → remove file
6. `rmdir(parent, name)` → remove directory
7. `rename(parent, name, newparent, newname, flags)` → move/rename

### Phase 3: Link operations
8. `link(ino, newparent, newname)` → hard link
9. `symlink(parent, name, link)` → symbolic link
10. `mknod(parent, name, mode, rdev)` → device/fifo/socket

### Each handler must:
- Check read-only mode → EROFS
- Begin MVCC transaction
- Delegate to FsOps implementation
- Commit or abort transaction
- Map errors to errno
- Log operation at appropriate level

## TESTS (REQUIRED)
1. Unit: create + write + read roundtrip
2. Unit: mkdir + create file inside + readdir
3. Unit: unlink reduces link count
4. Unit: rmdir on non-empty → ENOTEMPTY
5. Unit: rename across directories
6. Unit: EROFS on read-only mount
7. Unit: ENOSPC when disk full (simulated)
8. Integration: 100 concurrent file creates
9. Property: Random create/delete sequences maintain consistency

## LOGGING REQUIREMENTS
### Operation-level logging (all at debug unless error)
- create (debug): parent_ino, name, mode, result_ino
- write (trace): ino, offset, len, written_bytes (not data content)
- setattr (debug): ino, changed_fields (mode/size/time), old_values, new_values
- mkdir (debug): parent_ino, name, mode, result_ino
- unlink (debug): parent_ino, name, target_ino, remaining_links
- rmdir (debug): parent_ino, name, target_ino
- rename (debug): old_parent, old_name, new_parent, new_name, flags

### Transaction logging
- txn_begin (trace): txn_id, operation, inode(s)
- txn_commit (trace): txn_id, commit_seq, duration_us
- txn_abort (debug): txn_id, reason

### Error logging
- write_error (warn): operation, ino, errno, error_context
- permission_denied (info): operation, ino, uid, required_mode
- disk_full (warn): operation, requested_blocks, available_blocks

### Performance logging
- slow_operation (info): operation, ino, duration_ms (if > 100ms)

## POSIX COMPLIANCE NOTES
- create: EEXIST if exists and O_EXCL, ENOSPC if disk full
- mkdir: EEXIST, EACCES for permission
- unlink: EISDIR (use rmdir instead), ENOENT
- rmdir: ENOTEMPTY, ENOTDIR
- rename: atomic replace semantics (POSIX.1-2017)

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. All 10 FUSE write operations implemented
2. Transaction wrapping correct (commit on success, abort on error)
3. Read-only mount correctly rejects writes with EROFS
4. Errno mapping matches POSIX expectations
5. All operations logged with sufficient context for debugging

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: POSIX COMPLIANCE: Each write operation must handle specific error cases: create → EEXIST if exists, ENOSPC if disk full; mkdir → EEXIST, EACCES; unlink → EISDIR (use rmdir instead), ENOENT; rmdir → ENOTEMPTY, ENOTDIR; rename → atomic replace semantics. Reference POSIX.1-2017 for exact behavior.

# Implement FUSE setxattr and removexattr operations

## GOAL
Enable writing and removing extended attributes through the FUSE mount interface.

## BACKGROUND
listxattr and getxattr are already implemented (read-only). This bead adds the write side:
- setxattr: Create or replace an extended attribute
- removexattr: Delete an extended attribute

Requires inline xattr space management (may need to promote to external block if inline space exhausted).

## DELIVERABLES
1. FrankenFuse::setxattr() handler
2. FrankenFuse::removexattr() handler
3. Inline xattr space management (check if fits, promote if needed)
4. Integration with MVCC transaction wrapping

## TESTS
1. Set xattr on file, read it back
2. Replace existing xattr with larger value
3. Remove xattr, verify gone
4. Overflow inline space → external block (or ENOSPC if not supported)
5. EROFS on read-only mount

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. POSIX xattr semantics preserved
2. Transaction-wrapped for crash safety
3. No data corruption on inline/external boundary

title: Implement crash recovery on mount after unclean shutdown
description:
# Implement crash recovery on mount after unclean shutdown

## GOAL
When FrankenFS mounts a filesystem image that was not cleanly unmounted (crash, SIGKILL, power loss), it must detect the unclean state and perform recovery before serving any requests.

## BACKGROUND
Unclean shutdown can leave:
- Uncommitted MVCC transactions (partial writes in version chains)
- Stale RaptorQ symbols (dirty groups not refreshed)
- Incomplete journal transactions (JBD2 write path)
- Dirty inode/block caches not flushed to disk

Without crash recovery, mounting after a crash could expose partially-written data, stale versions, or inconsistent metadata.

## DESIGN

### Unclean Shutdown Detection
- Superblock dirty flag: set on mount, cleared on clean unmount
- If dirty flag is set on mount: unclean shutdown detected
- Journal recovery: check for uncommitted journal transactions

### Recovery Steps (ordered)
1. Journal replay: apply committed but unflushed journal transactions
2. MVCC rollback: abort any in-flight transactions (no commit record = abort)
3. Version chain cleanup: remove uncommitted versions from chains
4. RaptorQ symbol validation: mark all groups as needing re-verification
5. Trigger immediate scrub of metadata-critical groups (superblock, GDT, inode tables)
6. Clear superblock dirty flag after recovery completes
7. Log all recovery actions to evidence ledger

### Integration Points
- ffs-cli mount command: detect unclean state before serving FUSE
- Journal replay (bd-1xe.4): already handles ext4 journal replay
- MVCC subsystem: needs abort/rollback API for in-flight transactions
- Scrub daemon: needs priority queue for post-recovery metadata verification

### Error Handling
- If recovery fails (journal corrupt, metadata unreadable): refuse to mount, log detailed error
- If recovery partial (some transactions recovered, others not): mount read-only, log warning
- Never silently ignore unclean state

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] Unclean shutdown detected via superblock dirty flag
- [ ] Journal transactions replayed correctly on recovery mount
- [ ] In-flight MVCC transactions aborted cleanly
- [ ] RaptorQ symbols re-verified after crash recovery
- [ ] Evidence ledger records all recovery actions
- [ ] Mount refuses if recovery fails completely
- [ ] Read-only fallback if recovery is partial
- [ ] Unit tests: mock unclean state, verify recovery steps
close_reason: done

title: E2E test: mount, write 10000 files, unmount, remount, verify all files
description:
# E2E test: mount, write 10000 files, unmount, remount, verify all files

## GOAL
Verify that FrankenFS correctly persists data across mount/unmount cycles. This is the fundamental persistence correctness test.

## TEST SCENARIO

### Phase 1: Write
1. Create fresh 512MB ext4 image with FrankenFS
2. Mount via FUSE (read-write mode)
3. Create directory tree: 100 directories, 100 files each = 10000 files
4. Each file: deterministic content based on path (e.g., BLAKE3(path) repeated to fill 4KB)
5. Compute BLAKE3 checksum manifest for all 10000 files
6. Unmount cleanly

### Phase 2: Verify
7. Remount the same image (read-only mode)
8. Walk entire directory tree, verify all 10000 files present
9. Read each file, verify BLAKE3 checksum matches manifest
10. Verify directory structure (ls each directory, check file count)
11. Unmount

### Phase 3: Read-Write Cycle
12. Remount read-write
13. Modify 1000 random files (new deterministic content)
14. Delete 500 random files
15. Create 500 new files
16. Update manifest
17. Unmount, remount, verify updated manifest

### Timing Requirements
- Phase 1: < 30s for 10000 files on SSD
- Phase 2: < 15s for verification
- Phase 3: < 20s for modifications + verification

### Failure Modes to Detect
- File content corruption (checksum mismatch)
- Missing files (not persisted to disk)
- Extra files (stale data from previous test)
- Directory entry corruption (wrong file count)
- Permission/ownership corruption

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] All 10000 files survive mount/unmount cycle with correct content
- [ ] Directory structure exactly matches expected layout
- [ ] Read-write-modify cycle preserves all changes
- [ ] Test is deterministic (same seed = same result)
- [ ] Test completes in < 120s total
- [ ] Test passes in CI without root (FUSE with allow_other or user mount)
close_reason: done

title: E2E test: SIGKILL during write, remount, verify consistency
description:
# E2E test: SIGKILL during write, remount, verify consistency

## GOAL
Verify that FrankenFS maintains consistency guarantees after an unclean shutdown (SIGKILL during active writes). The filesystem must be mountable, crash recovery must work, and all committed data must be intact.

## TEST SCENARIO

### Phase 1: Setup
1. Create fresh 256MB ext4 image
2. Mount via FUSE, write baseline dataset (500 files, checksums recorded)
3. fsync all files, verify baseline is durable
4. Begin continuous write workload (background thread writing new files)

### Phase 2: Crash
5. After 2 seconds of active writing, send SIGKILL to FUSE process
6. Record which files had fsync called vs which were in-flight

### Phase 3: Recovery
7. Remount the filesystem image
8. Crash recovery should trigger automatically (journal replay, MVCC rollback)
9. Record evidence ledger output

### Phase 4: Verification
10. All baseline files (pre-crash) must be intact with correct checksums
11. Files that had fsync completed must be intact
12. Files that were in-flight at SIGKILL time: acceptable to be missing or truncated, but:
    - Must NOT contain garbage data
    - Must NOT corrupt other files
    - Directory must be consistent (no dangling entries)
13. No filesystem-level corruption (fsck-equivalent check passes)

### Invariants
- Atomicity: a file write either fully committed or fully absent
- No cross-file corruption: crash during file A write must never affect file B
- Metadata consistency: directory entries match existing inodes
- Journal consistency: no partial journal transactions visible

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] Filesystem mounts after SIGKILL without errors
- [ ] All pre-crash baseline files intact with correct checksums
- [ ] All fsync'd files intact
- [ ] In-flight files are either complete or cleanly absent (no partial garbage)
- [ ] No cross-file corruption
- [ ] Evidence ledger records crash recovery actions
- [ ] Test is reproducible (deterministic write pattern)
- [ ] Test completes in < 60s
close_reason: Completed: SIGKILL E2E now passes with deterministic crash invariants

title: Add comprehensive unit tests for FUSE dispatch and lifecycle
description:
# Add comprehensive unit tests for FUSE dispatch and lifecycle

## GOAL
Provide unit test coverage for the Production FUSE Runtime epic (bd-2s4). Currently the epic has E2E tests (bd-2s4.4, bd-2s4.5, bd-3en1) but NO dedicated unit tests for the dispatch layer, lifecycle management, or errno mapping.

## TEST PLAN

### 1. Dispatch Tests
- Thread pool spawns correct number of threads
- Concurrent requests are dispatched without deadlock
- Request timeout fires and returns EIO
- Queue depth metrics are updated correctly

### 2. Lifecycle Tests
- Mount initializes all subsystems in correct order
- Unmount flushes dirty data before teardown
- SIGTERM triggers clean unmount sequence
- Double-unmount is idempotent (no panic)
- Mount with invalid image returns descriptive error

### 3. Errno Mapping Tests
- Every FfsError variant maps to correct errno
- Unknown errors map to EIO
- Cancelled maps to EINTR
- CorruptData maps to EIO
- NoSpace maps to ENOSPC
- PermissionDenied maps to EACCES

### 4. State Tests
- FsOps state is Send + Sync (compile-time test)
- Concurrent getattr calls do not race
- Cache access under lock is bounded (no starvation)

## TESTS (REQUIRED)
1. All tests listed above
2. Total: ~20 unit tests
3. All deterministic, no I/O (mock BlockDevice)

## LOGGING REQUIREMENTS
- Test failures log full context (operation, expected, actual)

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. All dispatch, lifecycle, errno, and state tests pass
2. No flaky tests (deterministic)
3. Coverage of all FUSE op errno mappings
close_reason: done

title: Fix ext4 RW write persistence across clean unmount/remount
description:
## Summary
In privileged E2E runs, ext4 RW operations succeed, but data written in phase 3 is missing after clean unmount + RO remount.

## Repro
1. Build `ffs-cli` (or use prebuilt binary).
2. Run:
   `sudo -n env PATH="$PATH" FFS_SKIP_BUILD=1 FFS_CLI_BIN=/data/projects/frankenfs/target/release/ffs-cli FFS_AUTO_UNMOUNT=0 ./scripts/e2e/ffs_ext4_rw_smoke.sh`
3. Observe failure in phase 4: `persist.txt` is missing on RO remount.

## Evidence
- Run artifact: `artifacts/e2e/20260218_040650_ffs_ext4_rw_smoke/run.log`
- RO mount log: `artifacts/e2e/20260218_040650_ffs_ext4_rw_smoke/mount_ro_mnt_ro.log`

## Expected
Writes acknowledged in RW mount (and fsync-backed sentinel writes) remain visible after clean unmount/remount.

## Actual
`persist.txt` is readable in RW mount, but absent after remount.

## Notes
- Prior RW EIO mount failure appears resolved in this environment.
- Unmount robustness was improved in `scripts/e2e/lib.sh`, so this now consistently surfaces a persistence/durability issue rather than mount teardown noise.
close_reason: Completed: fixed RW persistence loss by using Apply replay mode for RW mount

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text:
Resolved root cause in CLI mount path: `mount_cmd` was always opening ext4 with `Ext4JournalReplayMode::SimulateOverlay`, which made RW writes session-local and non-persistent.

Change:
- `crates/ffs-cli/src/main.rs`: added `ext4_mount_replay_mode(read_write)` and switched RW mount to `Apply` while keeping RO mount on `SimulateOverlay`.
- Added unit test `ext4_mount_replay_mode_is_persistent_for_rw`.

Validation:
- `rch exec -- cargo check -p ffs-cli --all-targets` ✅
- `rch exec -- cargo clippy -p ffs-cli --all-targets -- -D warnings` ✅
- `rch exec -- cargo test -p ffs-cli --all-targets` ✅
- Privileged E2E run: `sudo -n env PATH="$PATH" FFS_SKIP_BUILD=1 FFS_CLI_BIN=/data/projects/frankenfs/target/release/ffs-cli FFS_AUTO_UNMOUNT=0 ./scripts/e2e/ffs_ext4_rw_smoke.sh`
  - Phase 4 now passes: `persist.txt` and appended `mtime-check` survive clean unmount + RO remount.
  - Artifact: `artifacts/e2e/20260218_041747_ffs_ext4_rw_smoke/run.log`.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: REVIEW FIX: Added blocks deps on bd-huh.2 (block allocation) and bd-1xe.4 (journal replay write-back). Orphan recovery is a mutation operation that frees blocks and deletes inodes. It must happen AFTER journal replay (to operate on committed state) and requires the allocator (to reclaim blocks). The mount order is: (1) journal replay write-back, (2) orphan list reading, (3) orphan recovery.
