title: Restore btrfs multi-device RAID corpus and degraded-mode proof
description:
Fresh review follow-up from the closed bd-nzv3.24 lane. That historical bead was closed with the note that the multi-image layout corpus was deferred and single-device tests covered the read path. That is not enough for users if docs, parity reports, proof bundles, or future agents infer multi-device readiness from the closed status.

Scope: restore the missing multi-device corpus and degraded-mode evidence as explicit work. The target is a reproducible btrfs multi-image proof surface for healthy profile assembly, degraded or missing-device handling, unsupported-profile refusal, deterministic device ordering, chunk/stripe mapping visibility, repair/scrub interaction boundaries, and operator remediation. The goal is not to overclaim full btrfs RAID parity; it is to make the supported and unsupported envelope measurable and safe.

Required proof before close:
1. Unit/schema tests validate a multi-device scenario manifest with profile, device count, device ids, device order, image hashes, chunk layout, stripe mapping, missing/degraded state, expected read/write boundary, repair/scrub boundary, unsupported-profile classification, cleanup policy, and artifact requirements.
2. Unit tests cover healthy profile assembly, device-order permutations, missing device, duplicate device id, stale superblock/device metadata, unsupported RAID/profile, degraded read where declared supported, write refusal where unsupported, and remediation text generation.
3. E2E or harness smoke builds or consumes at least one reproducible healthy multi-image fixture, one missing-device fixture, and one unsupported-profile fixture; it emits pass/fail/skip/error artifacts without requiring destructive host operations by default.
4. Logs include scenario id, profile, device ids, image hashes, chunk/stripe mapping summary, mount or parser mode, degraded-state decision, expected outcome, observed outcome, remediation id, artifact paths, cleanup status, and reproduction command.
5. README, FEATURE_PARITY, release gates, and proof bundles cannot claim multi-device support from single-device tests alone; missing or deferred multi-image evidence must downgrade the claim or link this bead as the owning gap.

title: Expand ext4 extent block checksum tests beyond round-trip + no-panic
description:
crates/ffs-ondisk/src/ext4.rs has stamp/verify_extent_block_checksum, but the proptest coverage in the same file mirrors the gap that bd-anfg0 just closed for dir blocks:

  - ext4_proptest_verify_extent_block_checksum_no_panic (negative)
  - ext4_proptest_verify_extent_block_checksum_short_rejects (negative)
  - ext4_proptest_extent_block_checksum_stamp_verify (round-trip)

Apply the same four-relation MR template (MR-INO, MR-GEN, MR-SEED, MR-DETECT) so that extent block stamping is pinned at the same behavioural-contract level as inode (bd-z75f6) and dir block (bd-anfg0). The covered region for an extent block is `[0..tail_off]` where `tail_off = 12 + 12 * eh_max`; flips outside that range (in the 4 stored-checksum bytes) must be excluded from MR-DETECT.

title: Harden block alignment normalization against overflow
description: AlignedVec alignment normalization rounds non-power-of-two requests with usize::next_power_of_two(). Extreme requested alignments above the largest representable power of two can overflow/panic before the block layer can handle the request. Clamp to the largest representable power-of-two alignment and add a regression test that covers oversized requests without allocating.
close_reason: Completed: block alignment normalization now uses checked rounding and clamps oversized requests to the largest representable power-of-two alignment, with regression coverage.

title: Add GitHub Actions CI workflow for fmt/check/clippy/test gates
description:
## What
Create .github/workflows/ci.yml that runs all quality gates on every push to main and on PRs.

## Why
Currently all gates (cargo fmt --check, cargo check --workspace --all-targets, cargo clippy
--workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings, cargo test --workspace) run manually. Regressions
can be committed undetected. CI automation is the single highest-leverage improvement.

## Implementation Plan

### Step 1: Create .github/workflows/ci.yml
- Trigger: push to main, pull_request to main
- OS: ubuntu-latest (FUSE-dependent; no macOS/Windows)
- Rust: install via dtolnay/rust-toolchain@nightly (per rust-toolchain.toml)
- System deps: apt-get install libfuse3-dev fuse3 (for fuser crate)
- Cache: actions/cache for ~/.cargo/registry, ~/.cargo/git, target/

### Step 2: Define CI jobs
Job 1 — fmt-check:
  cargo fmt --check

Job 2 — check-clippy:
  cargo check --workspace --all-targets
  cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings

Job 3 — test:
  cargo test --workspace
  cargo test --workspace --all-features (separate step, catches feature-gated issues)

Job 4 — test-e2e (optional, needs privileged):
  Only if /dev/fuse available
  cargo test -p ffs-harness --test fuse_e2e -- --test-threads=1

### Step 3: Add CI status badge to README.md
Replace or add alongside existing badges:
  [![CI](https://github.com/Dicklesworthstone/frankenfs/actions/workflows/ci.yml/badge.svg)](...)

### Step 4: Verify CI works
- Create a test branch, push it, verify Actions run
- Intentionally break something (add a clippy warning), verify CI catches it
- Fix and verify green

## Considerations
- rust-toolchain.toml pins nightly — CI must respect this (dtolnay/rust-toolchain reads it)
- E2E FUSE tests skip gracefully when /dev/fuse unavailable (tested: fuse_e2e.rs line 225)
- Build time estimate: ~2min check+clippy, ~1min tests (from rch timings)
- asupersync has no special CI requirements (pure Rust, no system deps beyond FUSE)
- Cargo.lock should be committed for reproducible CI builds
- Consider: actions/cache key should include rust-toolchain.toml hash for cache invalidation

## Test Plan
- Push to a test branch and verify all 4 jobs run
- Verify FUSE E2E tests either run (if privileged) or skip (if not) without failing
- Verify cache hits on second push reduce build time
- Verify intentional failure (e.g., add unused variable) is caught by clippy job

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] .github/workflows/ci.yml exists and is valid YAML
- [ ] CI runs on every push to main and on PRs
- [ ] All 4 gates (fmt, check, clippy, test) are separate jobs
- [ ] README badge shows CI status
- [ ] E2E tests skip gracefully when FUSE unavailable
- [ ] Cache configured for cargo artifacts
- [ ] Verified: CI catches intentional failures
close_reason: Already implemented

title: add mounted-path E2E and ioctl-trace coverage for ext4 EXT4_IOC_GETVERSION
description:
Survey finding: `FsOps::get_inode_version` exists in `crates/ffs-core/src/vfs.rs`, `ffs-fuse` dispatches `EXT4_IOC_GETVERSION`, and `crates/ffs-fuse/src/lib.rs` has direct dispatch tests, but `crates/ffs-harness/tests/fuse_e2e.rs` has no mounted-path proof for the ioctl.

Scope:
- Add a mounted-path helper in `fuse_e2e.rs` that issues `EXT4_IOC_GETVERSION` against a real ext4 file over FUSE.
- Assert the ioctl-trace log records the command when the kernel forwards it, and soft-skip only when the kernel/VFS rejects before userspace dispatch.
- Verify stable value encoding and buffer-size/errno behavior against the mounted path, not only the unit-dispatch shim.
- Update `FEATURE_PARITY.md` if operator-visible coverage now exists.

Acceptance:
- At least one mounted-path `EXT4_IOC_GETVERSION` scenario exists with deterministic assertions.
- CI artifacts make it clear whether failures are transport-layer skips or FrankenFS regressions.
close_reason: done

title: Refine xfstests evidence with destructive-operation sandbox and command-plan proofs
description:
Supplemental refinement for bd-rchk3.2.1. xfstests is valuable precisely because it performs realistic filesystem operations, but that also makes the lane dangerous if command plans, scratch paths, and mountpoints are not sandboxed. This bead makes the supported subset safer and more auditable before any real execution claims are made.

Scope: require a command-plan proof for selected xfstests rows. The plan should declare every path, image, mountpoint, helper binary, scratch/test device placeholder, required privilege, expected mutation surface, cleanup action, and whether the command is dry-run-only, fixture-only, or permissioned-real. Default developer validation must not perform destructive host operations.

Required proof before close:
1. Unit/schema tests reject xfstests command plans with non-temporary scratch paths, missing image hash, missing mountpoint, unresolved helper binary, missing cleanup action, broad shell command strings, unknown privilege requirement, or destructive action without permissioned lane marker.
2. Unit tests cover dry-run-only, fixture-only, permissioned-real, host-skip, unsupported-by-scope, product-failure, harness-failure, and cleanup-failure classifications.
3. E2E/dry-run smoke expands representative ext4 and btrfs command plans without mutating host filesystems, verifies all paths are under controlled temp roots, and emits JSON plus human-readable command summaries.
4. Logs include xfstests id, plan id, filesystem flavor, image hash, helper versions, required privileges, mutation surface, dry-run or real lane, cleanup status, selected/skipped decision, artifact paths, and reproduction command.
5. CI/README wording cannot count xfstests coverage unless the command-plan proof and preflight proof are both fresh and the selected subset policy records not-run, skipped, unsupported, and failed rows separately.

title: [HIGH] build_btrfs_image fixture no longer stamps root-tree tree_block_csum
description:
# Review finding

`build_btrfs_image()` is still constructing a synthetic btrfs root-tree leaf without stamping its `tree_block_csum` field.

## Evidence
- Builder: `crates/ffs-core/src/lib.rs:22119-22204`
- It writes a leaf header and item payload at `root_logical = 0x4000`, but never calls the existing test CRC helper used elsewhere (`stamp_btrfs_test_tree_block_crc32c` at `crates/ffs-core/src/lib.rs:22283-22287`).
- The workspace test rerun still fails all `build_btrfs_image()` consumers with `Format("invalid field: tree_block_csum (CRC32C checksum mismatch)")`:
  - `open_fs_from_btrfs_image`
  - `open_fs_btrfs_debug_format`
  - `open_fs_btrfs_superblock_accessor`
  - `open_fs_btrfs_walk_root_tree`

## Why this is high severity
This builder underpins the basic btrfs open/root-tree smoke tests. Right now those tests are red because the fixture no longer satisfies the parser’s checksum contract.

## Repro
`rch exec -- env CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/tmp/rch_target_frankenfs_cod cargo test --workspace --all-targets`

## Expected fix direction
Stamp the synthetic root-tree block checksum after finalizing the leaf contents, and confirm the minimal open/root-tree tests go green independently of the larger readdir fixture family.
close_reason: Fixed: build_btrfs_image at crates/ffs-core/src/lib.rs:22185 now stamps CRC32C on both root-tree leaf and fs-tree leaf at lines 22298-22301 (csum = crc32c(&image[leaf+0x20..leaf+nodesize])). All cited consumers (open_fs_from_btrfs_image, open_fs_btrfs_debug_format, open_fs_btrfs_superblock_accessor, open_fs_btrfs_walk_root_tree) pass under rch workspace run.

title: Refine mounted write matrix with namespace atomicity and fsyncdir durability proofs
description:
Supplemental refinement for bd-rchk0.3.2.1. The mounted write matrix covers operation classes and no-partial-mutation cases, but users also need namespace durability evidence: create, rename, link, unlink, rmdir, symlink, and xattr updates must survive reopen according to explicit fsync/fsyncdir boundaries, and rejected operations must not leave split-brain parent/child state.

Scope: define namespace atomicity and directory durability scenarios for ext4 and btrfs mounted paths. The scenarios should distinguish data fsync, parent directory fsync, file close, unmount, forced reopen, unsupported operation rejection, and host capability skips. They should check both visible namespace state and underlying image/reopen state.

Required proof before close:
1. Unit/schema tests reject mounted write scenarios missing parent directory id, child inode/path id, namespace operation kind, fsync/fsyncdir boundary, expected survivor set, expected link count, reopen expectation, and no-partial-mutation checks.
2. Unit tests cover rename overwrite, rename no-overwrite, cross-directory rename, link count update, symlink target persistence, unlink after open handle, rmdir non-empty rejection, xattr namespace update, and unsupported fallocate no-op/refusal classification.
3. E2E mounted smoke covers at least one ext4 and one btrfs namespace scenario in the permissioned lane, plus a dry-run or fixture lane for missing capability.
4. Logs include scenario id, filesystem flavor, mount options, operation sequence, fsync/fsyncdir calls, pre/post directory entries, link counts, xattr keys, image hashes, reopen observations, cleanup status, artifact paths, and reproduction command.
5. Readiness gates cannot count mounted rw namespace coverage if only status-code checks ran without reopen and parent-directory durability proof.

title: [MAINT] FuseInodeLocks.table grows unboundedly — every accessed inode allocates a FuseInodeLock that is never evicted
description:
# Maintainability / resource finding from modes-of-reasoning pass

## Finding

`FuseInodeLocks` at crates/ffs-fuse/src/lib.rs:703-737 stores a `Mutex<BTreeMap<InodeNumber, Arc<FuseInodeLock>>>`. Every FUSE mutation operation (create/mkdir/unlink/rmdir/rename/setattr) calls `acquire()`, which does `table.entry(ino).or_insert_with(...)` — inserting a new entry on first encounter and never evicting.

For a long-running FUSE mount that touches many inodes, the table grows monotonically. With ~80 bytes per entry (key + Arc + FuseInodeLock fields), a mount that accesses a million inodes parks ~80 MB of never-freed locks.

## Evidence

- Storage: crates/ffs-fuse/src/lib.rs:705 `table: Mutex<BTreeMap<InodeNumber, Arc<FuseInodeLock>>>`
- Insertion: line 727 `table.entry(ino).or_insert_with(|| Arc::new(FuseInodeLock::default()))`
- No `remove` call anywhere — confirmed via `rg 'table\.\(remove\|clear\|retain\)' crates/ffs-fuse/`
- No TTL, no LRU eviction, no drop-on-last-guard hook

## Why this wasn't caught earlier

Not a deadlock (inspected in /deadlock-finder pass, out of that skill's scope). Not a correctness bug. Pure resource accumulation that only matters for long-running mounts. Relevant as soon as FrankenFS targets real production workloads.

## Fix directions

1. **Drop-on-last-guard cleanup.** When `FuseInodeGuard::drop` runs, check if the `Arc<FuseInodeLock>` strong_count is 1 (we hold the last ref). If so, remove the entry from the table. Race-safe because `acquire()` holds the table mutex during insert, so we can hold it during the last-guard removal check.
2. **Epoch-based GC.** Periodically scan the table and evict entries whose `Arc` strong_count is 1 (no active guard) AND that haven't been acquired in N seconds.
3. **Cap-based eviction.** Soft cap the table size; evict idle entries LRU when the cap is exceeded.

Option 1 is cleanest and matches the natural lifecycle (entries exist only when a guard is live).

## Acceptance

- A stress test creating/unlinking 100K inodes shows FuseInodeLocks.table stays bounded (approximately proportional to concurrent guards, not to total inodes-ever-accessed).
- No regression in the sorted-ino total-order guarantee (the crate's only concurrency invariant). /deadlock-finder sweep remains CLEAN.
- Unit test: acquire+drop a single inode's guard, assert the table is empty afterward.

## Severity rationale

P3 (low) — not exploitable, not correctness, not currently a production concern (FrankenFS mounts are short-lived in V1.x). But it is a real resource leak that will matter when V2+ targets longer-running deployments. Worth tracking so it doesn't get lost.
close_reason: Reconciled stale JSONL row: FuseInodeLocks leak fix already implemented and closure is present in commit 776fe07.

title: Refine xfstests preflight with remediation safety and no-auto-install guarantees
description:
Supplemental refinement for bd-rchk3.1.1. The xfstests prerequisite preflight should help operators fix missing host capability, but it must not auto-install packages, mutate host mount state, or imply a lane is authoritative before the operator has provisioned it. This bead turns preflight remediation into explicit, safe guidance.

Scope: define preflight remediation output for missing packages, helper binaries, FUSE permission, mount namespace support, scratch/test directories, dpkg lock state, RCH worker mismatch, and unsupported local hosts. Remediation may print commands or links, but default validation must be read-only and side-effect free.

Required proof before close:
1. Unit/script tests cover remediation classification for missing headers/libs, missing xfstests helpers, missing mkfs helpers, /dev/fuse absence, fusermount permission failure, dpkg lock, non-root mount denial, unavailable scratch/test roots, RCH worker mismatch, and intentionally unsupported local lane.
2. Tests prove preflight does not run package installation, does not mount/unmount real filesystems, does not create persistent host directories outside controlled temp paths, and does not mark remediation as complete without a fresh follow-up probe.
3. E2E/dry-run smoke uses fixture host states to emit remediation manifests and verifies every blocking item has user-action text, risk level, authoritative-lane impact, and reproduction command.
4. Logs include preflight id, probe version, host/kernel, worker id if any, prerequisite id, observed value, classification, remediation text id, side-effect policy, output paths, cleanup status, and reproduction command.
5. xfstests coverage claims remain blocked or host-skipped until a fresh side-effect-free preflight proves the prerequisites are present in the intended authoritative lane.

title: Cap ext4 read buffers before allocation
description: Ext4 read surfaces allocate the caller requested size before applying EOF/file-size truncation. A large read at or near EOF can allocate an oversized buffer even when the correct response is empty or small. Share a checked read-length helper across Ext4FsOps, Ext4ImageReader::read_file, and OpenFs ext4 read, and add regression tests for oversized EOF and tail reads.
close_reason: Capped ext4 read allocations to the bytes available before allocating across Ext4FsOps, Ext4ImageReader::read_file, and OpenFs ext4 reads; added oversized EOF/tail read regressions; fmt/check/clippy/tests/UBS passed.

Seed with valid data-FS + external-journal images, then mutate the external JBD2 superblock, UUID pairing, block size, and recovery-required flag combinations.

- `crates/ffs-core/src/lib.rs:3045-3119` (`maybe_probe_external_journal`)
- `crates/ffs-core/src/lib.rs:3121-3141` (`maybe_replay_ext4_journal` external-journal branch)

Acceptance:

- UUID mismatch, bad JBD2 superblock, and block-size mismatch remain classified `FfsError` results instead of aborts.

title: [FUZZ-GAP] extend fuzz_mvcc_operations to cover commit_ssi, commit_fcw_prechecked, stage_write_with_proof, SafeMerge policy
description:
# Fuzz coverage gap — MVCC advanced commit paths

## Current coverage

`fuzz/fuzz_targets/fuzz_mvcc_operations.rs` exercises:
- `MvccStore::begin`
- `Transaction::stage_write` (plain)
- `MvccStore::commit` (generic)
- `MvccStore::current_snapshot` + `read_visible`
- `MvccStore::prune_safe`

## Gap

The README's signature claim is that FrankenFS has 'adaptive conflict policy (Strict/SafeMerge/Adaptive with expected-loss decision model).' The *policy selection* lives on these APIs, which are not exercised by the current fuzz harness:

- `Transaction::stage_write_with_proof(block, bytes, proof)` — line 324 of ffs-mvcc/src/lib.rs. Carries the `MergeProof` that SafeMerge relies on. No fuzz driver produces proofs or feeds them through commit.
- `MvccStore::commit_ssi(txn)` — line 1528. Serializable-snapshot-isolation commit path.
- `MvccStore::commit_fcw_prechecked(txn)` — line 1478. Strict first-committer-wins with caller-supplied pre-check.
- `MvccStore::resolved_writes_for_commit(txn)` — line 1500. Drives the conflict-resolution arbiter that selects Strict vs SafeMerge.
- `MvccStore::commit_with_cow_allocator(...)` / `commit_ssi_with_cow_allocator(...)` — COW-integrated commits.
- `MvccStore::set_conflict_policy(ConflictPolicy::{Strict, SafeMerge, Adaptive})` + `adaptive_config` — policy switches.

## Why this matters

The README documents a 120-writer stress test proving 'SafeMerge achieved 9.5x lower expected loss than Strict' — this is one of the project's signature differentiators. But the fuzz suite currently only drives the plain `commit` path; adversarial inputs to the SSI / FCW-pre-checked / merge-proof paths would surface policy-selection edge cases (e.g., conflict detection misses, merge-proof-shaped corrupt inputs, adaptive-policy thrashing under pathological interleavings).

## Scope

Extend `fuzz/fuzz_targets/fuzz_mvcc_operations.rs` (or split into a new `fuzz_mvcc_commit_policies.rs` if the harness grows) to:

1. Randomly flip `ConflictPolicy` on the store during the sequence.
2. Generate occasional `MergeProof` values (valid + malformed) and call `stage_write_with_proof`.
3. Route commits through `commit` / `commit_ssi` / `commit_fcw_prechecked` according to a fuzz-chosen arm.
4. After each commit, invariants:
   - Commit seq strictly monotonic on success.
   - `read_visible(block, snapshot_before_commit)` never sees post-commit bytes.
   - `read_visible(block, snapshot_after_commit)` sees post-commit bytes for visible blocks.
   - Adaptive policy decision remains consistent with `effective_policy()` reporting.
5. Seed corpus: single-writer, two-writer conflict on same block, non-overlapping concurrent writes, merge-proof with wrong seq, prune-between-commits sequence.

## Acceptance

- Target drives `commit`, `commit_ssi`, `commit_fcw_prechecked` with frequency proportional to a fuzzable selector byte.
- Policy toggles exercised (`Strict` / `SafeMerge` / `Adaptive`).
- `stage_write_with_proof` called at least in one branch.
- Invariant assertions use `assert!`; all assertions hold on current implementation (regression baseline).
- Exec/s ≥ 500 (stateful harness floor per /testing-fuzzing skill rules).
close_reason: Implemented in commit fec9579: fuzz_mvcc_operations extended to drive commit_ssi/commit_fcw_prechecked/stage_write_with_proof with I1-I4 invariants + seed_policy_commit_paths corpus.

title: Harden ffs-dir test coverage with property-based and boundary tests
description:
## What
Expand ffs-dir from 49 tests to 70+ with property-based tests (proptest) and additional
boundary/corruption tests.

## Why
ffs-dir has the lowest test count (49) of any non-trivial crate. Directory operations are
safety-critical: a bug in add_entry/remove_entry can corrupt the entire directory structure.
The crate has proptest as a dev-dependency but no property-based tests yet.

## Current Coverage (49 tests)
- init_dir_block: 5 tests (basic, tail, sizes, inode numbers)
- add_entry: 10 tests (split, reuse, enospc, reject, max name, types)
- remove_entry: 7 tests (coalesce, first entry, nonexistent, overlap)
- htree: 8 tests (insert, find, remove, edge cases)
- compute_dx_hash: 3 tests (deterministic, seeds, names)
- Multi-operation: 3 tests (fill, cycle, alternating)
- Corruption detection: 3 tests (rec_len zero, misaligned, exceeds block)

## Gaps to Fill
1. Property-based tests (proptest):
   - proptest_add_remove_roundtrip: random names (1..255 bytes), random inodes,
     add then remove → block parseable, entry gone
   - proptest_add_never_exceeds_block: random sequence of adds → never writes past limit
   - proptest_init_parse_roundtrip: random self_ino, parent_ino, reserved_tail →
     init_dir_block → parse_dir_block → exactly . and .. with correct inodes
   - proptest_htree_insert_always_sorted: random hash sequence → entries always sorted

2. Boundary tests:
   - add_entry with exactly 0 bytes of slack (entry fits perfectly, no split needed)
   - remove_entry of last entry in block (rec_len extends to block end)
   - init_dir_block with reserved_tail = block_size - 24 (minimum viable)
   - add_entry with name at every alignment boundary (1,2,3,4,5,8,12,16 bytes)

3. Corruption resilience:
   - add_entry with rec_len pointing past block boundary
   - remove_entry with circular rec_len chain (entry points back to itself)
   - add_entry to block of all zeros (no valid first entry)

4. Stress tests:
   - Fill 4096-byte block completely, verify parse_dir_block recovers all entries
   - Add 100 entries, remove 50, add 50 more, verify final state

## Implementation Plan
Add to the existing #[cfg(test)] module in crates/ffs-dir/src/lib.rs.
proptest is already a dev-dependency of ffs-dir.

## Test Plan
- cargo test -p ffs-dir --lib → all pass, count >= 70
- proptest cases: 128 per property test
- No flakes in 3 consecutive runs

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] 4+ property-based tests added
- [ ] 5+ boundary tests added
- [ ] 3+ corruption resilience tests added
- [ ] 2+ stress tests added
- [ ] Total test count >= 70
- [ ] Zero test flakes
close_reason: Completed

title: Bitmap CRC: remove or deprecate the wrong compute_bitmap_checksum (XOR-based)
description:
ffs-ondisk/src/ext4.rs has two bitmap checksum functions:
1. ext4_bitmap_checksum (line ~1255): Correct — chains group_number as LE data bytes.
2. compute_bitmap_checksum (line ~1743): WRONG — XORs group_number with csum_seed.

The wrong function is used by verify_bitmap_checksum (line ~1752). The correct function is used by verify_block_bitmap_checksum and verify_inode_bitmap_checksum.

Callers of verify_bitmap_checksum (if any exist outside tests) will get wrong results. Either:
(a) Remove compute_bitmap_checksum and verify_bitmap_checksum entirely if no production code uses them.
(b) Fix compute_bitmap_checksum to use the correct algorithm (chain, not XOR).
(c) At minimum, add a comment explaining the bug and deprecate.

ACCEPTANCE: The XOR-based function is either removed, fixed, or clearly marked as wrong with a deprecation warning.
close_reason: Removed compute_bitmap_checksum and verify_bitmap_checksum (XOR-based, wrong). Replaced tests with correct versions. Commit 7f4d6eb.

title: add ffs-core fuzz target for ext4 crash-state and journal-replay mount boundary
description:
Apply /testing-fuzzing to the ext4 crash-state and journal-replay mount boundary in `ffs-core`.

## Target surface
- `OpenFs::from_device(...)` / `open_with_options(...)`
- `detect_and_recover_crash()` at `crates/ffs-core/src/lib.rs:2755`
- ext4 journal replay integration around mount open (`maybe_replay_ext4_journal`, `ext4_journal_replay`, `crash_recovery`)
- external-journal / replay-mode combinations that decide whether mount proceeds, degrades, or errors

## Why this matters
The repo already fuzzes lower-level JBD2 replay, but not the `ffs-core` integration boundary where superblock state bits, replay policy, journal outcome, and recovery-report exposure interact. This is exactly where mount-time recovery regressions hide.

## Scope
- Add a fuzz target that mutates minimally-valid ext4 images plus replay-mode / external-journal knobs and opens through `ffs-core`.
- Seed from current clean/dirty/error/orphan state fixtures and journal replay tests.
- Assert:
  - no panic / no hang
  - `crash_recovery()` and `ext4_journal_replay()` remain deterministic for the same input
  - recovery reports never claim replay counts larger than available journal metadata
  - skip-validation mode never triggers unintended mount-time recovery work
  - malformed external-journal combinations fail gracefully with stable errno/error class

## Acceptance
- New fuzz target registered in `fuzz/Cargo.toml`
- Seed corpus added from existing ext4 recovery fixtures
- Crashers minimized and promoted to regression coverage

title: Add indirect block pointer write support for e2compr beyond direct range
description:
BACKGROUND: The e2compr compressed write path (write_ext4_compressed_cluster, write_ext4_uncompressed_cluster) uses set_indirect_block_ptr to write block pointers in the inode's i_block array. This function currently rejects logical_block >= 12 because entries 12-14 are the single/double/triple indirect pointers, and overwriting them with data block pointers would corrupt the indirect addressing table.

CURRENT LIMITATION: With the smallest cluster size (4 blocks, shift=2) and 4K block size, only clusters at logical blocks 0-3, 4-7, and 8-11 can be written — files up to 48KB. Cluster shift 3 (8 blocks) limits to 32KB. Larger files cannot be compressed at all.

THE READ PATH ALREADY WORKS: resolve_indirect_block handles single/double/triple indirect lookups for reading. The write side needs the inverse operation.

IMPLEMENTATION PLAN:
1. Add write_indirect_block_ptr(cx, scope, inode, alloc, logical_block, phys) that mirrors resolve_indirect_block but writes:
   - For logical_block < 12: direct write to inode.extent_bytes (existing code).
   - For 12 <= logical_block < 12 + ptrs_per_block: allocate or read the single-indirect block (i_block[12]), write the pointer at the correct offset within it.
   - For double-indirect range: allocate/read i_block[13] -> intermediate block -> write pointer.
   - For triple-indirect range: three-level allocation/read.
2. Intermediate blocks must be allocated on first use (when i_block[12/13/14] is 0).
3. Allocated intermediate blocks must be freed when the last pointer in them becomes 0 (during cluster freeing).
4. Update set_indirect_block_ptr callers to use the new function.
5. Add tests for files that span into the indirect range.

COMPLEXITY: Medium-high. The read path is ~120 lines; the write path will be ~200 lines due to allocation of intermediate blocks. Must correctly handle:
- First write to a new indirect level (allocate the intermediate block).
- Overwrite of existing pointers (read-modify-write the intermediate block).
- Freeing of intermediate blocks when all their pointers become 0.

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- Compressed write works for files up to at least 1MB (requires single-indirect support).
- Compressed read/write roundtrip test for a file spanning into indirect block range.
- set_indirect_block_ptr error at lb >= 12 is removed.
- No block leaks: freed clusters also free their intermediate block pointers if they become empty.
close_reason: All subtasks complete: single-indirect (bd-z15u, commit 18cd3d7) and double/triple indirect (bd-pv9l, commit 06a4288). e2compr can now compress files of any size.

title: EPIC: V1.1 Post-Parity Hardening and Operationalization
description:
# EPIC: V1.1 Post-Parity Hardening and Operationalization

## Context
Tracked V1 parity is complete (75/75), but FrankenFS is explicitly still early-stage and experimental for mount/write operations. The project now needs a rigorous post-parity program focused on production-grade correctness confidence, failure containment, observability, and predictable performance under stress.

This epic converts that post-parity intent into an executable dependency graph that is:
- granular enough for independent agent execution,
- explicit about acceptance gates,
- self-contained (no need to reopen planning docs during execution).

## Why this exists
Parity completion proves breadth of implemented behavior, not necessarily depth of operational hardening. Remaining risk lives in:
- durability under crash/fault scenarios,
- clarity of experimental behavior (especially btrfs RW path),
- incomplete operational wiring (CLI mount path for managed backpressure/per-core hooks),
- benchmark/regression signal fidelity,
- unresolved spec open questions that can cause architecture drift if left ambiguous.

## Scope
This epic is fulfilled when all child epics below are complete:
1. Durable MVCC version-store implementation and replay hardening.
2. CLI mount wiring for managed backpressure/per-core scheduling.
3. btrfs experimental RW hardening and explicit compatibility contracts.
4. Fuzzing and adversarial test-depth expansion.
5. Benchmark fidelity and regression-guard upgrades.
6. Closure of formal open questions (with accepted decisions integrated into docs/tests/code).

## Non-goals
- Reopening excluded V1 scope items (multi-device btrfs, fscrypt, inline_data, etc.) unless explicitly approved in a separate scope-change epic.
- Marketing/docs polish without technical acceptance deltas.

## Definition of Done
- Child epics closed with evidence-backed acceptance criteria.
- FEATURE_PARITY.md, spec docs, and harness signals remain internally consistent.
- New work items include explicit test and observability requirements, not implementation-only checklists.

title: EPIC: Durable MVCC Version Store and Crash Replay
description:
# EPIC: Durable MVCC Version Store and Crash Replay

## Problem Statement
MVCC currently provides strong in-memory isolation and conflict semantics, but the long-term architecture calls for a durable version-store path with restart replay guarantees. This is explicitly documented as planned in the spec and is a key reliability boundary between "experimental" and "operationally trustworthy" write paths.

## Why this matters
Without a durable MVCC version-store:
- commit visibility across process restart is less explicit,
- crash-time reconstruction relies on narrower recovery channels,
- validation of full write-path durability semantics remains incomplete.

## Scope
Implement and harden a persistent MVCC version-store workflow including:
1. append protocol and on-disk record format,
2. replay engine and corruption handling,
3. mount/open integration + observability,
4. deterministic crash matrix coverage.

## Required Evidence
- deterministic replay tests proving latest committed state reconstruction,
- corrupted/truncated log handling tests with explicit outcomes,
- metrics and CLI visibility for replay/apply/skip decisions.

## Done Criteria
- Child tasks completed with green gates and replay evidence.
- Spec/architecture docs updated from "planned" to implemented behavior.
- No silent recovery ambiguity (each replay path emits a reasoned outcome).
close_reason: All child beads and verification gate closed. Program gate bd-h6nz.8 passed.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text:
Future-self note:
Treat this epic as a correctness program, not a feature program. Every step should produce replay-oracle evidence. If a design choice cannot be tested deterministically under crash schedules, it is not ready to ship.

title: Define durable MVCC version-store record format and invariants
description:
## Objective
Turn the planned durable MVCC record model into an implementable contract with no ambiguity around ordering, checksums, atomicity boundaries, and forward compatibility.

## Background
Spec section 5.9 defines high-level goals. This task crystallizes the concrete wire/storage format and invariant ledger required before implementation.

## Deliverables
1. Record schema for version-store entries (header + payload + integrity fields).
2. Explicit ordering model (commit_seq monotonicity, tie-breaking, duplicate handling).
3. Crash consistency points (what is guaranteed after power loss at each append stage).
4. Corruption semantics (truncate vs reject vs quarantine behavior).
5. Compatibility/versioning strategy for future schema evolution.

## Required Artifacts
- Spec updates in COMPREHENSIVE_SPEC_FOR_FRANKENFS_V1.md + PROPOSED_ARCHITECTURE.md.
- Invariant checklist suitable for test oracles.
- Example binary layout snippets and decode walkthrough.

## Acceptance Criteria
- No undecided fields in the record layout.
- Replay behavior is fully deterministic for all malformed/torn cases.
- At least 5 explicit invariants are documented and test-mappable.
## Mandatory Test & Logging Contract
1. Unit Tests (required)
- Add or extend crate-local tests for happy path, boundary/edge cases, and explicit error paths.
- Include at least one invariant-focused negative test proving failure handling is intentional.

2. Deterministic E2E Scripts (required)
- Add or extend deterministic E2E coverage (prefer `scripts/e2e/` or existing harness E2E path) with explicit setup/teardown.
- Every scenario must emit machine-parseable pass/fail markers plus stable `scenario_id` values.

3. Structured Logging (required)
- Emit structured logs at key state transitions and failure boundaries.
- Critical events must include `operation_id`, `scenario_id`, and outcome/error classification fields.
- Add test assertions (unit/integration/E2E) validating critical log markers for both success and failure paths.

4. Validation Evidence (required)
- Closure must include artifact paths and concise evidence summary for tests + logs.
- CPU-intensive gates must be run via `rch exec -- ...` when invoking cargo builds/checks/tests/benches.

title: Implement append-only durable MVCC log writer with integrity checks
description:
## Objective
Implement the write path for durable version-store records in `ffs-mvcc`, including checksum emission, append atomicity discipline, and bounded fsync strategy.

## Scope
1. Writer API for staging + append + durability boundary.
2. Per-record integrity fields and verification-on-write.
3. Batching policy with explicit tradeoffs (latency vs durability).
4. Backpressure integration when persistence path saturates.

## Guardrails
- No silent drop of records on partial I/O.
- Distinguish retryable I/O errors vs fatal format/invariant violations.
- Emit structured logs for append lifecycle and failure classes.

## Tests
- Unit tests for normal append, short write, torn write simulation, checksum mismatch.
- Property test for monotonic ordering preservation across randomized append sequences.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Appended records decode correctly by a strict reader.
- Failure cases return explicit typed errors (not generic format strings).
- `cargo test -p ffs-mvcc` includes deterministic coverage for append failure modes.
## Mandatory Test & Logging Contract
1. Unit Tests (required)
- Add or extend crate-local tests for happy path, boundary/edge cases, and explicit error paths.
- Include at least one invariant-focused negative test proving failure handling is intentional.

2. Deterministic E2E Scripts (required)

- Every scenario must emit machine-parseable pass/fail markers plus stable `scenario_id` values.

- Critical events must include `operation_id`, `scenario_id`, and outcome/error classification fields.
- Add test assertions (unit/integration/E2E) validating critical log markers for both success and failure paths.

4. Validation Evidence (required)

- CPU-intensive gates must be run via `rch exec -- ...` when invoking cargo builds/checks/tests/benches.
close_reason: Implemented WalWriter append-only durable MVCC log writer. New module: crates/ffs-mvcc/src/wal_writer.rs with WalWriter, WalWriteError (typed error classification: retryable vs fatal), SyncPolicy (Immediate/EveryN/Manual), WalWriterConfig (verify_writes, backpressure_threshold_bytes), AppendResult. Enforces D1 (monotonicity) and D8 (sentinel rejection). Structured logging at all state transitions (wal_append_start/ok/err, wal_sync_ok/err, wal_backpressure, wal_verify_ok/err, wal_flush_ok). PersistentMvccStore now uses WalWriter instead of internal WalFile. 20 new unit tests + 1 property test (proptest_monotonic_ordering_preserved). E2E: scripts/e2e/ffs_wal_writer_e2e.sh (11 scenarios). title: Implement durable MVCC replay engine with corruption/torn-record recovery policy
description:
## Objective
Build restart replay for durable version-store records, producing deterministic reconstruction of latest committed visibility while handling partial/corrupt tails safely.

## Scope
1. Replay scanner and decoder with strict validation.
2. Tail-handling policy (truncate-to-last-good vs fail-fast with operator override).
3. Replay outcome classification (`clean`, `truncated_tail`, `corrupt_abort`, etc.).
4. Integration into MVCC in-memory structures without violating snapshot invariants.

## Tests
- Replay from clean log.
- Replay with truncated final record.
- Replay with mid-log corruption.
- Replay idempotence (multiple replays yield identical state).
- Replay ordering correctness across randomized commit sequences.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Replay outcomes are explicit and auditable.
- Invariant checks after replay pass for all supported scenarios.
- Corruption handling never produces silent partial visibility.
## Mandatory Test & Logging Contract
1. Unit Tests (required)

title: EPIC: CLI Mount Path Wiring for Managed Backpressure and Per-Core Scheduling
description:
# EPIC: CLI Mount Path Wiring for Managed Backpressure and Per-Core Scheduling

## Problem Statement
README explicitly documents that default `ffs-cli mount` does not wire optional backpressure/per-core scheduling hooks. This leaves high-value runtime controls available in code but not reliably exposed in the primary operator entrypoint.

## Why this matters
- Operators may believe pressure controls are active when they are not.
- Performance/latency investigations become noisy due to hidden runtime-mode differences.
- Hardening efforts in `ffs-fuse` and `ffs-core` are underutilized operationally.

## Scope
1. Expose runtime-mode controls in CLI in a clear and safe way.
2. Wire mount codepaths to managed/per-core execution when selected.
3. Ensure default mode remains conservative and predictable.
4. Add explicit runtime telemetry and tests for mode behavior.

## Done Criteria
- CLI supports explicit mode selection with documented defaults.
- Mount runtime reports active mode in logs/metrics.
- Integration tests verify behavior for standard vs managed modes.
close_reason: All child beads and verification gate closed. Program gate bd-h6nz.8 passed.

title: EPIC: btrfs Experimental RW Hardening and Compatibility Contracts
description:
# EPIC: btrfs Experimental RW Hardening and Compatibility Contracts

## Problem Statement
btrfs mount/write support exists in experimental form with intentionally limited feature coverage. The remaining risk is not just missing features, but ambiguity around what is guaranteed, what is best-effort, and how failures are surfaced.

## Why this matters
- Experimental modes require tighter guardrails, not looser ones.
- Clear unsupported-op semantics prevent silent corruption and operator confusion.
- Recovery/scrub pathways must be validated under write churn, not only static metadata scenarios.

## Scope
1. Formalize current supported operation matrix and strict unsupported behavior.
2. Harden runtime checks so unsupported paths fail deterministically and safely.
3. Expand crash/recovery/scrub tests for btrfs write scenarios.
4. Keep alignment between docs, harness, and runtime behavior.

## Done Criteria
- Capability matrix and runtime behavior match exactly.
- Unsupported cases return stable errno + explicit logs.
- btrfs experimental write path has adversarial test evidence, not just happy-path coverage.
close_reason: All child beads and verification gate closed. Program gate bd-h6nz.8 passed.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text:
Future-self note:
For experimental btrfs RW, deterministic rejection of unsupported behavior is a safety feature. Prefer explicit ENOTSUP/EROFS-style clarity over partial behavior that is difficult to reason about.

title: Add crash-consistency matrix for btrfs experimental write paths
description:
## Objective
Validate btrfs experimental RW behavior under crash/restart conditions with deterministic fault schedules.

## Scope
1. Target mutation scenarios (create/write/rename/unlink on supported ops).
2. Crash points around transaction boundaries and metadata updates.
3. Post-restart validation oracle for metadata consistency and visibility.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Deterministic crash matrix covers at least 10 distinct crash points across supported ops.
- No silent metadata divergence after replay/recovery.
- Failures produce actionable evidence artifacts.
## Mandatory Test & Logging Contract
1. Unit Tests (required)
- Add or extend crate-local tests for happy path, boundary/edge cases, and explicit error paths.
- Include at least one invariant-focused negative test proving failure handling is intentional.

2. Deterministic E2E Scripts (required)
- Add or extend deterministic E2E coverage (prefer `scripts/e2e/` or existing harness E2E path) with explicit setup/teardown.
- Every scenario must emit machine-parseable pass/fail markers plus stable `scenario_id` values.

3. Structured Logging (required)
- Emit structured logs at key state transitions and failure boundaries.
- Critical events must include `operation_id`, `scenario_id`, and outcome/error classification fields.
- Add test assertions (unit/integration/E2E) validating critical log markers for both success and failure paths.

4. Validation Evidence (required)
- Closure must include artifact paths and concise evidence summary for tests + logs.
- CPU-intensive gates must be run via `rch exec -- ...` when invoking cargo builds/checks/tests/benches.

comments.author: NobleKnoll
comments.text:
Implementation complete. Evidence summary:

## Deliverables

### Write-Churn Stress Tests (5 new, 300 total in ffs-repair)
1. **scrub_detects_corruption_after_write_churn** — 10 rapid writes dirty symbols, scrub still finds corruption
2. **recovery_succeeds_with_fresh_symbols_under_write_churn** — eager-policy refresh keeps symbols current; recovery succeeds
3. **evidence_ledger_captures_churn_context** — repair events recorded with corruption+repair evidence under churn
4. **no_false_clean_when_corruption_exists_under_heavy_churn** — INVARIANT: 50 writes, 3 corrupt blocks; scrub finds all 3
5. **write_churn_with_staleness_timeout_still_recovers** — staleness timeout refreshes symbols; subsequent corruption recovered

### Key Invariants Proven
- Scrub NEVER reports 0 corruption when blocks are actually corrupt, regardless of write churn state
- Recovery either succeeds (fresh symbols) or produces explicit abort with reason (evidence ledger)
- Evidence output always contains corruption_detected + repair_attempted/succeeded/failed events
- Staleness timeout fires under rapid writes, keeping symbols bounded-age fresh

### Evidence and Telemetry
- RefreshTelemetry (from bd-h6nz.6.3): dirty_groups, max_dirty_age_ms — proves churn state visibility
- Evidence ledger events: CorruptionDetected, RepairAttempted, RepairSucceeded, RepairFailed, SymbolRefresh
- Structured logs: refresh_group_marked_dirty, refresh_staleness_timeout_triggered, symbol_refresh_complete

### E2E Script
- scripts/e2e/ffs_btrfs_write_churn_e2e.sh (9 scenarios)

## Validation Gates
- cargo check --workspace: clean
- cargo clippy -p ffs-repair -- -D warnings: clean
- cargo fmt: clean
- cargo test -p ffs-repair --lib: 300 pass, 0 fail

title: EPIC: Fuzzing and Adversarial Input Expansion
description:
# EPIC: Fuzzing and Adversarial Input Expansion

## Problem Statement
The repo has a useful `tests/fuzz_corpus/` seed set, but a comprehensive fuzzing lifecycle requires first-class targets, repeatable automation, and crash-to-regression promotion workflows.

## Why this matters
Metadata parsers and recovery logic are high-risk surfaces where bug classes are often discovered by generated adversarial inputs rather than hand-authored unit tests.

## Scope
1. Add/maintain dedicated fuzz targets for critical parser and mutation surfaces.
2. Reuse and grow the existing corpus in a structured way.
3. Automate nightly fuzz runs and triage artifacts.
4. Guarantee reproducibility and promotion of minimized crashers into deterministic tests.

## Done Criteria
- Fuzz targets exist for priority surfaces and run in CI/nightly policy.
- Crasher minimization/promotion path is documented and exercised.
- Corpus growth is intentional (coverage-guided), not accidental file accumulation.
close_reason: All child beads and verification gate closed. Program gate bd-h6nz.8 passed.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text:
Future-self note:
Fuzzing value comes from lifecycle discipline: target quality, reproducible crashes, and regression promotion. Raw CPU hours without triage plumbing are low leverage.

title: EPIC: Benchmark Fidelity and Regression Guard Strengthening
description:
# EPIC: Benchmark Fidelity and Regression Guard Strengthening

## Problem Statement
Benchmark scaffolding and baselines exist, but post-parity evolution needs stronger anti-flake methodology, host-profile awareness, and explicit treatment of degraded/runtime-mode performance envelopes.

## Why this matters
Without disciplined benchmark governance, teams either miss regressions or chase noise. We need actionable signals that survive machine variance and evolving workloads.

## Scope
1. Expand benchmark taxonomy beyond current hot paths.
2. Add robust comparison methodology (effect sizes, noise thresholds, guardrails).
3. Validate performance under pressure/degradation modes.
4. Document operator workflow for interpreting and acting on regressions.

## Done Criteria
- Bench suite covers critical latency/throughput/write-amplification surfaces.
- Regression guard emits high-signal failures with clear next steps.
- Performance gates are reproducible across supported host profiles.
close_reason: All child beads and verification gate closed. Program gate bd-h6nz.8 passed.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text:
Future-self note:
Benchmarks must optimize decision quality, not vanity numbers. Build guardrails that catch meaningful regressions while suppressing machine-noise churn.

title: Define benchmark taxonomy, host profiles, and acceptance envelopes
description:

## Scope

2. Define host profiles and normalization rules.
3. Set actionable thresholds with rationale (not arbitrary percentages).

## Acceptance Criteria

- Thresholds encode noise tolerance and business risk rationale.
- CI guard configuration references taxonomy IDs directly.

title: Implement statistically robust regression comparator with anti-flake hysteresis

Replace simplistic threshold-only comparisons with a regression gate that accounts for noise, effect size, and repeated confirmation before failing builds.

- CI output includes workload, metric, baseline, delta, and confidence context.
- False-positive rate is measurably reduced versus prior guard.

title: EPIC: Formal Open-Question Closure and Decision Capture
description:
# EPIC: Formal Open-Question Closure and Decision Capture

## Problem Statement
The comprehensive spec tracks open questions (OQ1..OQ7). Leaving these unresolved creates drift risk: implementation may proceed with implicit assumptions that are not documented, not testable, and not auditable.

## Why this matters
FrankenFS is spec-first. Unresolved questions in critical areas (native-mode boundaries, conflict policy, symbol invalidation, persistence format, etc.) must become explicit decisions with evidence, loss tradeoffs, and integration plans.

## Scope
1. Close each OQ with a decision record (what, why, alternatives rejected).
2. Attach concrete verification and migration implications.
3. Convert accepted decisions into executable tasks and dependencies.
4. Keep parity/spec docs synchronized with implementation reality.

## Done Criteria
- OQ1..OQ7 each have an explicit accepted state (or explicit defer rationale).
- Decision artifacts link to tests/benchmarks where applicable.
- No architecture-critical behavior remains "implied but undocumented".
close_reason: All child beads and verification gate closed. Program gate bd-h6nz.8 passed.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text:
Future-self note:
Every OQ closure must include: decision, rejected alternatives, evidence basis, and what tests/docs change because of that decision. "We think" is insufficient.

title: OQ1 closure: Native-mode on-disk boundary and mutation contract
description:
## Objective
Close OQ1 by defining exact boundaries between compatibility-mode interpretation of ext4/btrfs images and FrankenFS native-mode mutation/storage semantics.

## Key Questions
1. What bytes may FrankenFS mutate in compatibility mode?
2. How are native-only structures/versioning stored without violating compatibility promises?
3. What opt-in controls are required before native mutations are allowed?

## Deliverables
- Decision record with alternatives and expected-loss tradeoffs.
- Explicit compatibility contract language for docs/CLI.
- Testable invariants for mode-boundary enforcement.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Boundary rules are explicit and unambiguous.
- Docs and runtime mode flags reflect the same contract.
- At least one enforcement test exists for accidental boundary crossing.
## Mandatory Test & Logging Contract
1. Unit Tests (required)
- Add or extend crate-local tests for happy path, boundary/edge cases, and explicit error paths.
- Include at least one invariant-focused negative test proving failure handling is intentional.

2. Deterministic E2E Scripts (required)
- Add or extend deterministic E2E coverage (prefer `scripts/e2e/` or existing harness E2E path) with explicit setup/teardown.
- Every scenario must emit machine-parseable pass/fail markers plus stable `scenario_id` values.

3. Structured Logging (required)
- Emit structured logs at key state transitions and failure boundaries.
- Critical events must include `operation_id`, `scenario_id`, and outcome/error classification fields.
- Add test assertions (unit/integration/E2E) validating critical log markers for both success and failure paths.

4. Validation Evidence (required)
- Closure must include artifact paths and concise evidence summary for tests + logs.
- CPU-intensive gates must be run via `rch exec -- ...` when invoking cargo builds/checks/tests/benches.
## Decision-Closure Validation Addendum
Because this bead may produce policy/spec decisions before code lands, closure requires:
1. An executable validation matrix mapping each accepted decision to concrete unit-test and E2E scenarios.
2. Explicit follow-on implementation beads (or updates to existing beads) carrying those scenarios into code/tests.
3. Structured logging expectations for decision-sensitive branches (required fields + failure classes).
4. Evidence that no accepted policy remains without a corresponding testable implementation path.
close_reason: OQ1 resolved: Native-mode boundary contract implemented with MountMode enum, boundary enforcement guards, ModeViolation error, CLI --native flag, decision record, structured logging, 12 unit tests, E2E script

title: OQ3 closure: Repair-symbol invalidation/refresh policy under heavy writes
description:
## Objective
Close OQ3 by defining robust policy for symbol freshness, invalidation, and refresh scheduling during sustained mutation workloads.

## Key Questions

comments.author: NobleKnoll
comments.text:
OQ3 closure: Repair-symbol invalidation/refresh policy under heavy writes — COMPLETE

## Formal Symbol Freshness Policy (State Machine)

The refresh policy is codified as a 3-variant state machine with 6 trigger modes:

### RefreshPolicy (pipeline.rs:56-68)
- **Eager**: Refresh immediately on every write (metadata groups)
- **Lazy { max_staleness }**: Defer refresh until scrub or staleness timeout (data groups, default 30s)
- **Adaptive { risk_threshold, max_staleness }**: Switch eager/lazy based on corruption posterior

### GroupRefreshState (pipeline.rs:122-141)
State machine per group: dirty (bool), dirty_since (Instant), policy (RefreshPolicy), last_refresh (Instant)

### RefreshMode (pipeline.rs:70-92)
6 trigger modes: Recovery, EagerWrite, LazyScrub, AdaptiveEagerWrite, AdaptiveLazyScrub, StalenessTimeout

### Trigger Conditions
1. Eager policy: every write triggers immediate refresh (EagerWrite)
2. Lazy policy: scrub boundary triggers refresh (LazyScrub); OR dirty_age >= max_staleness triggers timeout (StalenessTimeout)
3. Adaptive policy: posterior >= risk_threshold → eager behavior; else lazy behavior with staleness timeout
4. Recovery trigger: after successful block recovery (Recovery)
5. Flush trigger: write-back cache flush queues groups for deferred refresh via QueuedRepairRefresh

### Key Questions Answered
1. **When must symbols be marked stale?** — On any write to the group (mark_group_dirty_with_cause)
2. **How is stale-symbol risk bounded?** — max_staleness timeout forces refresh even under sustained writes
3. **What refresh cadence minimizes risk vs overhead?** — Adaptive policy uses Bayesian posterior to dynamically select eager/lazy. DurabilityAutopilot computes optimal symbol count via expected-loss minimization.

## New Deliverables

### RefreshTelemetry (pipeline.rs:94-120)
- GroupRefreshSummary: per-group observability (dirty, dirty_age_ms, policy, since_last_refresh_ms)
- RefreshTelemetry: aggregate snapshot (tracked_groups, dirty_groups, max_dirty_age_ms)
- refresh_telemetry() method: builds real-time state snapshot for CLI/telemetry consumers
- All types derive Serialize for JSON output

### Tests (7 new, 295 total pass in ffs-repair)
- refresh_telemetry_reports_clean_state
- refresh_telemetry_tracks_dirty_group
- refresh_telemetry_serializes_to_json
- refresh_telemetry_multiple_groups_sorted
- churn_writes_respect_staleness_budget (invariant: timeout fires under rapid writes)
- eager_policy_refreshes_on_every_write (positive: generation increments on each write)
- lazy_policy_defers_refresh_until_scrub_or_timeout (negative: no refresh within budget)

### E2E Script
- scripts/e2e/ffs_refresh_policy_e2e.sh (9 scenarios)

### Structured Logging
Key markers: refresh_policy_evaluated, refresh_staleness_timeout_triggered, refresh_group_marked_dirty, adaptive_refresh_policy_resolved, symbol_refresh_queued, symbol_refresh_complete, symbol_refresh_deferred

## Validation Gates
- cargo check --workspace: clean
- cargo clippy -p ffs-repair -- -D warnings: clean
- cargo fmt: clean
- cargo test -p ffs-repair --lib: 295 pass, 0 fail

## Follow-on Implementation Path
All policy decisions have corresponding testable implementations:
- Eager/Lazy/Adaptive policies: unit-tested via eager_policy/lazy_policy/churn tests
- Staleness timeout: staleness_timeout_forces_refresh + churn_writes_respect_staleness_budget
- Telemetry: refresh_telemetry_* tests validate observability
- Evidence: SymbolRefreshDetail already records all refresh events

title: Integrate accepted OQ decisions into specs, parity tracking, and executable backlog
description:
## Objective
Perform closure sweep after OQ decisions so all canonical docs and dependent work items reflect accepted outcomes.

## Scope
1. Update spec + architecture docs with final OQ statuses.
2. Update FEATURE_PARITY notes where decisions alter tracked behavior.
3. Create/adjust follow-on implementation beads for any accepted policy changes.

## Acceptance Criteria
- No OQ section remains stale after decision completion.
- Linked implementation beads exist for every non-trivial accepted change.
- Drift audit confirms docs, backlog, and code intent are synchronized.
## Mandatory Test & Logging Contract
1. Unit Tests (required)
- Add or extend crate-local tests for happy path, boundary/edge cases, and explicit error paths.
- Include at least one invariant-focused negative test proving failure handling is intentional.

2. Deterministic E2E Scripts (required)
- Add or extend deterministic E2E coverage (prefer `scripts/e2e/` or existing harness E2E path) with explicit setup/teardown.
- Every scenario must emit machine-parseable pass/fail markers plus stable `scenario_id` values.

3. Structured Logging (required)
- Emit structured logs at key state transitions and failure boundaries.
- Critical events must include `operation_id`, `scenario_id`, and outcome/error classification fields.
- Add test assertions (unit/integration/E2E) validating critical log markers for both success and failure paths.

4. Validation Evidence (required)
- Closure must include artifact paths and concise evidence summary for tests + logs.
- CPU-intensive gates must be run via `rch exec -- ...` when invoking cargo builds/checks/tests/benches.
## Decision-Closure Validation Addendum
Because this bead may produce policy/spec decisions before code lands, closure requires:
1. An executable validation matrix mapping each accepted decision to concrete unit-test and E2E scenarios.
2. Explicit follow-on implementation beads (or updates to existing beads) carrying those scenarios into code/tests.
3. Structured logging expectations for decision-sensitive branches (required fields + failure classes).
4. Evidence that no accepted policy remains without a corresponding testable implementation path.
close_reason: OQ decision integration complete. Created oq_decision_matrix.rs with canonical matrix mapping all 7 accepted OQ decisions (OQ1-OQ7) to their implementation evidence: decision docs, unit test patterns, E2E scripts, impl crates, and structured logging targets. Includes check_decision_docs() and check_e2e_scripts() validation functions. 10 unit tests pass. E2E gate ffs_oq_decision_integration_e2e.sh validates 9 scenarios (module existence, all 7 tracked, docs/scripts/crates exist, FEATURE_PARITY refs, no stale markers, JSON round-trip, unit tests): 9/9 PASS.

title: EPIC: Operator Tooling, Runbooks, and Observability Polish
description:
# EPIC: Operator Tooling, Runbooks, and Observability Polish

## Problem Statement
Core mechanics are present, but operator experience for diagnosis/recovery still depends heavily on source familiarity. Post-parity maturity requires turning expert-only workflows into routine, documented, observable operations.

## Scope
1. Tighten evidence/inspection UX for incident diagnosis.
2. Add runbook-grade procedures for common failure classes.
3. Ensure CLI/TUI signals map clearly to actionable next steps.

## Done Criteria
- Incident workflows are reproducible from runbooks + CLI outputs.
- Critical runtime states (degradation, replay, repair) are visible and interpretable.
- Documentation reflects actual operator paths, not aspirational architecture.
close_reason: All child beads and verification gate closed. Program gate bd-h6nz.8 passed.

Operator polish should be validated by drills. If runbooks are never executed in simulated incidents, they are documentation theater.

title: Write runbooks for replay failure, corruption recovery, and pressure incidents
description:
## Objective
Provide step-by-step operator procedures for top incident classes so recovery does not depend on tribal knowledge.

title: Run operator tabletop drills and capture remediation deltas
description:
## Objective
Validate operational readiness by running scripted incident drills and capturing documentation/tooling gaps discovered during execution.

## Drill Scenarios
1. Mount failure due to replay anomaly.
2. Corruption detected with partial repair success.
3. Sustained pressure causing throttle/shed behavior.

## Deliverables
- Drill transcripts and timings.
- Gap list with follow-on beads where needed.
- Updated runbooks from observed friction.

## Acceptance Criteria
- At least one drill per scenario completed end-to-end.
- Post-drill retro identifies concrete improvements, not generic notes.
- New remediation beads created for critical misses.

title: Standardize operator-facing error taxonomy and remediation hints across CLI/TUI/evidence

- Better automation ergonomics via stable error codes.

title: EPIC: Cross-Epic Verification Orchestration and Log Contract
description:
# EPIC: Cross-Epic Verification Orchestration and Log Contract

## Problem Statement
Current verification expectations are strong but can drift across epics without a shared scenario catalog, log-field contract, and artifact format.

## Why This Matters For Users
- Incident diagnosis is faster when evidence and logs are consistent across subsystems.
- Release confidence improves when E2E scripts and outputs are comparable across tracks.
- Automation is safer with stable machine-readable pass/fail and artifact schemas.

## Scope
1. Shared deterministic E2E scenario catalog and ID conventions.
2. Shared structured logging field contract for verification scenarios.
3. Shared artifact manifest + retention/redaction policy.
4. Shared verification runner conventions and quality checks.

## Done Criteria
- Verification gates across all epics consume the shared contract.
- Drift detection exists for scenario IDs/log fields/artifact schema.
- Operator and CI workflows have one canonical verification contract.
close_reason: All child beads and verification gate closed. Program gate bd-h6nz.8 passed.

title: [HIGH] 16 KiB btrfs readdir fixture bypasses the shared checksum helper and drifts from parser semantics
description:
# Review finding

The remaining red btrfs directory-walk family is the only synthetic-tree fixture in `ffs-core` still hand-rolling tree-block checksum logic instead of going through the shared test helper.

## Evidence
- Shared helper exists at `crates/ffs-core/src/lib.rs:22328-22332`: `stamp_btrfs_test_tree_block_crc32c(image, logical)`.
- That helper is used by the green 4 KiB synthetic-tree fixtures (`crates/ffs-core/src/lib.rs:22357-22561`, `23010-23011`).
- `build_btrfs_readdir_image()` instead inlines its own checksum math at `crates/ffs-core/src/lib.rs:23372-23375` while also switching the superblock `nodesize` to `16384`.
- The current full workspace gate still fails the six readdir-specific tests on `tree_block_csum (CRC32C checksum mismatch)`, which means the hand-rolled 16 KiB path has drifted from what the parser expects.

## Why this is high severity
This is now the only remaining workspace-red btrfs fixture family after the default-root smoke fix landed. Keeping a second checksum implementation in test builders is actively hiding the real contract and slowing down recovery.

## Expected fix direction
Generalize the shared test checksum helper to accept nodesize/logical span explicitly, then route `build_btrfs_readdir_image()` through that helper and add a narrow regression test that proves a 16 KiB stamped leaf round-trips through the current parser.
close_reason: Already shipped; targeted btrfs readdir repros now pass

title: [V1.x] Wire ffs-repair::ScrubDaemon into ffs mount path for background auto-scrub
description:
# Feature: automatic scrub daemon on mount

Tracks the remaining integration work identified by bd-9xitm. The README
and the corresponding Limitation entry are now honest that background
auto-scrub is not started by `ffs mount` in V1.0; this bead tracks the
V1.x follow-up.

## Current state

- `ffs-repair::ScrubDaemon` at crates/ffs-repair/src/pipeline.rs:695+ is
  implemented, backpressure-aware, and covered by
  `e2e_survive_five_percent_random_block_corruption_with_daemon`.
- RaptorQ encode/decode pipeline is wired end-to-end (`decode_group`,
  `RepairPipeline`, evidence ledger).
- CLI `ffs repair` / `ffs fsck --repair` expose explicit invocation.
- `RepairFlushLifecycle` fires post-write-flush so symbols refresh after
  legitimate mutations.

## Gap

- `crates/ffs-fuse`, `crates/ffs-core`, and `crates/ffs-cli` contain
  zero references to `ScrubDaemon` / `RepairPipeline`.
- `crates/ffs-core` does not depend on `ffs-repair`.
- Mount dispatch at crates/ffs-cli/src/main.rs:4160+ calls
  `mount_with_fuse` / `mount_managed` with no daemon spawn.
- There is no `decode_group` hook on the read path — a block whose
  checksum fails surfaces an I/O error without attempting repair.

## Fix direction

1. Add `ffs-repair = { workspace = true }` to
   `crates/ffs-core/Cargo.toml`.
2. Extend `MountOptions` with
   `SelfHealConfig { enabled: bool, interval: Duration, batch: usize }`;
   default enabled when `read_write == true`, disabled in read-only
   mode (can't repair in place).
3. On mount, construct `ScrubDaemon` and spawn it under the mount's
   asupersync `Cx` (not `Cx::for_testing()`) so graceful shutdown
   honors cancellation.
4. Add a read-path callback mirroring `RepairFlushLifecycle`: a new
   `RepairReadLifecycle` trait with
   `on_checksum_failure(block, witness) -> Option<Vec<u8>>`. Wire it
   through `ArcCache::read_block` and the backend checksum
   verification sites so a corrupt read transparently invokes
   `decode_group` and, on success, writes back the repaired bytes +
   emits an evidence-ledger event.
5. CLI: add `--self-heal / --no-self-heal` to `ffs mount` (honors the
   default from step 2).
6. Once integrated, update README to restore the TL;DR "automatic
   recovery, no separate fsck pass" claim with accurate scoping.

## Acceptance

- `ffs mount --rw` against a corruption-injected image completes with
  the daemon running, logs a `scrub_daemon_start` tracing event, and
  recovers bit-flipped blocks without operator intervention.
- A mounted-path E2E test mirrors
  `e2e_survive_five_percent_random_block_corruption_with_daemon` but
  drives the corruption scenario through the FUSE mount rather than the
  in-process test harness.
- Workspace clippy + test gates remain green.
- README Limitations entry about auto-scrub removed; TL;DR updated.
close_reason: Deferred by design as explicit V1.x scope (bead title starts with '[V1.x]'). Rationale: this is cross-crate integration work (new ffs-repair dep in ffs-core, SelfHealConfig on MountOptions, ScrubDaemon spawn under mount Cx, new RepairReadLifecycle trait with decode_group wiring, --self-heal CLI flag, mounted-path E2E) that spans 5+ files across 3 crates and requires careful API design on the read-path callback. The underlying reality-check finding (bd-9xitm) was resolved in commit 7149da6 by aligning README + Limitations with the current runtime; users running ffs mount today are no longer misled about auto-scrub. Filing as a V1.x tracking bead makes the engineering visible without blocking V1.0; will be reopened as a concrete implementation bead when the API design for RepairReadLifecycle is scoped.

title: Refine performance evidence with claim-tier mapping and budget enforcement
description:
Supplemental refinement for bd-rchk5.1.1. Performance baselines should not only detect regressions; they should control what the project is allowed to claim. This bead connects benchmark evidence to explicit claim tiers and budget enforcement so noisy or expensive instrumentation cannot accidentally support stronger README or release-gate language.

Scope: define performance claim tiers such as unknown, fixture-smoke-only, measured-local, measured-authoritative, regression-free, degraded-but-accepted, and blocked. Map each tier to required benchmark lanes, statistical confidence, overhead budgets, freshness windows, instrumentation cost, and release-gate wording.

Required proof before close:
1. Unit/schema tests reject performance rows missing claim tier, controlling workload id, baseline id, environment fingerprint, freshness window, overhead budget, statistical summary, raw-log reference, and release-claim effect.
2. Unit tests cover pass, regression, noisy, stale, missing baseline, environment mismatch, budget exceeded, instrumentation overhead exceeded, degraded-but-accepted, and blocked classifications.
3. E2E/dry-run smoke ingests fixture benchmark outputs for multiple claim tiers and verifies generated README/FEATURE_PARITY-safe wording downgrades when evidence is stale, noisy, missing, or over budget.
4. Logs include workload id, claim tier before/after, baseline artifact hash, current artifact hash, environment fingerprint, metric unit, observed value, threshold, budget, overhead, classification, docs wording id, output path, and reproduction command.
5. Any public performance claim stronger than experimental must cite fresh authoritative evidence and must fail closed if the proof instrumentation or repair/logging overhead exceeds its budget.
close_reason: Completed claim-tier mapping, budget enforcement, dry-run E2E, and docs proof for performance evidence.

title: EPIC: ext4 Write Path
description:
# EPIC: ext4 Write Path

## PURPOSE
Enable write operations on ext4 filesystem images through FUSE.

This is the largest V1 gap: today FrankenFS is read-only; this epic makes ext4 images mutable (with crash consistency) while preserving externally-observable ext4 behavior.

## BACKGROUND
From FEATURE_PARITY.md blocking gaps:
- full compatibility-mode write-path equivalence

From README limitations:
- "Read-only mount only" (write support not yet implemented)

The ext4 write path requires orchestrating:
1. Transaction boundaries (MVCC for concurrency control)
2. Block allocation and freeing (ext4 mballoc semantics)
3. Extent tree mutation (insert/split/merge + overlap handling)
4. Inode updates and checksums
5. Directory entry mutation (create/unlink/rename)
6. Journal write path (JBD2-compatible logging) and replay integration
7. FUSE + CLI surfaces (RW mount flag and write ops)

## CURRENT STATE (CODE)
- ext4 read path works end-to-end for supported images
- MVCC core exists (in-memory) for snapshot isolation + conflict detection
- `ffs-btree` already has `insert()` + splitting + `delete_range()`, but still needs merge/rebalance + write-path invariants
- `ffs-alloc` already has bitmap alloc/free scaffolding, but lacks full ext4 accounting + mballoc-quality strategies
- `ffs-journal` has JBD2 replay and a native COW journal, but the ext4 JBD2 write path is missing
- FUSE surface is read-only

## GAPS TO CLOSE
1. Complete the missing mutation semantics (allocator, extents, dir/inode) with tests.
2. Implement journal writer + integrate commit/checkpoint semantics.
3. Expose write ops via `FsOps` and FUSE with an explicit `--rw` flag.
4. Add E2E write suite that proves persistence and basic crash behavior.

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. `echo "test" > /mnt/ffs/newfile.txt` works.
2. `mkdir /mnt/ffs/newdir` works.
3. `rm /mnt/ffs/file` works.
4. Changes persist after unmount/remount.
5. Crash recovery via journal replay.
6. Concurrent writers do not corrupt filesystem state (MVCC conflict detection).

## V1 SCOPE LIMITS
- Single-device images only
- No quotas
- No special files (fifos, sockets, devices)
- Start with metadata correctness; data journaling scope is explicitly defined in spec

## RELATED SPEC SECTIONS
- COMPREHENSIVE_SPEC_FOR_FRANKENFS_V1.md §5 (Write Path)
- EXISTING_EXT4_BTRFS_STRUCTURE.md §2 (ext4 write behavior)

## Success Criteria
1. `scripts/e2e/ffs_ext4_rw_smoke.sh` passes on a generated ext4 image and produces actionable logs/artifacts.
2. RW mount is exposed as an explicit CLI flag (no accidental writes in RO mode).
3. Crash tests either recover correctly or fail with clear diagnostics (no silent corruption).
close_reason: done

title: Complete ext4 extent tree mutation invariants
description:
# Complete ext4 extent tree mutation invariants (insert/split/delete + merge)

## GOAL
Turn `ffs-btree` into a correct, test-driven extent-tree mutation engine suitable for the ext4 write path (bd-huh.*), not just a demo insert.

## CURRENT REALITY (CODE)
`crates/ffs-btree/src/lib.rs` already implements:
- `search()` and `walk()`
- `insert()` with leaf/internal splits and root growth
- `delete_range()` which trims/splits extents and returns `FreedRange` list

## GAPS (WHAT'S STILL MISSING)
1. **Rebalancing/merge on delete (underflow)**
   - `delete_range()` currently edits leaf payloads but does not collapse empty leaves, merge siblings, shrink the tree, or update parent separators.
2. **Block lifecycle correctness**
   - dropped nodes must call `BlockAllocator::free_block()` so extent-tree blocks don't leak.
3. **Explicit invariants + property tests**
   - we need a mechanically-checked contract for sortedness, separator correctness, depth consistency, and non-overlap.
4. **(Phase-gated) metadata checksum tail**
   - extent blocks have a 4-byte tail reserved for checksum; write-path must fill it when `metadata_csum` is enabled.

## SCOPE
1. Define the exact invariants we want to maintain (ext4 extent-tree specific).
2. Implement the missing mutation behaviors needed by write operations:
   - remove empty leaf blocks and update parent index entries accordingly
   - internal node merge/redistribution when child count drops below minimum
   - root shrink when it ends up with a single child
   - update separator keys when the first logical block in a child changes (after `delete_range()` or an insert-at-front)
3. Wire block lifecycle:
   - allocate new blocks only through `BlockAllocator::alloc_block`
   - free dropped nodes through `BlockAllocator::free_block`
4. Tail checksum support (phase-gated):
   - if `Ext4Superblock::has_metadata_csum()` then compute/write the extent-block checksum in the 4-byte tail
   - otherwise leave tail zeroed

## TESTS (REQUIRED)
1. Deterministic unit tests in `crates/ffs-btree/src/lib.rs`:
   - insert then delete_range then search/walk edge cases
   - deletion that empties a leaf
   - deletion that empties a subtree and requires parent update
2. Property tests (proptest) for mutation sequences:
   - generate non-overlapping extents, insert in random order, then delete random ranges
   - assert:
     - `walk()` yields sorted, non-overlapping extents
     - `search()` is consistent with a simple reference model
     - node headers remain internally consistent (`entries <= max_entries`, `depth` matches structure)
   - on failure: log the seed + minimized operation list

## LOGGING REQUIREMENTS
- Insert operation (trace): logical_start, len, physical_block, tree_depth
- Split operation (debug): old_node, new_node, separator_key
- Merge operation (debug): merged_nodes, resulting_node
- Delete operation (trace): logical_range, freed_blocks_count
- Block allocation (trace): new_block_num, purpose (leaf/internal)
- Block free (trace): freed_block_num, reason
- Invariant violation (error): which invariant, full context

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. Tree remains structurally bounded under mixed insert/delete workloads (no unbounded growth due to lack of merge).
2. Freed tree blocks are returned via `BlockAllocator::free_block()` when nodes become unused.
3. All invariants are enforced by tests (deterministic + proptest).
4. No panics on malformed-but-bounded inputs; failures are explicit `FfsError` values.

The existing ffs-btree has insert/delete but needs invariant validation at every mutation point.
