title: Refine bd-rchk0.3.2 mounted write matrix with data-safety scenarios
description:
Supplemental plan-space refinement for bd-rchk0.3.2. The existing mounted write matrix bead is directionally correct, but users need it to prove data-safety boundaries, not only that common operations return expected status codes. This child bead should be completed as part of the mounted write matrix work or used to revise that matrix once the br dotted-ID mutation bug is fixed.

Required proof before close:
1. Unit/schema tests validate the mounted write scenario manifest, including stable scenario IDs, filesystem flavor, image fixture hash, mount flags, operation sequence, expected survivor set, expected error class, required cleanup, and whether the scenario is positive, refusal, crash/reopen, or no-partial-mutation proof.
2. The manifest covers ext4 and btrfs create, mkdir, unlink/rmdir, rename, write/readback, setattr, link/symlink, xattr create/replace/list/get, supported fallocate modes, unsupported fallocate modes, read-only rejection, rw repair rejection before serialization, and host capability skips.
3. E2E scripts run through the mounted CLI/FUSE path in the permissioned lane and emit shared QA artifacts for every scenario, including stdout/stderr paths, mount logs, image before/after hashes where relevant, cleanup status, and exact reproduction command.
4. Negative cases assert no partial mutation by checking file size, link count, directory entries, xattrs, and reopen state after the rejected operation.
5. Logs include operation_id, scenario_id, flavor, mount_options, expected_outcome, actual_outcome, error_class, remediation_id or follow-up bead, artifact paths, and cleanup status.
6. Any scenario that is too expensive or environment-dependent becomes a narrow long-campaign or host-lane bead instead of being omitted from the matrix.
close_reason: Completed: mounted write matrix now covers xattr create/replace/list/get, fallocate zero/punch, read-only EROFS, repair pre-serialization refusal, and host capability skip proof artifacts

title: Exercise crash, unmount, and reopen recovery for mounted writes
description: Prove mounted write behavior across lifecycle edges. Exercise clean unmount, forced unmount if safely possible in the lane, process termination during staged writes, reopen after writes, fsync/fsyncdir boundaries, and journal/MVCC recovery behavior for supported paths.
design: Depends on the mounted write workload matrix. This is essential before relaxing experimental caveats.
acceptance_criteria: Acceptance: deterministic or controlled integration tests capture pre-crash operations, crash/unmount point, recovery command, expected surviving data/metadata, actual state, and logs. Unit tests cover recovery classifier helpers, safe process-control boundaries, and artifact validation. E2E scripts cover clean unmount, forced unmount if safely available, process termination during staged writes, fsync/fsyncdir boundaries, reopen, and cleanup, with shared QA artifacts and failure classification as product, harness, host limitation, or unsupported V1 scope.
notes: Future self: do not use destructive host commands; use safe harness-level process control and temporary images only. Preserve partial artifacts if the process dies so users can inspect the last known operation.
close_reason: Added mounted recovery matrix validator, lifecycle fixture, E2E artifact smoke, safe process-control checks, and docs/catalog coverage for crash/unmount/reopen recovery evidence.

dependencies.metadata: {"why":"recovery tests require the mounted write workload matrix"}
{"why":"mounted recovery tests should preserve structured crash/reopen artifacts"}

title: Expose actionable error classes and evidence for mounted writes
description: Audit mounted write failures from the workload and recovery matrices and ensure operators get actionable errors rather than opaque EIO/EINVAL where a better class exists. Evidence ledger or structured logs should include operation ID, scenario ID, outcome, error class, and artifact references for unsupported and failed paths.
design: Depends on SLOs and workload evidence so the audit is grounded in real paths.
acceptance_criteria: Acceptance: error mapping and evidence/log fields are documented and tested for representative rw failures, unsupported operations, repair interactions, cancellation, permission errors, stale snapshots, and host capability skips. Unit tests cover mapping tables, broad EIO fallback justifications, and log redaction. E2E or scenario scripts prove representative mounted failures emit actionable shared QA artifacts with operation ID, scenario ID, expected error class, actual error class, remediation hint, stdout/stderr paths, and linked follow-up bead for every unresolved broad fallback.
notes: Future self: this is not cosmetic; precise errors are how future agents and users distinguish product bugs from unsupported scope or host setup. Do not collapse DefaultPermissions EACCES or FUSE capability skips into generic filesystem corruption.

dependencies.metadata: {"why":"error audit should use the readiness taxonomy"}

title: Standardize operational QA scripts, artifact schema, and detailed logging
description: The current bridge graph correctly names many unit, integration, mounted, xfstests, fuzz, and performance gates, but those gates should not each invent their own logging format or pass/fail artifact vocabulary. This workstream defines the common QA contract that makes the later work useful to users and future agents: one consistent scenario ID model, structured JSON/JSONL artifacts, detailed logs, cleanup behavior, skip classification, and explicit unit plus E2E script expectations for every operational readiness bead.
design: This is a foundation layer, not a replacement for domain-specific tests. It should make all later work more consistent and more useful to operators without reducing any feature scope.
acceptance_criteria: Acceptance: every implementation or validation bead in the reality-check bridge can point to a common artifact/logging contract; future E2E scripts produce machine-readable summaries and human-debuggable logs; unit tests cover the artifact validators and failure classifiers; skip/pass/fail/error states are distinguishable; no operational readiness parent closes unless its leaves cite unit-test evidence, E2E script evidence where applicable, and artifact/log paths.
notes: Future self: build this before one-off scripts proliferate. It should improve the user experience by making failures explain themselves instead of requiring archaeology through raw stdout.

title: Define the operational artifact schema and logging vocabulary
description: Specify the exact fields every operational E2E or long-running validation script must emit. The schema should cover run identity, bead ID, scenario ID, git SHA, command line, host/worker, kernel, toolchain, FUSE capability result, filesystem flavor, image hash, mount options, expected outcome, actual outcome, exit status, duration, stdout/stderr paths, ledger paths, checksums, cleanup status, pass/fail/skip classification, error class, remediation hint, and artifact references.
design: This is intentionally detailed: the point is that future users should understand what failed without reading agent transcripts.
acceptance_criteria: Acceptance: a checked-in design/doc section and machine-checkable schema or Rust data model define required versus optional fields, severity/classification vocabulary, redaction rules, examples for pass/fail/skip/error, and validation commands. Unit tests or schema tests must reject missing required fields, invalid classification values, malformed artifact refs, ambiguous skip reasons, missing log paths, and missing cleanup status. Include sample artifacts for xfstests, FUSE capability probes, mounted scenario runs, fuzz smoke, performance runs, and writeback-cache crash tests.
notes: Future self: include known FrankenFS pitfalls in the vocabulary: FUSE permission skip, root-owned btrfs testdir EACCES, unsupported V1 scope, product failure, harness bug, worker dependency missing, stale tracker/tooling failure, and unsafe cleanup failure. The schema must be useful to users reading artifacts without agent transcripts.
close_reason: Implemented operational artifact schema and logging vocabulary with strict validation, docs, E2E contract checks, and tests

title: Build the shared E2E runner harness with unit-tested artifact validation
description: Implement the reusable script/test harness that domain beads can call instead of hand-rolling ad hoc shell. It should create temporary workspaces/images safely, run commands with detailed logging, trap cleanup, collect stdout/stderr, capture environment metadata, validate artifacts against the schema, and emit a final JSON summary suitable for CI/RCH and local runs.
design: This should be small and composable. Domain-specific scenarios still live in their own beads, but they inherit consistent logging and cleanup.
acceptance_criteria: Acceptance: checked-in E2E runner script(s) or Rust harness entrypoints exist; unit tests cover artifact validation, classification mapping, cleanup-on-failure behavior, timeout handling, missing capability handling, log path generation, command redaction, and partial-artifact preservation. Smoke scenarios prove the runner emits valid pass, fail, skip, and error records with stdout/stderr paths, environment metadata, duration, cleanup status, and artifact checksums. Scripts must avoid destructive host operations and use temporary images/directories.
notes: Future self: prefer Rust validation for schema correctness and shell only for orchestration. Use set -euo pipefail in shell scripts, include trap-based cleanup, and make every runner failure preserve enough logs for a user to diagnose without rerunning immediately.
close_reason: Implemented shared operational E2E runner helpers, manifest validator CLI, shell validation hook, docs, and tests

dependencies.metadata: {"why":"shared E2E runner must validate the agreed artifact schema"}

title: Create a one-command operational readiness report aggregator
description: After individual scripts emit common artifacts, users need a single report that answers whether FrankenFS is working for real in the tested environment. Build or specify a report command that reads the E2E artifacts, groups by workstream and scenario, summarizes pass/fail/skip/error counts, highlights product failures versus environment blockers, and links logs/artifacts.
design: This improves users directly: the final state should be inspectable without opening dozens of logs.
acceptance_criteria: Acceptance: a command or documented script aggregates artifacts from xfstests, FUSE lane, mounted scenario matrix, repair policy, writeback-cache, fuzz smoke, and performance runs; report output is both human-readable and JSON; unit tests cover aggregation of mixed pass/fail/skip/error inputs, missing logs, duplicate scenario IDs, stale git SHAs, and environment-only blockers; an E2E smoke script aggregates a small fixture directory and preserves links to raw logs/artifacts. Docs show how to attach the report to bead closure.
notes: Future self: do not block first implementation slices on a fancy dashboard; reliable JSON plus Markdown/text is sufficient. The report should answer what works for real, what skipped because of host capability, what failed as product behavior, and which bead owns each unresolved row.
close_reason: Completed in commit 93b2f90: added ffs-harness operational-readiness-report JSON/Markdown aggregator, readiness module tests, E2E smoke script, and E2E docs. Evidence: cargo fmt --check --package ffs-harness; git diff --check; cargo test -p ffs-harness operational_readiness_report; cargo check --workspace --all-targets; scripts/e2e/ffs_operational_readiness_report_e2e.sh; ubs changed readiness files. br close/update returned ISSUE_NOT_FOUND despite br show --no-db finding the bead, so this exact JSONL record was closed directly.

dependencies.metadata: {"why":"readiness report aggregates artifacts emitted by the shared runner"}

title: Ambition round: make FrankenFS prove trust, not just pass parity
description:
Reality-check Phase 4 ambition workstream. The existing bd-rchk0 bridge closes concrete runtime caveats from the README. This workstream deliberately raises the bar beyond parity closure: FrankenFS should generate executable evidence that users can trust mounted writes, repair, crash recovery, writeback-cache policy, and release claims in hostile real-world conditions.

This epic must not dilute or replace bd-rchk0. It overlays higher-grade proof systems and user-facing confidence artifacts on top of the operational repair/writeback/FUSE/conformance work already planned. The output should be implementation-ready beads, not prose aspirations.
design:
Design intent:
- Treat README caveats as the floor, not the ceiling. The ambition layer asks what evidence would make a careful operator believe FrankenFS is safe to mount read-write, recover, repair, and eventually enable writeback-cache.
- Prefer executable artifacts over narrative confidence: invariant models, differential oracles, fault-injection labs, chaos/replay schedules, and proof bundles.
- Keep current operational beads authoritative for concrete runtime fixes. Ambition beads should consume their artifacts, raise proof quality, and open narrower fix beads when they expose failures.
- Make every generated artifact durable and inspectable by future agents without needing this conversation.
acceptance_criteria:
Epic can close only when all child ambition beads exist and each child has:
1. Clear background and user value.
2. Explicit scope and non-scope.
3. Unit-test requirements.
4. E2E or mounted-path script requirements where applicable.
5. Logging/artifact requirements aligned with bd-rchk0.4.1 through bd-rchk0.4.3.
6. Dependency links to the existing operational proof beads it relies on.
7. Documentation and FEATURE_PARITY/README update requirements for any changed public claim.
notes:
Reality-check skill Phase 4 asks for ambition rounds that push beyond incremental checklist closure. For this repo, the high-leverage ambition is not another isolated feature: it is a trust system around correctness, recovery, and operational proof. Do not close this epic because child beads were merely created; close it only after the ambition workstream has shipped usable evidence.

Execution order intent:
1. bd-rchk0.4.1 defines the common artifact schema and logging vocabulary.
2. bd-rchk0.4.2 provides the shared E2E runner.
3. bd-rchk0.5.7 curates reusable workloads once schema/runner are available.
4. bd-rchk0.5.1 builds the invariant oracle.
5. bd-rchk0.5.2 and bd-rchk0.5.3 add mounted differential and repair-confidence proof.
6. bd-rchk0.5.5 adds crash/replay using the model and runner.
7. bd-rchk0.5.4 bundles evidence.
8. bd-rchk0.5.6 sets release gates and kill-switch criteria from the bundle.

Tooling note for future self: br dep add in this repo currently trips stale SQLite/import behavior for dotted child IDs; the persisted graph has parent-child edges, while this note records the intended cross-workstream order until tracker repair lands.

title: Build an executable filesystem consistency model and invariant oracle
description:
Create an executable model for the correctness properties FrankenFS must preserve across core operations. This is the ambition-layer oracle for risky logic: inode state, directory entries, link counts, extents, MVCC snapshots, journal commit/replay, repair writeback, and FUSE-visible outcomes.

The model should be small and intentionally abstract. It is not a second filesystem implementation. Its purpose is to catch impossible states and compare observed operations against invariants that matter to users: no lost committed write, no stale directory alias after rename/unlink, no overlapping live extents, no journal replay mutation after the first successful replay, and no repair path that bypasses serialization rules.
design:
Implementation shape:
- Define a compact state-machine model in the most appropriate existing test/support crate, avoiding a production dependency unless it earns its place.
- Model only externally meaningful effects: files, dirs, xattrs if already in scope, extent ownership, transaction boundaries, snapshots, repair attempts, and replay.
- Provide adapters from existing operation traces or harness events into model operations.
- Make failures diagnostic: every invariant violation should report operation index, seed/scenario id, affected inode/path/extent, expected state, observed state, and suggested owning subsystem.
- Keep the model deterministic and seedable so failures can be replayed locally and through remote RCH lanes.
acceptance_criteria:
Required proof before close:
1. Unit tests cover empty filesystem, single file lifecycle, nested directories, rename/unlink edge cases, extent split/merge ownership, snapshot visibility, journal replay idempotence, and repair writeback serialization.
2. Property or metamorphic tests drive random legal operation sequences through the model and at least one real FrankenFS core or harness surface.
3. The model emits structured artifacts compatible with the bd-rchk0.4.1 logging vocabulary and can be consumed by the bd-rchk0.4.2/0.4.3 proof runner path.
4. At least one existing risky regression or documented caveat is encoded as an invariant test.
5. README, FEATURE_PARITY, or the canonical spec is updated only for claims that are now executable and measured.
notes: Root cause this bead targets: parity fixtures can pass while cross-subsystem invariants are still untested. The model should give future repair, writeback-cache, and crash-consistency work a shared oracle instead of each test inventing local assertions.

title: Refine invariant oracle with shrinkable traces and false-positive tests
description:
Plan-space polish for bd-rchk0.5.1 after fresh review. The invariant oracle bead is conceptually right, but implementation must be constrained so it becomes a usable proof tool rather than a parallel toy filesystem.

Scope: define replayable operation traces, deterministic seed handling, shrinking/minimization for failing traces where practical, oracle self-tests, and structured failure artifacts. This sub-bead does not replace bd-rchk0.5.1; it sharpens the acceptance contract before implementation.
design:
Implementation refinement:
- Define a trace schema with operation id, seed, precondition, expected model delta, observed subsystem event, and artifact references.
- Include deterministic replay and minimal-failure reproduction commands in every failure report.
- Add model self-tests that intentionally feed impossible states and valid edge cases so the oracle is not silently overfitted.
- Keep the oracle abstract: model externally meaningful state and invariants, not every production data structure.
- Make output compatible with the bd-rchk0.4.1 artifact vocabulary and bd-rchk0.4.2 runner path.
acceptance_criteria:
Required proof before close:
1. Unit tests cover trace schema parsing, deterministic replay, false-positive guard cases, expected invariant failures, shrink/minimization behavior where implemented, and structured report rendering.
2. E2E or integration smoke drives at least one real FrankenFS operation sequence into the model and emits a valid artifact with seed, trace id, operation index, violated invariant, expected state, observed state, and reproduction command.
3. Logs distinguish model bug, production bug, unsupported operation, and harness bug.
4. A future implementer can use this sub-bead as a checklist for bd-rchk0.5.1 without consulting the original planning discussion.
notes: Why this matters for users: invariant evidence is only useful if failures are reproducible and diagnosable. The user should eventually see a precise proof artifact, not an opaque property-test panic.
close_reason: Implemented replayable invariant-oracle trace schema, deterministic replay hashing, shrinking/minimization reports, false-positive and expected-failure tests, CLI validation, E2E suite, docs, and scenario catalog. Evidence: cargo fmt --check; bash -n scripts/e2e/ffs_invariant_oracle_e2e.sh; python3 -m json.tool scripts/e2e/scenario_catalog.json; git diff --check; ffs_invariant_oracle E2E 6/6 at artifacts/e2e/20260503_095823_ffs_invariant_oracle/result.json; rch cargo check --workspace --all-targets; rch cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings; UBS changed files exit 0 with 0 criticals.

title: Create ambition evidence matrix and dependency gate map
description:
Fresh review gap: br dependency edges for several dotted child IDs are currently unreliable in this checkout, and the ambition workstream depends on cross-cutting prerequisites such as artifact schema, E2E runner, FUSE lane, corruption corpus, proof bundle, and release gates.

Scope: create a checked-in evidence matrix or data-backed plan artifact that maps every ambition bead to user risks, required prerequisites, proof artifacts, unit tests, E2E scripts, logging fields, and downstream consumers. This makes the intended dependency structure explicit even when tracker graph edges are temporarily incomplete.
design:
Implementation shape:
- Represent the matrix as data plus generated docs where practical, not just prose.
- Columns should include bead id, user risk, prerequisites, owning proof lane, unit-test requirements, E2E script requirements, logging fields, artifacts emitted, capability skips, feature gates consumed, and downstream release claim.
- Include all bd-rchk0.5 children and the key operational prerequisites: bd-rchk0.4.1, bd-rchk0.4.2, bd-rchk0.4.3, bd-rchk4.2, bd-rchk4.3, bd-rchk7.2, bd-rchk0.1.3, bd-rchk0.2.3, and bd-rchk0.3.3.
- Validate the matrix so missing test/log/artifact fields fail.
- Use it to guide future dependency-edge repair once br handles this graph shape safely.
acceptance_criteria:
Required proof before close:
1. Unit tests or schema tests reject missing user-risk mapping, missing unit-test contract, missing E2E contract, missing logging fields, missing artifact consumers, and stale bead references.
2. E2E or script smoke renders the matrix into a readable report and verifies all referenced beads exist via br.
3. Matrix explicitly records current tracker limitation around dotted child dependency edges and lists the intended cross-workstream dependency order.
4. Proof bundle and release-gate beads can consume or reference the matrix.
5. README or project docs tell future agents to consult this matrix before claiming an ambition bead.
notes: Why this improves the system for users: it prevents ambitious proof work from fragmenting. Every test and artifact remains tied to a user-visible risk and release claim.
close_reason: Implemented executable ambition evidence matrix parent gate with dependency gate map, operational prerequisite validation, E2E smoke, docs pointer, and RCH check/clippy evidence.

title: Refine ambition evidence matrix for safety remediation demos and budgets
description:
Plan-space polish for bd-rchk0.5.10 after the second fresh review. The matrix bead already requires every ambition bead to map to user risk, tests, artifacts, logs, and release claims. The added ambition beads expand that matrix scope: hostile-image safety, remediation, low-privilege proof demos, and overhead budgets must be first-class fields rather than optional notes.

Scope: update the eventual evidence matrix design so it cannot omit threat-model coverage, user remediation, proof-demo status, or cost/budget evidence when future implementation starts.
design:
Implementation refinement:
- Add matrix fields for threat class, expected safe behavior, hostile-artifact handling, remediation id, demo profile, proof-demo status, budget profile, measured overhead, and exception expiry.
- Require every ambition bead to declare whether these fields are applicable, not applicable, blocked, or intentionally deferred, with a reason and owning follow-up when blocked.
- Cross-check matrix rows against bd-rchk0.5.11 through bd-rchk0.5.14 so security, remediation, demo, and budget requirements are not lost during implementation.
- Preserve the original bd-rchk0.5.10 goal of data-backed docs and stale-bead reference validation.
- Keep the matrix as an implementation control plane, not a standalone prose report.
acceptance_criteria:
Required proof before close:
1. Unit/schema tests reject matrix rows missing threat-model status, remediation mapping, demo/low-privilege proof status, overhead-budget status, or explicit non-applicability rationale.
2. E2E/script smoke renders a report that groups ambition beads by user risk, security coverage, remediation coverage, demo coverage, budget status, and release-gate consumer.
3. Validation verifies that all bd-rchk0.5.11 through bd-rchk0.5.14 references exist and that their outputs are represented in the matrix.
4. Logs include matrix version, source bead ids, stale-reference checks, missing-field diagnostics, generated artifact paths, and reproduction command.
5. Future implementation beads must be able to consume the matrix without re-reading this planning conversation.
notes: Why this improves the system for users: the second review found that trust proof needs safety, remediation, demonstrability, and cost controls. This refinement keeps those dimensions attached to the evidence matrix so the implementation phase cannot accidentally optimize them away.
close_reason: Added required output coverage proof to ambition evidence matrix.

title: Add adversarial image security and safety threat model for ambition gates
description:
Second fresh-review gap: the ambition proof workstream validates correctness and repair, but users also need assurance that hostile or malformed filesystem images, repair ledgers, and proof artifacts cannot trick FrankenFS into unsafe host behavior or misleading readiness claims.

Scope: define an adversarial-image and hostile-artifact threat model for ext4/btrfs parsing, FUSE mount boundaries, repair metadata, proof-bundle ingestion, artifact paths, resource exhaustion, and operator commands. This is not a replacement for fuzzing; it is the security/safety contract that tells fuzzing, E2E scripts, release gates, and docs what must be defended.
design:
Implementation shape:
- Define a versioned threat model covering hostile ext4/btrfs images, malformed superblocks, cyclic or oversized metadata, truncated repair ledgers, tampered proof bundles, artifact path traversal attempts, unsupported mount options, resource-exhaustion corpus bombs, and unsafe operator-command combinations.
- Map every threat class to an expected safe behavior: reject before mount, classify as unsupported, degrade to read-only/detection-only, cap resources, refuse mutation, or preserve evidence for follow-up.
- Treat host boundaries as first-class: no host path traversal, no ambient mutation outside temporary workspaces, no proof-bundle ingestion that follows untrusted symlinks, and no confusing success status for quarantined artifacts.
- Connect the threat model to fuzzing, E2E scripts, proof-bundle validation, release gates, operator recovery drills, and README/FEATURE_PARITY wording.
- Keep this security contract small enough to execute in CI smoke while leaving hooks for longer fuzz and hostile-image campaigns.
acceptance_criteria:
Required proof before close:
1. Unit tests cover threat-model schema validation, hostile artifact path canonicalization, symlink/path traversal refusal, redaction behavior, resource-cap classification, unsafe host-operation rejection, and generated security-status wording.
2. E2E/security smoke scripts exercise at least one malformed image, one hostile proof bundle or artifact path, one missing host capability, one resource-cap case, and one repair-ledger tamper case.
3. Logs include threat scenario id, input hash, parser/mount/repair capability, expected safe behavior, observed classification, resource limits, cleanup status, and reproduction command.
4. Release gates fail closed for unreviewed critical threat classes and cannot promote hostile-image readiness from docs alone.
5. README/operator docs distinguish hostile-image safety, ordinary corruption repair, unsupported formats, detection-only behavior, and mutating repair readiness.
notes: Why this improves the system for users: correctness proof is incomplete if a malicious or malformed image can escape the declared sandbox, exhaust the host, or produce a misleading readiness bundle. This bead makes the safety envelope explicit before implementation work spreads across fuzzing, mount, repair, and release gates.
close_reason: Completed: added executable adversarial-image threat model, validator, CLI, E2E smoke, docs, and fail-closed security wording

dependencies.metadata: {"why":"threat-model implementation should consume the refined evidence-matrix fields for security coverage, remediation, demo status, and budget status"}

title: Create user remediation catalog for proof failures and readiness blockers
description:
Fresh review gap: the ambition plan requires many proof artifacts and gate decisions, but users need a deterministic answer when something fails, skips, or remains unsupported. A proof bundle that says fail or skip is only useful if it also tells the operator what happened, whether data is safe, and what action is next.

Scope: build a remediation catalog that maps every readiness outcome, proof-lane failure, host capability skip, security refusal, repair confidence state, stale artifact, and unsupported feature state to a user-facing explanation, owning bead or subsystem, reproduction command, escalation path, and documentation target.
design:
Implementation shape:
- Define a versioned remediation table keyed by proof lane, feature state, error class, threat class, host capability, repair confidence state, and artifact freshness state.
- Each entry must include user-facing summary, technical cause, data-safety implication, immediate operator action, reproduction command, required artifact links, owning bead/subsystem, escalation path, and docs target.
- Generate both machine-readable JSON and human-readable CLI/TUI/markdown output from the same catalog.
- Keep product failures, host skips, unsupported features, stale evidence, and intentional security refusals separate so release gates and docs cannot blur them.
- Make missing remediation entries fail validation before proof bundles or release-gate reports are published.
acceptance_criteria:
Required proof before close:
1. Unit tests cover classification lookup, missing remediation rejection, stale bead/subsystem references, generated wording, docs-link validation, artifact-link validation, and no-actionable-next-step failures.
2. E2E smoke feeds sample artifacts for product failure, host capability skip, unsupported operation, stale bundle, security refusal, unsafe repair refusal, and passing-but-experimental status, then verifies both JSON and human report output.
3. Logs include outcome code, remediation id, source proof lane, source artifact hash, data-safety classification, recommended command, owner/bead, and docs target.
4. Release gates consume the catalog so every fail/skip/warn state includes a concrete next action or explicit non-goal rationale.
5. README/operator docs explain how to interpret remediation output without requiring knowledge of this plan.
notes: Why this improves the system for users: evidence without a next action leaves operators stuck. This catalog turns proof outcomes into supportable, auditable user guidance and prevents vague readiness failures from being normalized.

dependencies.metadata: {"why":"remediation catalog must use the refined matrix fields so failure guidance maps to user risk and proof consumers"}

title: Build low-privilege local trust demo and sample proof bundle
description:
Fresh review gap: several ambition beads require mounted/FUSE or privileged host lanes, but many users and CI agents will first evaluate FrankenFS without mount permissions. The plan needs a low-privilege trust path that still proves real behavior honestly instead of hiding behind skipped FUSE lanes.

Scope: create a deterministic local demo/proof-bundle path that runs without FUSE capability where possible, exercises core parsing/oracle/repair dry-run behavior, emits a sample proof bundle, and clearly marks mounted lanes as skipped, unavailable, or separately permissioned rather than silently absent.
design:
Implementation shape:
- Provide a single command or script path that runs in an unprivileged workspace and produces a small sample proof bundle from deterministic fixtures.
- Exercise real code paths where available: image inspection, core invariant oracle, repair dry-run or detection-only scrub, artifact-schema validation, remediation catalog output, and release-gate classification.
- If FUSE or mount capability is unavailable, record mounted lanes as structured host skips with exact capability checks and instructions for a permissioned rerun.
- Include an optional permissioned lane that extends the same demo to a mounted smoke test without changing the artifact schema.
- Keep outputs small, reproducible, and suitable for README quickstart, CI smoke, and support tickets.
acceptance_criteria:
Required proof before close:
1. Unit tests validate demo manifest parsing, fixture selection, capability probing, skip classification, proof-bundle field population, cleanup guarantees, and remediation/gate wiring.
2. E2E script runs on a low-privilege host, produces a sample proof bundle, validates it from disk, prints a concise human summary, and preserves detailed logs.
3. Optional permissioned E2E lane reuses the same manifest and records mounted-path pass/fail/skip without weakening the low-privilege result.
4. Logs include git SHA, toolchain, kernel, command line, working directory policy, fixture hashes, capability checks, proof lanes executed, skipped lanes, cleanup status, and reproduction command.
5. README provides a real command users can run locally and accurately describes what the demo proves, what it does not prove, and where to find the full proof bundle.
notes: Why this improves the system for users: a trust story that only works on a privileged FUSE host is too fragile. This bead gives users and CI an honest low-friction first proof path while preserving clear separation from permissioned mounted readiness.

dependencies.metadata: {"why":"low-privilege proof demo should report matrix-backed demo coverage, skipped lanes, remediation IDs, and budget profile"}

title: Set overhead budgets for proof instrumentation repair and logging
description:
Fresh review gap: the ambition plan adds proof bundles, detailed logging, repair labs, crash/replay schedules, and long-running campaigns. Those systems can make FrankenFS safer, but they can also make normal workflows too slow, too noisy, or too storage-heavy if overhead is not measured and bounded.

Scope: define and enforce overhead budgets for proof instrumentation, artifact capture, logging volume, repair-symbol generation/validation, differential oracle runs, crash/replay traces, and soak/canary campaigns. The goal is not to weaken evidence; it is to make the evidence system practical and predictable for users and CI.
design:
Implementation shape:
- Define budget categories: runtime overhead, memory overhead, artifact disk usage, log volume, repair-symbol storage, network/RCH upload size, campaign duration, and operator report size.
- Establish separate budgets for developer smoke, CI required, nightly/stress, permissioned mount, and release-readiness profiles.
- Build a metrics parser/evaluator that consumes proof-bundle and campaign artifacts, compares them against budgets, and emits fail/warn/pass decisions for release gates.
- Preserve detailed logs by sampling, compression, manifests, and retention policy rather than by dropping diagnostic fields needed for reproduction.
- Require every budget exception to include reason, user impact, expiry/review date, and follow-up bead.
acceptance_criteria:
Required proof before close:
1. Unit tests cover budget schema parsing, metric extraction, pass/warn/fail thresholds, missing metrics, stale baselines, exception handling, retention policy, and human-summary generation.
2. E2E smoke runs a bounded proof workflow, captures timing/memory/artifact/log metrics, evaluates them against a sample budget, and writes detailed logs.
3. Logs include scenario id, profile, baseline id, observed value, budget value, unit, threshold decision, artifact sizes, compression/retention decision, and reproduction command.
4. Release gates consume budget results so expensive proof lanes can be required without making normal user workflows unpredictable.
5. README or harness docs explain how to run budget checks locally, how to interpret failures, and how to update baselines without hiding regressions.
notes: Why this improves the system for users: stronger evidence is valuable only if users and CI can afford to run it. Budget gates keep the proof system operational, prevent accidental log/artifact explosions, and make performance cost visible before feature claims change.
close_reason: Added proof overhead budget evaluator, release-gate CLI, and E2E smoke.

dependencies.metadata: {"why":"overhead budget evaluator should expose the refined matrix budget fields and release-gate consumers"}
{"why":"overhead budgets must include security resource-exhaustion and hostile-artifact scenarios from the threat model"}

title: Add a differential mounted oracle against kernel ext4 and btrfs
description:
Build a mounted-path differential oracle that compares supported FrankenFS behavior with kernel ext4 and btrfs behavior for the V1 operation envelope. This should extend fixture conformance into real filesystem workflows: create, read, write, truncate, rename, unlink, mkdir/rmdir, metadata updates, fsync-like boundaries where supported, unmount/reopen, and declared error behavior.

The goal is not to claim full kernel equivalence. The goal is to make every supported scenario produce a normalized observation that can be compared, diffed, and archived. Unsupported or intentionally different behavior must be classified explicitly instead of silently skipped.
design:
Implementation shape:
- Define scenario manifests that run once against kernel ext4/btrfs and once against FrankenFS for declared-supported operations.
- Normalize observations into stable records: syscall/action, path, errno/result, bytes, metadata subset, ordering-sensitive events, mount capability status, and artifact paths.
- Keep kernel baselines explicit and refreshable; do not hide differences behind broad allowlists. Every allowlisted difference needs a reason, owning bead, and planned removal or explicit permanent exclusion.
- Handle worker infrastructure honestly: FUSE permission failures should be recorded as capability skips, not FrankenFS logic failures; btrfs image-root EACCES under DefaultPermissions should be diagnosed as image setup unless the scenario intentionally tests root-only behavior.
acceptance_criteria:
Required proof before close:
1. Unit tests cover observation normalization, errno normalization, difference classification, and baseline/FrankenFS diff rendering.
2. E2E scripts run at least one ext4 and one btrfs mounted scenario end-to-end when the worker has mount/FUSE capability, with detailed logs and artifacts.
3. Permission-denied FUSE setup and btrfs DefaultPermissions image-root ownership cases are classified separately from filesystem behavior failures.
4. Reports include scenario id, filesystem type, image seed, mount options, kernel baseline command, FrankenFS command, normalized observations, and explicit pass/diff/skip status.
5. Any newly discovered parity miss becomes a narrow follow-up bead linked from this one before closure.
notes: Root cause this bead targets: fixture-level conformance can miss VFS/mount behavior and permission-boundary mistakes. A differential mounted oracle makes supported behavior measurable while preserving honest skip semantics for host capability gaps.

title: Refine mounted differential oracle with strict allowlist and host-skip semantics
description:
Plan-space polish for bd-rchk0.5.2 after fresh review. The mounted differential oracle is high-value, but it must be strict about difference classification and honest about host capability skips.

Scope: make allowlists narrow and expiry-backed, classify FUSE permission and btrfs DefaultPermissions/image-root ownership separately from product behavior, and require normalized per-operation logs that users can inspect without rerunning the scenario.
design:
Implementation refinement:
- Define an allowlist record with scenario id, normalized field, expected difference, reason, owner bead, expiration or review condition, and removal plan.
- Normalize kernel and FrankenFS observations before comparison; raw stdout/stderr stays linked as artifacts.
- Treat FUSE mount permission failures, missing kernel tools, and btrfs root ownership under DefaultPermissions as environment/setup classifications unless the scenario intentionally targets those conditions.
- Use temporary images/mountpoints only and include cleanup status in every artifact.
- Keep kernel version, mount options, image hash, user id, and capability probe result in every run log.
acceptance_criteria:
Required proof before close:
1. Unit tests cover allowlist validation, expired allowlist rejection, errno/result normalization, host-skip classification, btrfs DefaultPermissions/image-root diagnosis, and diff report rendering.
2. E2E script runs at least one ext4 and one btrfs scenario when the lane is permissioned and emits pass/diff/skip/error records with detailed logs.
3. A simulated missing-FUSE or permission-denied setup produces a skip artifact, not a product failure.
4. A deliberate unsupported behavior produces an explicit unsupported classification with an owning bead or documented non-goal.
5. No broad string allowlists or silent scenario drops are accepted.
notes: Why this matters for users: differential testing is only trustworthy if differences are visible, narrow, and explainable. Hidden allowlists would make the proof bundle less useful than the current caveats.
close_reason: Completed: added mounted differential oracle report validation with narrow expiring allowlists, errno/result normalization, host-skip classes for FUSE permission and btrfs DefaultPermissions root-owned setup, unsupported-scope ownership checks, CLI validation, Markdown rendering, and dry-run E2E artifacts. Evidence: bash -n scripts/e2e/ffs_mounted_differential_oracle_e2e.sh; python3 -m json.tool scripts/e2e/scenario_catalog.json >/dev/null; cargo fmt --check; git diff --check; rch cargo test -p ffs-harness --lib mounted_differential_oracle -- --nocapture (6 passed); rch ./scripts/e2e/ffs_mounted_differential_oracle_e2e.sh (passed, artifacts/e2e/20260503_104759_ffs_mounted_differential_oracle/result.json); mounted differential catalog evidence check passed (6 scenarios); rch cargo check --workspace --all-targets; rch cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings; ubs changed files exit 0, 0 criticals. Note: full scenario catalog validation remains blocked by pre-existing ffs_benchmark_taxonomy evidence mismatch before this suite.

title: Create a quantitative self-healing repair confidence and fault-injection lab
description:
Turn self-healing repair from a qualitative claim into a quantitative evidence system. Define corruption and loss models, inject deterministic damage into supported images or block streams, run repair attempts, and report recovery probability, confidence, residual risk, and failure classification.

This bead should make repair behavior understandable to operators and future agents: what corruption classes are repaired, what classes are detected-only, how many symbols or redundancy budget are required, and what the system does when confidence is insufficient.
design:
Implementation shape:
- Start with an explicit fault model: bit flips, block erasure, reordered/stale blocks, truncated repair metadata, mismatched symbol sets, and adversarial but deterministic seed cases.
- Provide deterministic injectors that produce reproducible corrupt images or block traces and record seed, affected offsets, affected logical structure, and expected repair class.
- Compute and report repair confidence with a clear decision rule; where probabilistic claims are made, include confidence interval or conservative bound and enough sample metadata to reproduce.
- Keep detection-only, partial repair, clean repair, false positive, and unsafe-to-repair as separate outcomes.
- Feed failures into narrow follow-up beads instead of broad tuning.
acceptance_criteria:
Required proof before close:
1. Unit tests cover fault injector determinism, corruption manifest parsing, repair outcome classification, confidence math, and unsafe-to-repair decisions.
2. E2E or harness scripts run a minimal corpus across supported repair paths and emit detailed per-seed JSON plus a summary table.
3. Artifacts include original image/hash, corrupted image/hash or corruption manifest, repair-symbol budget, decoder settings, observed outcome, confidence value, logs, and reproduction command.
4. At least one adversarial negative case proves that the system refuses repair or remains detection-only when confidence is insufficient.
5. README/FEATURE_PARITY wording distinguishes measured automatic repair, detection-only scrub, and not-yet-supported corruption classes.
notes: Root cause this bead targets: repair promises are high-risk because users infer durability from them. A repair system needs quantified evidence and explicit failure taxonomy before public claims can become less experimental.

title: Refine repair confidence lab with mutation safety thresholds
description:
Plan-space polish for bd-rchk0.5.3 after fresh review. Quantitative repair evidence must include explicit mutation safety gates, not just recovery success counts.

Scope: define confidence thresholds, detection-only outcomes, unsafe-to-repair outcomes, dry-run versus mutating repair behavior, and decision logs that explain why the system did or did not alter user data.
design:
Implementation refinement:
- Separate detect, dry-run, mutate, verify, rollback-needed, and refused outcomes.
- Define confidence math and conservative thresholds as data so release gates can consume them.
- Record repair-symbol budget, corruption class, evidence ledger state, candidate repair plan, pre/post hashes, and final verification verdict.
- Include adversarial negative cases where the right answer is no mutation.
- Make automatic repair writeback go through the same safety vocabulary used by mounted write and recovery beads.
acceptance_criteria:
Required proof before close:
1. Unit tests cover threshold evaluation, outcome classification, dry-run plan validation, refused-repair decisions, confidence summary rendering, and ledger/artifact integrity checks.
2. E2E or harness script includes detect-only, successful dry-run, successful mutating repair where supported, unsafe-to-repair, and failed-verification scenarios.
3. Logs include enough data for a user to answer: what was corrupt, what evidence existed, what repair was proposed, what changed, how it was verified, and how to reproduce the run.
4. Public docs distinguish automatic mutating repair from detection-only scrub and from unsupported corruption classes.
5. Any threshold chosen without evidence is marked experimental and mapped to a follow-up measurement bead.
notes: Why this matters for users: automatic repair can destroy trust if it mutates data without a visible confidence decision. The lab should prove both repair success and disciplined refusal.
close_reason: Completed repair confidence mutation-safety thresholds, validator, docs contract, and E2E smoke

title: Design an operator proof bundle for real-world readiness
description:
Produce a single portable readiness bundle that answers the operator question: can I trust this FrankenFS build for the declared support envelope? The bundle should gather mount capability, conformance, xfstests, mounted differential results, repair fault-lab results, performance baselines, writeback-cache policy status, scrub/repair status, and known caveats into one inspectable artifact set.

This is user-facing operational proof, not marketing. It must preserve uncomfortable facts: skipped lanes, unsupported features, failing scenarios, confidence limits, and the exact build/configuration tested.
design:
Implementation shape:
- Define a versioned bundle manifest and directory layout that can be produced by CI, local RCH, or a developer workstation.
- Include machine-readable JSON and human-readable markdown summaries; the markdown is generated from the JSON so claims cannot drift.
- Bundle should link to raw logs, normalized reports, scenario manifests, build metadata, git commit, cargo profile, mount environment, kernel version, and explicit caveat map.
- Redact host-specific secrets or paths where needed, but never redact information required to reproduce a result.
- Make missing required artifacts a hard failure in readiness mode and a clear warning in exploratory mode.
acceptance_criteria:
Required proof before close:
1. Unit tests validate manifest schema, artifact path integrity, required/optional lane classification, redaction rules, and markdown rendering from JSON.
2. E2E smoke script generates a bundle from sample or real artifacts and then re-validates it from disk without depending on ambient build state.
3. Bundle includes structured sections for conformance, xfstests, FUSE capability, mounted differential oracle, repair fault lab, performance baseline, writeback-cache status, scrub/repair status, and known deferrals.
4. README documents the command to generate and inspect the bundle, including how to interpret skip/fail/pass.
5. Any public readiness claim points to bundle fields instead of hand-written status prose.
notes: Root cause this bead targets: users need one durable evidence artifact, not scattered logs across cargo tests, harness outputs, and README caveats. The bundle becomes the accountability layer for future release claims.
close_reason: Completed: proof bundle now requires scrub/repair status and known-deferral lanes, README documents generation/inspection flow, and ffs-harness gates pass

title: Refine proof bundle with validator CLI and redaction guarantees
description:
Plan-space polish for bd-rchk0.5.4 after fresh review. The proof bundle should be a validated artifact, not a folder of logs assembled by convention.

Scope: require a manifest validator, generated human summary from machine-readable data, redaction rules that preserve reproducibility, and failure behavior for missing or stale evidence.
design:
Implementation refinement:
- Define a versioned manifest with required lanes, artifact paths, hashes, git SHA, toolchain, kernel, mount capability, scenario ids, and gate inputs.
- Provide a validator command that can run against a bundle on disk and return pass/fail with structured diagnostics.
- Generate markdown/text summaries from the JSON manifest so user-facing prose cannot drift from proof data.
- Redact host-specific secrets or disposable paths only when reproduction commands remain usable.
- Include stale artifact detection by git SHA, schema version, and generated timestamp.
acceptance_criteria:
Required proof before close:
1. Unit tests validate manifest schema, required lane detection, stale SHA/schema handling, redaction, artifact hash checks, broken links, duplicate scenario ids, and generated summary output.
2. E2E script builds a sample bundle, validates it, corrupts or removes one required artifact, and proves the validator fails with a useful diagnostic.
3. The bundle has pass/fail/skip/error totals and links raw logs for conformance, xfstests, FUSE, differential oracle, repair lab, crash/replay, performance, writeback-cache, and release gates.
4. Users can inspect the bundle offline without a live build tree.
5. README or harness docs describe bundle creation, validation, and interpretation.
notes: Why this matters for users: a readiness bundle only helps if it is portable, validated, and honest about missing evidence. The validator is the guardrail against attractive but stale reports.
close_reason: Completed: added validate-proof-bundle CLI, versioned proof-bundle schema/validator, Markdown summary, redaction/reproduction guards, e2e proof bundle smoke (7/7), targeted unit tests (9/9), workspace check/clippy, and UBS.

title: Add a chaos and replay laboratory for crash consistency under adversarial schedules
description:
Create a deterministic chaos/replay lab that stresses crash consistency and recovery under adversarial operation schedules. Cover fsync-like boundaries where implemented, rename/write/truncate/unlink sequences, transaction commit and abort points, unmount/reopen, journal replay, MVCC visibility, and repair interactions.

This bead should expose whether FrankenFS preserves user-visible guarantees when operations are interrupted or reordered within allowed semantics. Failures must be replayable by schedule id and linked to narrow implementation beads.
design:
Implementation shape:
- Define a schedule/fault taxonomy: pre-commit crash, post-commit/pre-flush crash, replay interruption, repair interruption, concurrent writer conflict, metadata/data ordering boundary, and mount teardown race.
- Use deterministic seeds and recorded operation traces so every failure can be replayed.
- Prefer existing asupersync deterministic facilities for core/concurrency paths where possible; mounted-path lanes should use explicit scripts and artifact capture.
- Compare survivors against the executable model from bd-rchk0.5.1 when available and against fixture/differential expectations otherwise.
- Distinguish true filesystem failure from worker capability limitations or unsupported host mount semantics.
acceptance_criteria:
Required proof before close:
1. Unit tests cover schedule generation, crash-point enumeration, replay manifest parsing, expected survivor classification, and failure report rendering.
2. At least one core-path replay suite and one mounted-path smoke script exist, with mounted path allowed to capability-skip only with structured skip reason.
3. Artifacts capture schedule id, seed, operation trace, injected crash point, mount/core mode, before/after hashes, replay logs, expected survivors, observed survivors, and invariant/differential verdict.
4. Tests include positive recovery cases and negative cases where unsupported or unsafe recovery remains explicitly blocked.
5. Failures discovered during lab development become narrow follow-up beads before this closes.
notes: Root cause this bead targets: filesystem correctness is schedule-dependent. Without deterministic crash/replay evidence, a clean happy-path write test does not justify reducing experimental caveats in the README.

title: Refine crash replay lab with schedule minimization and dual-lane coverage
description:
Plan-space polish for bd-rchk0.5.5 after fresh review. Crash-consistency evidence needs both deterministic core schedule exploration and mounted-path smoke coverage, with minimization so failures are actionable.

Scope: split core LabRuntime/asupersync schedule tests from permissioned mounted scripts, define crash-point taxonomy, and require minimized reproduction artifacts for any failure.
design:
Implementation refinement:
- Core lane: use asupersync/LabRuntime or equivalent deterministic schedule control for MVCC, journal, repair, and writeback-cache paths.
- Mounted lane: use permissioned FUSE scripts for smoke coverage, with capability skips recorded through the shared artifact schema.
- Define crash points as data: pre-commit, post-commit/pre-flush, fsync boundary, replay interruption, repair interruption, unmount/reopen, and process termination where safe.
- Minimize failing operation traces where practical and preserve full raw logs.
- Compare survivors against invariant oracle output when available, otherwise against explicit scenario expectations.
acceptance_criteria:
Required proof before close:
1. Unit tests cover schedule generation, crash-point enumeration, replay manifest parsing, minimization behavior, survivor-set comparison, and failure report rendering.
2. E2E scripts cover at least one mounted write/reopen case and one repair-interruption or writeback-cache-relevant case when the lane supports it.
3. Artifacts include schedule id, seed, lane type, operation trace, crash point, expected survivors, observed survivors, cleanup status, raw logs, minimized reproduction command, and classification.
4. Host capability skips are structured and do not hide product failures.
5. Any unreduced flaky failure gets a follow-up bead with the raw schedule and logs.
notes: Why this matters for users: crash-consistency bugs are expensive because they are hard to reproduce. Minimized schedules and dual-lane coverage make failures actionable instead of anecdotal.
close_reason: Completed crash replay schedule minimization, survivor-set artifacts, structured host skips, and refinement E2E smoke

title: Set release-grade ambition gates and kill-switch criteria
description:
Define the objective criteria that control FrankenFS public claims and feature exposure. The project needs explicit gates for when README caveats can be softened, when mounted read-write can be recommended, when automatic repair is allowed to mutate data, when writeback-cache can be enabled, and when a feature must stay hidden or disabled.

This bead turns ambition into policy: every risky user-visible claim needs evidence, and every missing evidence lane needs a clear deferral or kill-switch rule.
design:
Implementation shape:
- Define gates as data, not scattered prose: required lanes, minimum pass criteria, acceptable skip classes, confidence thresholds, required artifact versions, and feature-state outcomes.
- Include kill-switch criteria for read-write mount, automatic repair mutation, writeback-cache, background scrub mutation, and any native-mode feature that can affect user data.
- Make the evaluator conservative: missing evidence fails readiness unless explicitly marked exploratory.
- Tie every README/FEATURE_PARITY status phrase to a gate result, a deferral, or a blocked bead.
- Keep policy close to the proof bundle so users can inspect why a feature is recommended, experimental, hidden, or disabled.
acceptance_criteria:
Required proof before close:
1. Gate policy document or data file exists with criteria for mount/write, automatic repair, writeback-cache, scrub/repair, conformance, xfstests, performance, fuzz, and crash/replay evidence.
2. Unit tests cover pass/fail/skip semantics, missing artifact handling, confidence thresholds, and kill-switch decisions.
3. E2E or integration smoke evaluates a sample proof bundle and fails when a required artifact is missing or below threshold.
4. README and FEATURE_PARITY claims are updated to reference gate-backed statuses, not informal optimism.
5. Every caveat remaining after this bead maps to an enabling bead, a concrete blocked dependency, or an explicit non-goal.
notes: Root cause this bead targets: without objective gates, ambitious evidence can still decay into subjective release claims. Gates keep public wording, feature exposure, and proof artifacts synchronized.

title: Refine release gates with fail-closed evaluator and feature states
description:
Plan-space polish for bd-rchk0.5.6 after fresh review. Release gates should be executable policy with fail-closed behavior, not a checklist that docs can bypass.

Scope: define feature states, kill-switch semantics, missing-evidence behavior, and a policy evaluator that consumes proof bundles and produces README/FEATURE_PARITY-safe statuses.
design:
Implementation refinement:
- Define stable feature states such as hidden, disabled, experimental, detection-only, dry-run-only, opt-in mutating, validated, and deprecated/blocked.
- Make missing required evidence fail closed for readiness claims. Exploratory mode may warn, but cannot upgrade public status.
- Consume proof-bundle fields directly and produce machine-readable status plus generated human wording.
- Include kill-switch criteria for read-write mount, mutating repair, writeback-cache, background scrub mutation, and native-mode features affecting user data.
- Require every status downgrade to include remediation beads or explicit non-goal rationale.
acceptance_criteria:
Required proof before close:
1. Unit tests cover every feature state transition, missing artifacts, stale artifacts, threshold failures, capability skips, explicit deferrals, and kill-switch decisions.
2. E2E/integration smoke runs the evaluator against a passing sample bundle, a missing-evidence bundle, a stale bundle, and a threshold-failing bundle.
3. Generated README/FEATURE_PARITY-safe wording is tested from policy data so docs cannot drift from gates.
4. Logs identify which artifact or threshold controlled each decision.
5. No public claim can be upgraded by hand-editing docs without corresponding gate evidence.
notes: Why this matters for users: gates are how the project stops saying less experimental before the evidence really supports it. The evaluator makes safety policy enforceable.
close_reason: Completed fail-closed release gate evaluator, CLI, catalog docs, and E2E coverage

title: Curate a real-world workload and failure corpus for ambitious proof runs
description:
Build a curated corpus of realistic filesystem workloads and failure scenarios that can feed the differential oracle, crash/replay lab, repair fault lab, performance gates, and operator proof bundle. The corpus should include small deterministic scenarios, boundary cases, and representative user workflows rather than only synthetic unit cases.

This bead exists so ambition proof remains grounded in how users actually use filesystems: editor save patterns, package extraction, build directory churn, database-like append/fsync patterns where supported, metadata-heavy trees, small-file storms, sparse files if in scope, xattrs if in scope, and mount/unmount recovery sequences.
design:
Implementation shape:
- Define corpus schema with scenario id, operation sequence, required capabilities, supported filesystems, expected invariants, expected differential behavior, workload class, seed data, and logging requirements.
- Keep corpus entries compact and deterministic; large generated artifacts should live under the existing conformance/golden or tests fixture patterns rather than bloating tracker data.
- Include both positive and negative scenarios: success paths, unsupported operations, permission-boundary cases, corruption cases, and capability-skip cases.
- Make scenarios reusable across unit/model tests, mounted E2E scripts, repair lab runs, and operator bundle generation.
- Every corpus item should state why it exists and what user risk it covers.
acceptance_criteria:
Required proof before close:
1. Corpus schema and initial entries cover at least editor-save, package-extract-like churn, metadata-heavy tree operations, append/truncate workflow, mount/unmount/reopen, one ext4-specific scenario, one btrfs-specific scenario, one repair corruption scenario, and one expected capability-skip case.
2. Unit tests validate schema parsing, capability classification, stable scenario ids, and expected-artifact declarations.
3. At least one E2E script consumes corpus entries and emits per-scenario logs with pass/fail/skip plus reproduction commands.
4. Corpus integrates with at least two ambition beads, such as differential oracle plus crash/replay or repair fault lab.
5. README or harness docs explain how to add a scenario without hiding unsupported behavior.
notes: Root cause this bead targets: ambitious proof systems can become brittle if every harness invents its own scenario format. A shared corpus turns user-risk examples into reusable evidence inputs.

title: Refine workload corpus with P1 user-risk coverage and schema validation
description:
Plan-space polish for bd-rchk0.5.7 after fresh review. The workload corpus should be treated as a P1 proof substrate because it feeds the invariant oracle, differential oracle, repair lab, crash/replay lab, proof bundle, and release gates.

Scope: define versioned corpus schema, user-risk traceability, positive and negative scenarios, capability requirements, and schema validation so scenario drift is caught early.
design:
Implementation refinement:
- Corpus entries must include scenario id, user risk, operation class, supported filesystems, required capabilities, expected artifacts, expected logs, positive/negative status, and linked proof consumers.
- Include scenarios for editor-save, package extraction, metadata-heavy tree churn, append/truncate, mount/unmount/reopen, ext4-specific behavior, btrfs-specific behavior, repair corruption, unsupported operation, permission boundary, and expected host-skip.
- Keep entries deterministic and compact; large generated images should be referenced through fixture/golden mechanisms.
- Treat schema validation as a hard gate before any proof runner consumes the corpus.
- Make every scenario reusable by at least two proof systems where practical.
acceptance_criteria:
Required proof before close:
1. Unit tests validate corpus schema, stable ids, capability tags, user-risk fields, required artifact declarations, duplicate detection, and unsupported/skip classification.
2. E2E smoke consumes at least three corpus scenarios across two proof consumers and emits per-scenario logs with reproduction commands.
3. Corpus docs explain how to add scenarios without weakening support scope or hiding unsupported behavior.
4. The initial corpus explicitly includes one host capability skip and one btrfs DefaultPermissions/image-ownership diagnostic case.
5. The proof bundle can summarize corpus coverage by user risk and filesystem flavor.
notes: Why this matters for users: the proof systems should be driven by realistic risk examples, not only by developer-selected happy paths.
close_reason: Completed P1 workload corpus validator, initial corpus, docs, and E2E smoke

title: Add operator recovery and rollback drills for automatic repair
description:
Fresh review gap: the ambition workstream proves repair internals and bundles evidence, but users also need an end-to-end recovery drill that answers what they should actually do when corruption is detected.

Scope: create a user-facing dry-run to mutation workflow for repair, including preflight checks, backup or snapshot guidance where applicable, repair plan display, mutating repair execution where supported, post-repair verification, rollback or refuse-to-continue behavior, and clear logs. This bead should turn automatic repair from a subsystem claim into an operator-safe workflow.
design:
Implementation shape:
- Define an operator recovery drill that runs from a corrupted fixture or image through detect, dry-run, plan review, optional mutate, verify, and rollback/refusal.
- Require clear CLI/TUI or documented command output that explains risk, confidence, evidence, and next action without requiring code knowledge.
- Integrate with repair confidence outcomes from bd-rchk0.5.3 and proof-bundle artifacts from bd-rchk0.5.4.
- Use temporary images and non-destructive defaults; mutating repair must be opt-in and artifact-backed.
- Preserve complete logs for preflight, corruption manifest, repair plan, user-facing warnings, mutation decision, verification, cleanup, and reproduction.
acceptance_criteria:
Required proof before close:
1. Unit tests cover recovery-plan validation, preflight failures, dry-run rendering, refuse-to-mutate decisions, verification summaries, rollback classification, and log/artifact schema validation.
2. E2E script runs at least one detection-only drill, one dry-run successful plan, one mutating repair where supported, and one refused unsafe repair.
3. Logs include exact commands, image hashes, corruption manifest, confidence threshold, repair plan, operator warnings, post-repair verification, rollback/refusal outcome, and cleanup status.
4. README or operator docs describe the drill and make clear which paths are experimental, detection-only, dry-run-only, or validated.
5. The proof bundle can include the drill result as a first-class readiness lane.
notes: Why this improves the system for users: proof is not enough if the operator workflow is unclear at the moment of corruption. This bead creates a rehearsed recovery path with safe defaults.
close_reason: Completed: added the operator recovery drill validator/spec/CLI, proof-bundle lane artifacts, runbook and E2E docs, plus unit/E2E/validator coverage for detection-only, dry-run, verified mutating, and refused unsafe recovery paths.

title: Add long-running soak and canary campaigns for mount and repair readiness
description:
Fresh review gap: single E2E scenarios and crash tests are necessary but not enough to know whether mounted writes, repair, and writeback-cache policy work for real over time.

Scope: define long-running but bounded soak/canary campaigns that repeatedly exercise realistic workloads, mount/unmount cycles, repair checks, writeback-cache states where enabled, and artifact aggregation. The campaigns should be suitable for CI nightly, RCH, or a controlled local host, with detailed logging and flake triage.
design:
Implementation shape:
- Define campaign profiles: smoke, nightly, stress, and canary. Each profile lists duration, workload mix, required capabilities, failure thresholds, and artifact retention.
- Reuse the workload corpus from bd-rchk0.5.7 and classify host capability skips separately from product failures.
- Include repeated mount/unmount/reopen, metadata-heavy churn, read/write/verify loops, repair scrub/dry-run checks, and writeback-cache scenarios only when gated.
- Emit heartbeat logs and periodic summaries so long runs are diagnosable before completion.
- Convert reproducible product failures and recurring flakes into narrow follow-up beads with seed/campaign ids.
acceptance_criteria:
Required proof before close:
1. Unit tests cover campaign manifest validation, duration/profile parsing, failure-threshold evaluation, heartbeat summary rendering, flake classification, and artifact aggregation.
2. E2E smoke campaign runs in a bounded time and emits valid pass/fail/skip/error artifacts with detailed logs.
3. Long profile is documented for RCH/CI/manual permissioned hosts and records kernel, FUSE capability, toolchain, git SHA, workload ids, seeds, duration, resource usage, and cleanup status.
4. Campaign output feeds the operator proof bundle and release gate evaluator.
5. Failures are never swallowed as flakes without preserving reproduction data and creating or linking a follow-up bead.
notes: Why this improves the system for users: a filesystem can pass isolated tests and still degrade under repeated realistic use. Soak and canary campaigns provide endurance evidence before reducing experimental warnings.
close_reason: Completed: added executable soak/canary campaign manifest, validator, CLI, E2E smoke, docs, resource caps, heartbeat summaries, flake follow-up rules, and proof-bundle/release-gate consumers

title: Reconcile docs and specs between tracked parity and runtime readiness
description: Reality-check gap: FEATURE_PARITY.md reports 100 percent tracked V1 parity, while README.md, COMPREHENSIVE_SPEC_FOR_FRANKENFS_V1.md, conformance/COVERAGE.md, conformance/DISCREPANCIES.md, and docs/xfstests-known-failures.md still describe runtime hardening work. Scope: make the distinction explicit and consistent across canonical docs, link the new bridge beads, avoid production-ready claims, and preserve the tracked matrix denominator rule. Acceptance: docs clearly separate tracked V1 parity from operational readiness; each remaining bridge gap points at a bead; no hidden exclusions are introduced.
notes: Closed manually in JSONL because br create/update/close paths hang in this checkout; Agent Mail registration partially created BlueFalcon but normal registration, reservation, and send returned database errors.
close_reason: Canonical docs now separate tracked V1 parity from runtime readiness and link each remaining reality-check bridge gap to bd-rchk2 through bd-rchk7; validation passed via jq, bv, git diff --check, UBS, and rch parity-report test.

title: Resolve DISC-004 btrfs delayed-ref semantics with executable evidence
description: Reality-check gap: conformance/DISCREPANCIES.md still tracks DISC-004 as INVESTIGATING for full btrfs delayed-reference semantics, especially heavy write workloads. Scope: inspect the current ffs-btrfs delayed-ref implementation and tests, compare the claimed contract to extracted btrfs behavior, then either implement the missing lifecycle or explicitly scope the unsupported cases with tests and docs. Acceptance: DISC-004 is closed, downgraded with evidence, or split into narrower beads; behavior is backed by crate or harness tests; FEATURE_PARITY and discrepancy docs reflect the result.
notes: Claimed and closed manually in JSONL because br update/create/close paths hang and Agent Mail reservations fail with database errors.
close_reason: Delayed-ref flush now validates against a candidate refcount map before draining the queue or publishing refcount changes; insert overflow is rejected as a broken invariant; regression tests cover failed-flush retry atomicity and insert-overflow atomicity; DISC-004 and parity docs now mark the scoped V1 model accepted with executable evidence.

title: Run and document a fresh xfstests baseline
description: Reality-check gap: docs/xfstests-known-failures.md contains historical passability notes and future work, but current parity claims need a fresh executable baseline. Scope: identify the smallest supported xfstests subset that can run in this environment or an RCH worker, execute it with captured commands and artifacts, classify failures into product bugs versus environment limitations, and create follow-up beads for real failures. Acceptance: docs/xfstests-known-failures.md contains a dated command/result table, artifact locations, current pass/fail counts, and tracker links for each actionable failure.
design: Decompose into prerequisites, subset policy, real execution, and product-failure bead creation. Do not widen scope to all xfstests before the curated V1 subset is reproducible. The baseline is a gate for operational confidence, not a new parity denominator unless docs are intentionally revised.
acceptance_criteria: Acceptance: a future agent can run or reproduce a current xfstests baseline without relying on old notes. The work must record prerequisite packages and helper build state, the selected supported subset and skip rationale, exact commands, environment metadata, logs/artifact paths, pass/fail/not-run counts, and one follow-up bead per product failure. Environment blockers such as missing xfs/xfs.h, libaio, fsstress, FUSE permissions, or dpkg locks must be captured as reproducible host blockers rather than silently treated as product failures. The baseline runner must emit the shared QA artifact format, and its helper/unit tests must cover result parsing, skip classification, and failure-to-bead extraction.
notes: Known blocker from 2026-05-01: previous selection logic passed against not-run results, while real execution stopped before tests because xfstests helpers were unbuilt and required development packages were unavailable behind a dpkg lock. Preserve that history but replace it with fresh evidence when the host/worker is ready.

title: Resolve xfstests build prerequisites and host permissions
description: Prepare an environment where the curated xfstests subset can actually execute. The previous attempt reached the wrapper/regression layer but did not run real xfstests because helper binaries were missing and required development packages could not be installed while dpkg was locked. This bead is only about making the runner capable: packages, helper builds, mount permissions, scratch/test image directories, and artifact paths.
design: Keep this host-capability work separate from product failures. If prerequisites cannot be satisfied locally, define the worker image or RCH target that can satisfy them.
acceptance_criteria: Acceptance: document and verify required packages and helper binaries; build or locate ltp/fsstress and other selected helpers; verify xfs/xfs.h and libaio availability or record an RCH/CI worker that has them; verify FUSE/device/mount permissions needed by the selected tests; capture exact commands, capability probe output, environment metadata, stdout/stderr paths, and cleanup status using the shared operational artifact schema from bd-rchk0.4.1. Unit tests or script tests must cover prerequisite detection and missing-tool classification.
notes: Future self: do not close this because a wrapper produced not-run rows. Close only when real helper binaries and permissions are available or a reproducible external lane is documented, and make the environment proof machine-readable so later xfstests failures are not confused with host setup failures.

title: Refine bd-rchk3.1 with hermetic xfstests prerequisite preflight
description:
Supplemental plan-space refinement for bd-rchk3.1. The xfstests lane has already been blocked by helper binaries, package prerequisites, FUSE permissions, and dpkg lock state. This child bead makes the prerequisite proof hermetic and reproducible so future xfstests work cannot confuse host setup failures with FrankenFS behavior.

Required proof before close:
1. Unit/script tests cover prerequisite detection for xfs headers, libaio, ltp/fsstress, xfstests helpers, mkfs/mount helpers, /dev/fuse, fusermount3, user namespace or mount permissions, scratch/test directories, dpkg lock state, and RCH/CI worker identity.
2. The preflight emits a machine-readable manifest with each prerequisite classified as present, missing, blocked-by-host, blocked-by-lock, unsupported-locally, or available-on-worker, plus remediation text and whether the missing item blocks real xfstests execution.
3. E2E/dry-run script runs the preflight on the current host and a fixture/simulated host state, preserving command transcript, stdout/stderr paths, package/probe versions, worker identity if applicable, cleanup status, and reproduction command.
4. The xfstests runner refuses to report a product baseline when prerequisite proof is missing, stale, or failed; it must emit skip/error artifacts instead of not-run rows that look like passing coverage.
5. The closeout links the preflight artifact to bd-rchk3.2 and bd-rchk3.3 so selected-test policy and real execution share the same host capability vocabulary.

title: Define the supported V1 xfstests subset and skip policy
description: Select the minimal xfstests subset that matches FrankenFS V1 scope. The suite must be small enough to run routinely but broad enough to cover mount, directory, file data, metadata, xattr, fsync, and supported write behavior. Anything outside V1 scope should be skipped with an explicit reason tied to README/FEATURE_PARITY scope rather than hidden in harness logic.
design: The subset definition should become the stable contract that makes future baseline diffs meaningful.
acceptance_criteria:
Required proof before close:
