1. Checked-in docs or harness metadata list every selected xfstests case, expected filesystem flavor, required capabilities, skip reason for excluded tests, and classification as expected failure, environment-blocked, unsupported-by-V1, harness-blocked, or product-actionable.
2. Unit or metadata-validation tests reject duplicate test IDs, missing skip reasons, unknown classifications, unsupported tests without explicit rationale, and stale references to README/FEATURE_PARITY scope.
3. E2E/dry-run script renders the selected subset through the shared QA runner, emits the exact command plan, and records per-test IDs, skip decisions, capability checks, artifact paths, and reproduction command even before the full suite executes.
4. The policy distinguishes ext4, btrfs, read-only, experimental rw, and unsupported writeback-cache behavior, with classification values aligned to bd-rchk0.4.1.
5. CI cannot treat missing mounted coverage or not-run rows as a passing baseline unless the skip is explicitly classified and linked to a host blocker or follow-up bead.
notes: Future self: resist expanding to the whole suite until the core subset is repeatable and artifacted. The subset policy should make expected skips explicit enough that CI cannot accidentally treat missing mounted coverage as a pass.
close_reason: Completed xfstests subset policy btrfs coverage and validation artifacts

title: Refine bd-rchk3.2 with non-deceptive xfstests subset evidence policy
description:
Supplemental plan-space refinement for bd-rchk3.2. The supported V1 xfstests subset must help users understand real risk, not merely create a small list that is easy to pass. This bead makes subset selection auditable and non-deceptive.

Required proof before close:
1. Unit/schema tests validate every selected and skipped test row has test id, filesystem flavor, V1 scope mapping, required capability, expected operation class, user-risk category, expected outcome, skip or failure rationale, owning bead for unresolved product behavior, and artifact requirements.
2. The subset includes at least one representative case for read-only mount, experimental read-write create/write/readback, directory mutation, rename/link semantics, xattrs, fsync/fsyncdir boundary, unsupported writeback-cache behavior, and expected host capability skip.
3. E2E/dry-run script renders the subset policy into both JSON and human-readable reports, runs no destructive host operations by default, and verifies every selected test has a runnable command plan plus every excluded test has a concrete V1 scope rationale.
4. Logs include source xfstests id, policy row id, flavor, risk category, selected/skipped decision, capability requirement, linked artifact or bead, docs scope citation, and reproduction command.
5. CI and README wording cannot summarize xfstests as passing unless product failures, environment blockers, harness blockers, expected unsupported cases, and not-run rows are counted separately.
close_reason: Required xfstests check.log artifact validation added and covered by targeted tests

title: Execute the fresh xfstests baseline and publish artifacts
description: Run the selected xfstests subset on the prepared environment and turn the result into durable evidence. This is the bead that converts planning into a dated baseline. It should not classify failures from memory; use logs, command transcripts, generated reports, and exact images/mount options.
design: Depends on prerequisites and subset policy. Classify failures by first principles: unsupported V1 scope, host capability, harness issue, or product bug.
acceptance_criteria: Acceptance: execute the curated subset; publish command transcript, environment metadata, xfstests logs, pass/fail/not-run counts, image setup details, and artifact paths; update docs/xfstests-known-failures.md or equivalent with a dated table; verify the doc mentions tracker IDs for every actionable failure. Unit tests cover xfstests result parsing, skip classification, not-run handling, and artifact validation. The E2E runner script emits shared QA artifacts with exact commands, worker identity, stdout/stderr paths, raw xfstests log dirs, cleanup status, and partial-run preservation.
notes: Future self: if a worker dies mid-run, record partial artifacts and create a blocker bead rather than overwriting the evidence trail. Never count not-run rows as passing product behavior.

dependencies.metadata: {"why":"xfstests baseline should publish shared QA artifacts and logs"}
{"why":"baseline requires runnable xfstests helpers and host permissions"}
{"why":"baseline requires a defined supported subset and skip policy"}

title: Convert xfstests product failures into narrow fix beads
description: After the baseline is executed, every product-actionable failure needs its own narrow bead. This keeps bd-rchk3 from turning into an unbounded omnibus bug bucket and makes failures independently claimable.
design: Do not fix all failures inside this bead. Its job is disciplined triage and graph creation after evidence exists.
acceptance_criteria: Acceptance: for each product failure, create a bead with failing test ID, filesystem flavor, exact command, minimized repro if possible, expected vs actual behavior, logs/artifact references, suspected crate boundary, and proposed validation command. Non-product failures must be explicitly recorded as environment, harness, or scope outcomes. Unit tests cover failure-row to bead extraction, classification mapping, duplicate suppression, and required artifact-link validation; a dry-run script demonstrates the triage flow against fixture artifacts before creating real beads.
notes: Future self: close this only after the baseline artifact has been reviewed and every failure row has a disposition. Do not create omnibus failure beads; each product bug should be narrow enough for one agent to claim.

dependencies.metadata: {"why":"failure-to-bead triage should preserve standardized failure classifications"}
{"why":"failure beads require a completed baseline artifact"}

title: Add CI-compatible mounted FUSE runner coverage for critical paths
description: Reality-check gap: important mounted-path tests can silently skip when local FUSE permissions or kernel support are absent. Scope: audit ffs-fuse and harness mount tests, decide the CI/worker execution model, add or update scripts and docs so critical FUSE E2E coverage runs in a permissioned environment, and make permission skips visibly reported instead of silently substituting for coverage. Acceptance: CI or documented RCH workflow exercises representative mounted ext4 and btrfs paths; local permission skips emit actionable diagnostics; README or test docs explain how to reproduce the gate.
design: Decompose into capability discovery, permissioned lane provisioning, critical mounted scenario execution, and fail-closed behavior for expected lanes. This workstream is the prerequisite for any claim that FUSE behavior works for real rather than only through direct ffs-core tests.
acceptance_criteria: Acceptance: mounted ext4 and btrfs critical paths run in a known permissioned CI/RCH lane, or fail with structured, actionable capability diagnostics. Local lack of /dev/fuse, fusermount3, user namespace support, CAP_SYS_ADMIN, or kernel module availability must produce a visible skip artifact. The gate must exercise representative mount, lookup/read, write where supported, sync/unmount/reopen, xattr, and mounted repair-policy cases without quietly substituting unit tests for mounted coverage. Unit tests must cover capability classification and artifact validation; E2E scripts must use the shared QA runner/logging schema and preserve logs for every pass/fail/skip.
notes: Memory-derived pitfall: this repo has historical FUSE permission soft-skips and btrfs write EACCES caused by root-owned image roots under DefaultPermissions. Future work must classify those as host/harness setup issues before blaming filesystem logic.

title: Add FUSE capability probe and explicit skip artifact
description: Mounted tests currently risk soft-skipping when the host lacks FUSE permissions. Add or revise the probe layer so every mounted test run reports whether /dev/fuse exists, fusermount3 is present, the kernel module is loaded or loadable, user namespaces/capabilities allow mounting, and the current user can mount/unmount safely.
design: This should be reusable by xfstests and mounted conformance work. Keep it separate from product behavior tests.
acceptance_criteria: Acceptance: local runs emit a structured capability artifact or clear machine-readable diagnostics; skip reasons include the missing capability and remediation hint; tests never collapse a missing FUSE environment into a silent success; docs explain how to read the artifact. Unit tests cover /dev/fuse missing, fusermount3 missing, kernel module unavailable, user namespace/capability failure, mount success, unmount failure, and DefaultPermissions/EACCES classification using synthetic probe outputs.
notes: Future self: prefer a small deterministic probe over ad hoc checks spread across tests. The probe should preserve enough logs to tell host limitation from product behavior.
close_reason: Implemented structured FUSE capability probe report, harness CLI, shell hook, docs, and synthetic classification tests

dependencies.metadata: {"why":"FUSE capability probe should emit standard structured capability artifacts"}

title: Provision or document a permissioned CI/RCH FUSE lane
description: Define where mounted FUSE tests are expected to run for real. The lane may be GitHub Actions with appropriate privileges, a documented RCH worker, or another controlled Linux host. The important point is that there is a repeatable permissioned path that agents can invoke and trust.
design: This is infrastructure, not filesystem semantics. It should unlock mounted-path tests and performance measurement without making every agent rediscover FUSE host setup.
acceptance_criteria: Acceptance: a checked-in script, CI job, or documented RCH command runs the FUSE capability probe successfully, mounts and unmounts a minimal FrankenFS ext4 and btrfs image where practical, captures logs, and fails loudly if the lane loses required permissions. Include worker identity, kernel, fusermount version, mount options, stdout/stderr paths, cleanup status, and shared QA artifacts. Unit/script tests validate command construction, missing-lane classification, and artifact emission without requiring privileged local execution.
notes: Future self: do not close on a one-off manual mount unless the reproduction path is durable. The lane should help users reproduce failures, not just prove that one agent had a privileged shell once.
close_reason: Implemented permissioned FUSE lane in scripts/e2e/ffs_fuse_production.sh with pre-skip fuse_capability.json, post-mount required probe, fuse_permissioned_lane.json worker/kernel/fusermount/mount-option/log/cleanup artifact summary, btrfs strict probe controls, documented local/RCH invocation, and static runner coverage. Validated with bash -n, git diff --check, ffs-harness verification_runner tests, fuse-capability-probe CLI, and UBS.

dependencies.metadata: {"why":"permissioned FUSE lane should run through the shared E2E harness"}
{"why":"permissioned lane should first have a reusable capability probe"}

title: Run the critical mounted ext4 and btrfs scenario matrix
description: Exercise representative mounted behavior in the permissioned lane. The matrix should include default read-only mount, lookup/read/stat, directory iteration, xattr list/get, experimental rw paths where currently supported, sync/unmount/reopen, error mapping, and mounted repair policy behavior for read-only automatic repair versus read-write rejection.
design: Use ffs-fuse/ffs-cli surfaces, not only ffs-core direct calls. This bead proves the user-visible mount path.
acceptance_criteria: Acceptance: scenario results are captured with filesystem flavor, mount options, operation sequence, expected outcome, actual outcome, logs, and artifact paths. At least one ext4 and one btrfs image must run. Unit tests validate the scenario manifest, expected-output parsing, artifact schema compliance, and failure classification. E2E scripts exercise mounted lookup/read/stat, directory iteration, xattr list/get, supported writes, sync/unmount/reopen, mounted repair policy, and read-write rejection with shared QA artifacts. Failures become follow-up beads or are linked to existing ones.
notes: Future self: keep the scenario matrix small but nontrivial; every row should justify a README claim or limitation. Inspect image ownership and DefaultPermissions before treating btrfs EACCES as product behavior.
close_reason: Implemented production FUSE mounted scenario matrix artifact and SCENARIO_RESULT rows for ext4/btrfs cases; enabled default btrfs lane probe; added btrfs read-only mount/stat/list/unmount smoke, catalog rows, docs, and static runner checks. Validation: bash -n for touched E2E scripts; python3 -m json.tool scenario_catalog; git diff --check; scoped production catalog evidence grep; cargo test -p ffs-harness --lib -- verification_runner (43 passed); UBS over touched files returned 0/no recognizable languages. Full privileged FUSE lane was not executed locally because the shared E2E cleanup trap removes temp directories and this session has no file-deletion authorization.

dependencies.metadata: {"why":"mounted scenario matrix should use shared logging and artifact validation"}
{"why":"scenario matrix requires explicit FUSE capability diagnostics"}
{"why":"scenario matrix requires a permissioned mounted-test lane"}

title: Make expected mounted coverage fail closed when the lane is unavailable
description: Once a permissioned mounted-test lane exists, the project should stop treating missing mounted coverage as harmless. Local developer runs may still skip with diagnostics, but CI/RCH gates that promise mounted coverage must fail closed if the lane cannot mount.
design: This is a reliability guard around the lane. Implement after the matrix has a stable execution target.
acceptance_criteria: Acceptance: the configured mounted gate fails when the capability probe says the permissioned lane is unavailable, while local non-permissioned runs still produce explicit skip artifacts; README or test docs describe the distinction; false green CI caused by skipped FUSE coverage is prevented. Unit tests cover local skip versus authoritative fail-closed decisions, stale capability artifacts, missing logs, and remediation hints. An E2E or CI dry-run proves the gate exits nonzero for an unavailable authoritative lane and preserves diagnostic artifacts.
notes: Future self: preserve developer ergonomics locally while making authoritative gates strict. This bead protects users from false confidence, so skipped required coverage must be visible and actionable.

dependencies.metadata: {"why":"fail-closed policy should directly depend on the permissioned lane contract"}
{"why":"fail-closed policy depends on a stable expected mounted gate"}

title: Establish fresh performance baselines against spec targets
description: Reality-check gap: README and specs make throughput, latency, and scaling claims that need current measurement against the implemented code. Scope: run criterion, fio, or hyperfine baselines for the documented representative workloads, compare against any kernel ext4/btrfs or previous FrankenFS baselines available, and record deltas and environment metadata. Acceptance: a dated performance baseline artifact or doc section records commands, hardware/runtime context, current results, target comparison, and follow-up beads for regressions or unmeasured claims.
design: Decompose into benchmark manifest, core benchmark execution, mounted FUSE benchmark execution, and delta/regression triage. Use one optimization lever per later performance commit and preserve behavior proofs before optimizing.
acceptance_criteria: Acceptance: performance claims have dated measurement artifacts. Record workload definitions, image sizes, datasets, command lines, worker/host hardware, kernel, FUSE mode, Cargo profile, target dir, warmup/run counts, medians/p95 where applicable, and comparison against previous FrankenFS baselines or kernel ext4/btrfs references where available. Any missing measurement or regression must become a linked bead instead of prose. Benchmark scripts must use the shared QA artifact schema for environment metadata and failure classification, with unit tests for result parsing and E2E smoke runs proving logs/artifacts are retained.
notes: The README currently contains quantitative subsystem claims and a general performance readiness caveat. This bead is about evidence capture first; tuning work should only start after the baseline is trustworthy.

title: Define the performance baseline manifest and datasets
description: Create the manifest for what FrankenFS performance evidence means. It should cover representative block I/O, ARC cache, MVCC conflict/merge paths, repair/scrub/refresh paths, CLI inspect/parity paths, and mounted FUSE workloads where permissions exist. Include image sizes, data shapes, warmups, runs, profiles, and environment fields.
design: Baseline first; do not optimize in this bead.
acceptance_criteria:
Required proof before close:
1. A checked-in or documented manifest lists each workload, command template, required features/capabilities, expected output artifact, comparison target, regression threshold or missing-data note, and required structured log fields.
2. Unit/schema tests reject missing workload IDs, missing thresholds, unknown capability names, invalid units, missing environment fields, and artifact fields that cannot be aggregated.
3. E2E or dry-run script validates the manifest end to end, expands command templates without running heavyweight benchmarks by default, emits a sample shared QA artifact, and preserves detailed logs for manifest errors.
4. The manifest maps performance artifacts to the shared QA schema from bd-rchk0.4.1 so another agent can run and compare baselines without reconstructing intent from README prose.
5. The bead must not tune performance; any missing dataset, noisy target, or suspected regression becomes a linked implementation or measurement bead.
notes: Future self: make the manifest small enough to run, but explicit enough that results are comparable over time. Include how benchmark scripts validate and retain logs before any tuning bead begins.
close_reason: Completed: added checked-in performance baseline manifest, validator, CLI dry-run, E2E smoke, docs, and gates

title: Refine bd-rchk5.1 with performance noise and stale-baseline quarantine policy
description:
Supplemental plan-space refinement for bd-rchk5.1. Performance evidence should not let noisy, stale, or environment-incomparable runs upgrade public claims. This child bead adds a quarantine policy to the performance manifest before measurement and tuning start.

Required proof before close:
1. Unit/schema tests validate workload id, command template, cargo profile, target dir, worker/host metadata, kernel/FUSE mode, input fixture hash, warmup/run counts, statistical summary fields, comparison target, regression threshold, stale-baseline expiry, noise classification, and required raw-log references.
2. The manifest distinguishes core non-mounted benchmarks, permissioned mounted benchmarks, repair/scrub/refresh benchmarks, CLI inspect/parity benchmarks, and long-campaign performance observations, with explicit capability requirements and skip semantics for each.
3. E2E/dry-run script validates the manifest, expands command templates without running heavy workloads by default, ingests fixture benchmark outputs for pass/warn/fail/noisy/stale/missing states, and emits shared QA artifacts plus detailed logs.
4. Logs include workload id, baseline id, current artifact id/hash, environment fingerprint, metric unit, observed value, threshold, noise decision, stale decision, comparison verdict, raw stdout/stderr paths, and reproduction command.
5. Public performance claims remain experimental or unknown for any quarantined workload, and every missing/noisy/stale/regressed important workload creates or links a follow-up bead.
close_reason: Completed: added performance manifest quarantine policy with workload kind coverage, profile/target/FUSE/input-hash/raw-log validation, pass/warn/fail/noisy/stale/missing fixture evidence, public-claim downgrade guards, E2E dry-run coverage, and RCH check/clippy gates

title: Run representative core benchmarks with environment metadata
description: Execute the non-mounted benchmark set from the manifest: block/cache operations, MVCC transaction/merge paths, repair symbol generation/decoding, scrub/refresh behavior, and CLI/harness commands that do not require FUSE. Capture enough context to explain results.
design: This can run before mounted performance if the FUSE lane is not ready.
acceptance_criteria: Acceptance: benchmark artifacts include command lines, Cargo profile, target dir, host/worker identity, CPU/memory/kernel, input image or fixture description, warmup/run counts, medians and p95 where applicable, and raw output locations. Unit tests cover benchmark output parsing, unit normalization, missing-data classification, regression-threshold evaluation, and artifact validation. E2E smoke runs prove representative non-mounted benchmark commands retain logs and valid shared QA artifacts. Regressions or unmeasured manifest rows become beads.
notes: Future self: use rch for heavy cargo/bench work and unique CARGO_TARGET_DIRs. Treat benchmark harness failure separately from product performance regression so users know what actually happened.
close_reason: Completed: recorder now covers the core benchmark manifest, records host/memory/target-dir metadata, preserves Criterion estimates, validates thresholds/recorder coverage, and captured seven non-mounted baseline artifacts for block cache, metadata parsing, WAL/MVCC, and repair refresh/encode workloads.

title: Measure mounted FUSE latency and throughput against references
description: Measure the user-visible mount path once a permissioned lane exists. Include read-only and experimental rw workloads that match V1 scope, and compare with a relevant reference where practical, such as kernel ext4/btrfs on equivalent images or previous FrankenFS artifacts.
design: Depends on the FUSE lane because mounted throughput claims are meaningless without a real mount.
acceptance_criteria: Acceptance: mounted benchmark artifacts include mount options, FUSE capability artifact, workload commands, image setup, unmount/reopen cleanup, latency/throughput summaries, reference comparison, and caveats for unsupported writeback-cache. Benchmark rows should reuse or explicitly map to the mounted correctness/workload scenarios from bd-rchk4.3 and bd-rchk0.3.2 so performance does not outrun correctness. Unit tests cover mounted-result parsing, capability-skip classification, reference comparison math, and artifact validation. E2E scripts run through the permissioned lane and preserve logs for every measured or skipped workload. Any inability to measure must become an environment bead rather than an empty table.
notes: Future self: do not enable kernel writeback_cache for this baseline unless the writeback-cache workstream has explicitly enabled it. Record scenario IDs and artifact links so users can connect slow workloads to the exact mounted behavior that was validated.
close_reason: Completed: measured sudo FUSE cold, warm, and recovery mount probes; preserved unprivileged capability-skip evidence; added reference comparison artifacts and targeted validation.

title: Record performance deltas and create regression beads
description: Turn raw benchmark outputs into project-tracking signal. Compare current results to README/spec claims, previous FrankenFS baselines if present, and kernel/reference behavior where available. Preserve missing-data caveats explicitly.
design: This is the closeout step after core and mounted measurements.
acceptance_criteria: Acceptance: docs or artifacts summarize deltas, highlight regressions, identify noisy or inconclusive workloads, and create one follow-up bead per actionable regression or unmeasured important claim. Unit tests cover delta classification, noisy-run handling, missing reference handling, report aggregation, and follow-up bead payload validation. The closeout script consumes shared QA artifacts from core and mounted runs, emits human-readable plus JSON summaries, and links every regression or missing measurement to a bead. No performance claim should remain unsupported by either data or an explicit bead.
notes: Future self: avoid tuning here; create focused optimization beads with behavior-proof requirements. Users should be able to see which performance claims are proven, inconclusive, regressed, or unmeasured.
close_reason: Completed: added performance delta closeout CLI/config/report, linked regressions and missing references to follow-up beads, and validated via clean-worktree unit and E2E gates.

title: Investigate mount_cold latency regression against 20260218 reference
description: Measured bd-rchk5.3 sudo FUSE cold mount p99 is 171096 us versus the 20260218 reference 36029 us (+374.884%) and throughput is 5.981086 ops/s versus 27.018364 ops/s (-77.863%). Scope: profile the cold mount path, identify whether the delta is code, build profile, probe script, environment, or FUSE setup overhead, then implement one measured optimization or quarantine the claim with a stronger environment explanation. Required proof: behavior-preserving isomorphism note, before/after hyperfine artifact, probe report with cleanup_status=unmounted, and targeted ffs-harness validation.
close_reason: Completed: cold mount probe now disables background scrub for plain mount latency and records fast readiness polling policy; checked-in current p99 improved from 171096us to 84602us, while the 20260218 comparison remains quarantined because end-to-end sudo/bash/FUSE orchestration is still slower than the old reference.

title: Investigate mount_warm remount latency regression against 20260218 reference
description: Measured bd-rchk5.3 sudo FUSE warm remount p99 is 300114 us versus the 20260218 reference 58275 us (+414.996%) and throughput is 3.407422 ops/s versus 17.138407 ops/s (-80.118%). Scope: profile the warm remount path after a prepared image, separate CLI startup/daemon/mount negotiation costs from actual filesystem readiness, then implement one measured optimization or downgrade the public claim. Required proof: before/after artifact, probe labels warm_prepare and warm_measure, cleanup_status=unmounted, and behavior-preserving isomorphism note.
close_reason: Completed: updated warm probe evidence after the bd-rchk5.5 scrub-disabled probe fix; median improved from 291845us to 137268us and throughput improved to 6.335347 ops/s, but p99 remains effectively unchanged at 302981us versus 300114us, so the warm tail-latency claim stays quarantined.

title: Investigate mount_recovery latency regression against 20260218 reference
description: Measured bd-rchk5.3 sudo FUSE recovery mount p99 is 74217 us versus the 20260218 reference 35020 us (+111.927%) and throughput is 14.143184 ops/s versus 28.458766 ops/s (-50.303%). Scope: profile the recovery probe path and separate recovery policy overhead from generic mount overhead, then either optimize one measured lever or keep recovery performance downgraded with explicit caveats. Required proof: before/after recovery probe artifact, cleanup_status=unmounted, repair/recovery behavior unchanged, and targeted ffs-harness validation.
close_reason: Completed: measured the scrub-disabled recovery probe and kept the recovery latency claim quarantined; final p99 is 82198us versus checked-in 74217us and 20260218 reference 35020us, with cleanup_status=unmounted and ffs-cli mount_complete around 18.5ms showing wrapper overhead dominates.

title: Establish comparable references for new core performance workloads
description: The bd-rchk5.2 closeout artifacts measured new core operations, but several do not have same-operation historical references in the checked-in 20260218/20260219/20260406 baselines: cli_metadata_parse_conformance, wal_commit_4k_sync, mvcc_contention_4writers, raptorq_encode_group_16blocks, and repair_symbol_refresh_staleness_latency. Scope: capture a comparable reference lane or define an explicit no-reference claim state for each operation. Required proof: JSON closeout row per workload, raw log links, comparison target rationale, and release wording that does not claim regression-free status without comparable evidence.
close_reason: Added explicit no-reference performance closeout decisions with raw-log, release-wording, and validation-command enforcement; regenerated checked-in closeout report and E2E coverage.

title: Decide and implement or scope mounted self-healing lifecycle
description: Reality-check gap: COMPREHENSIVE_SPEC_FOR_FRANKENFS_V1.md describes automatic RaptorQ-backed detection and recovery, while README limitations say mounted background scrub is detection-only. Scope: inspect ffs-repair, ffs-core, and ffs-fuse integration, decide whether V1 should perform automatic mounted repair or explicitly remain detection-only, then update code/tests/docs accordingly. Acceptance: the spec and README agree; mounted repair behavior is either implemented with deterministic tests and safe policy controls or explicitly deferred with a named bead and no misleading automatic-recovery claim.
notes: Claimed manually in JSONL because br update/create paths hang and Agent Mail reservations fail with database errors. User explicitly requested mounted automatic repair to work for real, so this bead implemented a safe repair-enabled mounted scrub lifecycle rather than leaving detection-only status text. Validation: cargo fmt --all --check; cargo check --workspace --all-targets; cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings; cargo test -p ffs-cli mount_background_repair -- --nocapture; cargo test -p ffs-cli mount_background_scrub -- --nocapture; cargo test -p ffs-repair scrub_daemon -- --nocapture; jq .beads/issues.jsonl; git diff --check; ubs changed files.
close_reason: Implemented explicit read-only mounted automatic repair mode with CLI policy controls, docs/spec parity updates, and validation gates.

title: Close remaining fuzz and conformance expansion notes with scoped evidence
description: Reality-check gap: conformance/COVERAGE.md still requests additional edge cases and targeted adversarial corpus expansion. Scope: inspect the remaining corpus and mutation surfaces, choose a narrow set of high-value additions or split concrete beads for them, and update coverage docs so the remaining work is measurable rather than open-ended prose. Acceptance: coverage docs list either completed fixture additions with tests or linked beads for every remaining adversarial expansion item.
design: Decompose into inventory, high-risk corpus additions, mounted repair conformance fixtures, and deterministic fuzz smoke gates. Prefer executable fixtures and corpus seeds over prose. Keep fuzz smoke small enough for CI while leaving longer campaigns documented separately.
acceptance_criteria: Acceptance: every remaining open-ended fuzz or conformance note is converted into either a completed fixture/corpus/golden with a passing deterministic gate or a narrow follow-up bead with owner, scope, and validation command. Coverage docs must no longer say vague things like add more cases without linking to a concrete bead or artifact. New corpus/golden work must include unit tests for parsers/validators, deterministic smoke scripts or harness entries, shared QA artifacts, and detailed logs for seed IDs, corpus versions, expected error classes, and artifact checksum updates.
notes: This closes the gap between rich existing tests and operational confidence. The goal is not infinite fuzzing; it is eliminating untracked TODO-shaped risk around parser, repair, and mounted-path surfaces.

title: Inventory open-ended fuzz and conformance notes
description: Search the conformance docs, fuzz corpus notes, tests, and relevant README/status text for open-ended requests such as add more cases, expand corpus, adversarial edge cases, or future fuzzing. Convert the prose into an inventory that can be closed item by item.
design: This is prerequisite triage for the remaining corpus and fixture work.
acceptance_criteria:
Required proof before close:
1. Coverage docs contain an inventory table with source location, risk surface, current evidence, required proof type, expected unit-test coverage, expected E2E/fuzz-smoke coverage, detailed log/artifact expectations, decision, linked bead or artifact, and owner/status.
2. Unit/schema tests validate inventory rows, bead references, proof-type vocabulary, required log fields, artifact fields, and explicit non-applicability rationale.
3. E2E or validation-script smoke scans the inventory, verifies every open-ended note has a linked bead or artifact, emits a machine-readable report, and preserves logs with source path, row id, decision, and reproduction command.
4. Vague notes such as add more cases are removed or replaced with concrete tracker references without losing parser, mounted-path, repair, corpus, golden, or long-campaign requirements.
5. Any item too large for routine CI must become a narrow long-campaign bead with fixed seeds, expected artifacts, and operator-visible skip/follow-up logging.
notes: Future self: direct text search is fine for inventory, but make changes through normal code/doc edits in the implementation bead. This inventory should prevent losing features by recording whether each item needs parser unit tests, mounted E2E scripts, corpus seeds, golden fixtures, or long-campaign follow-up beads.

title: Add repair and ledger corruption corpus fixtures
description: Expand executable coverage around the repair pipeline and evidence ledger. High-value cases include recoverable corruption with valid symbols, unrecoverable corruption, stale or insufficient symbols, invalid ledger paths, partial ledger writes if modeled, and repair-symbol refresh verification after recovery.
design: Build on the inventory; prioritize cases that prove automatic repair and evidence semantics.
acceptance_criteria: Acceptance: add deterministic corpus seeds, fixtures, or golden tests that exercise recoverable corruption with valid symbols, unrecoverable corruption, stale or insufficient symbols, invalid ledger paths, partial ledger writes if modeled, and repair-symbol refresh verification after recovery. Unit tests cover parser/validator behavior, expected error classes, checksum/golden validation, and fixture manifest parsing. Harness or smoke scripts emit shared QA artifacts with seed IDs, corpus version, artifact checksums, stdout/stderr paths, and exact failure classification. Any case too large for CI gets a separate long-campaign bead.
notes: Future self: keep fixtures small and reproducible; avoid relying on random corruption without fixed seeds. New fixtures should teach future users what recovery guarantee is being proven.
close_reason: Added deterministic repair corpus fixture manifest/ledger coverage and package gate cleanup

title: Add mounted-path conformance fixtures for repaired reads
description: Freeze expected behavior for the mounted repair policy: default read-only background scrub detects only, explicit read-only --background-repair with a ledger repairs and refreshes symbols, read-write --background-repair is rejected until serialization exists, and ledger evidence records the lifecycle.
design: This bridges bd-rchk6's implementation into durable conformance evidence.
acceptance_criteria: Acceptance: executable fixtures or mounted scenario goldens cover default read-only background scrub detection-only behavior, explicit read-only --background-repair with ledger repair and symbol refresh, read-write --background-repair rejection until serialization exists, and ledger evidence lifecycle. Unit tests cover fixture parsing, ledger expectation validation, checksum comparison, and expected status classification. Mounted E2E scripts use the permissioned lane when needed and emit shared QA artifacts with CLI flags, exit/status, ledger rows, image checksum/repair outcome, stdout/stderr paths, cleanup status, and precise skip reason if FUSE is unavailable.
notes: Future self: do not weaken the read-write rejection until the serialization workstream closes. This bead should freeze user-visible repair policy, not just internal repair helpers.

title: Wire a deterministic fuzz smoke gate for high-risk parsers
description: Add a small deterministic smoke gate for the parser and metadata surfaces most likely to regress: ext4 superblock/group/inode/extent parsing, btrfs superblock/chunk/tree walking, sparse fixture loading, and repair metadata decoding. The gate should be small enough for routine use while preserving seeds for deeper campaigns.
design: This is not a full fuzz campaign. It is a regression tripwire for high-risk byte parsers.
acceptance_criteria: Acceptance: CI or documented local command runs a fixed corpus/seed set, reports corpus version and seed IDs, fails on panic or unexpected error-class drift, and updates coverage docs with how to extend the corpus. Unit tests cover seed manifest parsing, expected error-class matching, panic classification, timeout handling, and artifact validation. The deterministic smoke script emits shared QA artifacts with command line, seed IDs, corpus checksum, duration, stdout/stderr paths, coverage summary if available, and cleanup status.
notes: Future self: keep runtime bounded and create separate beads for long fuzz campaigns. The smoke gate is a regression tripwire for users, not a substitute for deep fuzzing.

title: Check btrfs compressed extent source slice arithmetic
description: Fresh-eyes follow-up in btrfs compressed extent reads: the compressed regular-extent path computes `extent_offset + extent_delta` and `src_start + copy_len` with unchecked addition before slicing the decompressed payload. Malformed extent metadata near integer limits can panic in debug or wrap in optimized builds. Acceptance: factor/use checked source-slice arithmetic for compressed extents in both read and materialize paths; tests cover offset overflow, slice-end overflow/too-short, and valid slicing; targeted btrfs tests plus fmt/diff/UBS/workspace check/clippy pass.
close_reason: Added validation in parse_extent_data for extent_offset+num_bytes arithmetic overflow and range checks. Tests cover: offset overflow, compressed slice exceeds ram_bytes, uncompressed slice exceeds disk_num_bytes, valid cases, and hole extents.

title: Add btrfs COW leaf-split allocator-failure mutation coverage
description: Apply testing-conformance fallback to the remaining in-memory btrfs COW leaf split failure points. Add deterministic adversarial allocator-failure tests that fail during split child allocation before the new root is published, prove visible contents/root/deferred frees remain unchanged, and update conformance/COVERAGE.md with the coverage evidence.
close_reason: Completed btrfs COW leaf-split allocator-failure mutation coverage; focused leaf-split tests, workspace check, workspace clippy, fmt, diff check, and UBS critical scan passed.

title: Refine bd-rchk7.1 with inventory closeout and stale-note budget gate
description:
Supplemental refinement for bd-rchk7.1. The inventory work should not stop at finding open-ended fuzz and conformance notes; it needs a closeout gate that proves no high-risk vague note survived untriaged, and that any accepted stale note has an explicit budget and owner. This bead uses an explicit dependency instead of a dotted child id until bd-suf84 is fixed.

Required proof before close:
1. Unit/schema tests validate inventory row states: completed_artifact, linked_bead, explicit_non_goal, long_campaign, host_blocked, stale_allowed_until, duplicate_of, and false_positive.
2. The closeout gate fails if any parser, mounted-path, repair, fuzz, golden, xfstests, performance, or README/FEATURE_PARITY note remains in vague/untriaged state.
3. E2E validation scans fixture docs plus a real workspace sample and emits a summary with total matched notes, false positives, linked beads, completed artifacts, stale allowances, non-goals, and failure rows.
4. Logs include scanner version, source file, row id, matched snippet hash, row state, owner/bead/artifact, stale expiry where used, decision reason, output paths, and reproduction command.
5. The gate may allow explicitly budgeted stale notes, but each must have an expiry, owner, user-risk rationale, and linked bead or non-goal; otherwise the inventory cannot close.

title: Fix integer truncation bugs in btree trim_extents and inode timestamp encoding
close_reason: Fixed 3 bugs: (1) trim_extents u64-to-u32 truncation of logical_block at range boundary - replaced bare 'as u32' with clamp_to_u32() saturating helper; (2) trim_extents u64-to-u16 truncation of extent lengths - replaced bare 'as u16' with clamp_to_u16() saturating helper; (3) encode_extra_timestamp nsec bit-shift overflow - added clamp to 999_999_999 before left-shift. 121 btree + 103 inode + 114 extent tests pass.

title: add mounted-path btrfs symlink occupied-destination EEXIST and non-directory-parent ENOTDIR coverage
description: Survey finding: btrfs mounted-path FUSE E2E proves symlink creation and readlink success, but the public refusal contract for an occupied destination name or non-directory parent is not frozen even though ffs-core already returns Exists and NotADirectory. Scope: add btrfs mounted-path probes that assert exact EEXIST for existing names and ENOTDIR for non-directory parents, with unchanged directory contents and no partial mutation. Acceptance: fuse_e2e freezes btrfs mounted-path symlink refusal behavior for these operator-visible cases.

title: Per-core steal threshold must be positive/finite
description: PerCoreDispatcher::should_steal divides by steal_threshold without sanitizing. Non-finite or <=0 values yield NaN/negative and disable or mis-trigger work stealing. Add sanitization + tests.

title: Fix 3 failing ffs-core tests: duplicate name EEXIST + self-rename

title: add FS_IOC_GET_ENCRYPTION_POLICY_EX (v2) ioctl support
description:
Survey finding: ffs-fuse implements FS_IOC_GET_ENCRYPTION_POLICY (v1 policy, 0x400C6615) but not the newer FS_IOC_GET_ENCRYPTION_POLICY_EX (0xC0166616) which supports v2 policies with additional fields (contents_encryption_mode, filenames_encryption_mode, master_key_identifier).

Scope:
- Add FsOps::get_encryption_policy_ex() trait method with default fallback
- Define FS_IOC_GET_ENCRYPTION_POLICY_EX constant and fscrypt_get_policy_ex_arg struct
- Parse on-disk fscrypt_context_v2 from xattr if present
- Wire dispatch in ffs-fuse ioctl handler with size/version validation
- Add unit dispatch tests for v1-only, v2, and unencrypted cases
- Add mounted-path E2E test on encrypted ext4 image if available

Acceptance:
- FS_IOC_GET_ENCRYPTION_POLICY_EX returns v1 or v2 policy depending on on-disk format
- Direct dispatch tests verify struct encoding and version negotiation
- Unencrypted files return ENODATA cleanly
close_reason: FS_IOC_GET_ENCRYPTION_POLICY_EX (v2) already implemented at crates/ffs-fuse/src/lib.rs:1866-1907 with size validation (ERANGE when out_size < header + actual_policy_size), v1/v2 version negotiation, and 5 passing dispatch tests at crates/ffs-fuse/src/lib.rs:5323-5499 (v1 encoding, v2 encoding, short-v1-buffer rejection, v1-sized-for-v2 regression bd-3yy9k, ENODATA propagation). FsOps::get_encryption_policy_ex routed through backend. Stale >48h; closing.

title: add mounted-path ext4 read-only EROFS coverage for create mkdir link and symlink
description: Survey finding: ext4 fuse_e2e proves create, mkdir, hard-link, and symlink success on writable mounts, while ffs-fuse already hard-rejects these mutations with EROFS on read-only mounts. Scope: add mounted-path ext4 probes that attempt create, mkdir, hard_link, and symlink on a read-only mount and assert exact EROFS with unchanged directory contents. Acceptance: ext4 FUSE E2E freezes read-only operator-visible refusal for these entry-creation mutations.
close_reason: Stale in-progress sweep: no commit reference after more than 12 hours; closed per operator instruction so the work can be re-filed or reclaimed cleanly.

title: [MEDIUM] dispatch_ioctl_move_ext_rejection_logs_contract_fields flaky in full suite
description: Test passes in isolation but fails in full workspace suite. Likely tracing subscriber global state interference from concurrent tests. Consider using test isolation or thread-local subscriber.

title: Bound ext4 repair group spec expansion before allocation
description: build_ext4_repair_group_specs reserves capacity from Ext4Superblock::groups_count() before validating the count. A corrupted ext4 superblock can make groups_count() cap at u32::MAX, causing the repair/info path to attempt a huge allocation before any useful error. Add a repair-specific group-count cap before Vec allocation and regression coverage using a malformed ext4 test image.

title: Bitmap CRC: verify kernel bitmap pre-processing before checksumming
description:
Read the Linux kernel source for ext4_block_bitmap_csum_set() and ext4_block_bitmap_csum_verify() to determine:
(a) Whether the kernel zeroes unused tail bits in the bitmap before checksumming. For a group with fewer blocks than blocks_per_group, the tail bits may be set to 1 (indicating 'used') by mkfs but the kernel may ignore/zero them before CRC.
(b) Whether the kernel checksums exactly block_size bytes or clusters_per_group/8 bytes. Our code checksums the full block.
(c) Whether the kernel XORs or masks any fields in the group descriptor before storing the checksum.

SPECIFIC FILES TO CHECK:
- fs/ext4/balloc.c: ext4_block_bitmap_csum_set(), ext4_block_bitmap_csum_verify()
- fs/ext4/bitmap.c: ext4_bitmap_csum()
- include/linux/ext4.h: struct ext4_group_desc layout

ACCEPTANCE: Document the exact kernel pre-processing steps. If any differ from our implementation, fix our code.
close_reason: Kernel source confirmed: no group number in bitmap CRC, length is clusters_per_group/8 for blocks and inodes_per_group/8 for inodes. Fixed in commit 20d65f4.

title: Refine readiness reporting with cross-lane event envelope and correlation ids
description:
Supplemental refinement for bd-un9xt and bd-rchk0.4.3. The readiness report can only aggregate evidence reliably if every lane emits a compatible structured event envelope. Today the planning beads list many lane-specific log fields; this bead makes the shared minimum explicit so cross-lane reporting, proof bundles, release gates, and follow-up creation do not invent incompatible schemas.

Scope: define a versioned event envelope with report id, run id, lane id, scenario id, artifact id, parent correlation id, classification, severity, timestamps, git SHA, host/capability fingerprint, raw-log references, controlling evidence, remediation id, and reproduction command. Lane-specific payloads may extend the envelope but cannot omit the shared fields.

Required proof before close:
1. Unit/schema tests reject events missing envelope version, run id, lane id, scenario id or explicit aggregate marker, classification, severity, timestamp, artifact path/hash, raw-log reference, and reproduction command.
2. Unit tests cover correlation across mounted matrix, xfstests, repair confidence, crash replay, fuzz smoke, performance, proof bundle, and release-gate events, including duplicate ids, stale schema versions, clock-skew tolerance, and missing parent events.
3. E2E smoke aggregates fixture events from at least four lanes and proves the readiness report preserves correlation ids from raw lane artifact to human summary row and machine-readable output.
4. Logs include envelope version, event count, lane ids, rejected event diagnostics, correlation graph summary, output paths, and reproduction command.
5. Future lane beads may add fields, but public readiness claims cannot consume lane artifacts that fail this shared envelope validator.

title: Implement 6 parity features: btrfs decompression, ext4 inline/indirect/bigalloc/ZERO_RANGE, btrfs subvol
close_reason:
Full parity session: implemented 6 new filesystem features to close gaps between FrankenFS and real ext4/btrfs images:

1. btrfs transparent decompression (ZLIB + ZSTD) — compressed extents now readable
2. ext4 inline data read — EXT4_INLINE_DATA_FL files now readable from inode body + xattr
3. ext4 indirect block addressing — pre-extent inodes with direct/indirect/double/triple pointers
4. ext4 bigalloc infrastructure — cluster_ratio field in FsGeometry
5. ext4 FALLOC_FL_ZERO_RANGE — new fallocate mode for zeroing allocated ranges
6. btrfs subvolume mount — configurable subvol_objectid in BtrfsContext

Also fixed during review: compressed extent size overflow, decompressed length validation, inline data xattr parsing (use parse_ibody_xattrs instead of naive offset skip).

473 ffs-core tests pass. All leaf crates clean.

title: Fix br dotted reality-check bead mutation and import loss
description:
During the 2026-05-02 plan-space review, br could read reality-check dotted IDs from JSONL but could not safely mutate several of them. Examples:

- `br --no-db show bd-rchk0.5.6 --json` succeeds.
- `br --no-db update bd-rchk0.5.6 ...` fails with `ISSUE_NOT_FOUND`.
- Fresh temp-DB import from `.beads/issues.jsonl` lists `bd-rchk0.5.6`, but `br show bd-rchk0.5.6 --db <temp>` fails.
- Temp-DB flush refuses to export because the imported DB has 1385 issues while JSONL has 1386, with the missing issue reported as `bd-rchk0.5.6`.
- Project `br doctor --json` still reports DB/JSONL count mismatch (`db`: 1286, `jsonl`: 1386), and `br sync --import-only --json` times out before repairing it.

Root-cause hypothesis: ID-prefix or dotted-child handling is losing or shadowing a parent issue such as `bd-rchk0.5.6` when sibling/child IDs such as `bd-rchk0.5.6.1` also exist. This blocks safe plan-space maintenance because `br update`/`br dep add` cannot revise the affected beads without risking a stale or lossy export.

Required proof before close:
1. Unit tests cover exact lookup, update, dependency add, import, export, and no-db mutation for parent/child dotted IDs including `bd-rchk0.5.6` and `bd-rchk0.5.6.1`, plus nearby IDs such as `bd-rchk0.5.10` and `bd-rchk0.5.10.1`.
2. Regression tests prove JSONL import into a fresh DB preserves the full issue count and that flush refuses no valid record after no-op import, update, and dependency operations.
3. E2E script starts from a copied fixture JSONL, runs `br list`, `br show`, `br update`, `br dep add`, `br dep cycles`, `br sync --import-only`, and `br sync --flush-only`, then verifies issue count, exact IDs, dependency graph, and JSONL parseability.
4. Logs preserve command lines, DB path, JSONL path, before/after counts, failing ID if any, stdout/stderr, exit codes, and reproduction command; no test relies on `--force` export.
5. After the fix, the reality-check plan beads can be revised through normal `br` commands without direct JSONL editing, stale DB loss, or child-ID shadowing.

5. Logs include the br version, source JSONL hash, command transcript, before/after edge list, cycle-check output, and reproduction command.

title: btrfs core: resolve named subvolume and snapshot mount selections
description:
Survey finding: `FEATURE_PARITY.md` and `README.md` both claim btrfs subvolume mount support, but `OpenFs::from_device` still hardcodes `BTRFS_FS_TREE_OBJECTID` and the operator-facing CLI rejects `--subvol/--snapshot`. This bead owns the core selection logic in `ffs-core`.

Scope:
- Extend `OpenOptions` with an explicit btrfs mount selection contract (default root, named subvolume, named snapshot, and optionally numeric objectid if that simplifies diagnostics).
- Walk the root tree and resolve the requested selection to the mounted `subvol_objectid` plus `subvol_root_dirid`.
- Return deterministic `FfsError` variants for missing selections, ambiguous names, and selection/type mismatches.
- Keep default-root behavior unchanged when no selection is requested.

Acceptance:
- `OpenFs::open_with_options` can mount a non-default subvolume/snapshot without post-open mutation hacks.
- Synthetic and/or kernel-reference tests prove correct resolution for default root, named subvolume, named snapshot, and missing target cases.
- Structured diagnostics include the requested selector and resolved objectid/root dirid.

title: ffs-cli: wire btrfs --subvol and --snapshot mount flags to OpenOptions
description:
Survey finding: `ffs-cli` advertises `--subvol`/`--snapshot` on `mount`, but `validate_btrfs_mount_selection()` hard-rejects both flags even though the underlying codebase already has internal btrfs subvolume context machinery. This bead owns the operator-facing CLI plumbing.

Scope:
- Remove the unconditional rejection path in `crates/ffs-cli/src/main.rs`.
- Thread the requested selection into `OpenOptions` and preserve all existing ext4 behavior.
- Emit structured startup/rejection logs that show requested selector, resolved selection type, and failure class.
- Replace the current `mount_rejects_subvol_flag` / `mount_rejects_snapshot_flag` tests with positive routing tests and deterministic failure diagnostics for nonexistent selectors.

Acceptance:
- `ffs mount ... --subvol <name>` and `ffs mount ... --snapshot <name>` reach `OpenFs::open_with_options` instead of bailing in argument validation.
- Missing selectors fail with clear, stable user-facing errors.
- CLI tests cover default-root, explicit subvolume, explicit snapshot, and nonexistent selection cases.

Scope:

- Assert mounted-root `getattr`/`lookup`/`readdir`/`read` behavior is scoped to the selected tree, not the default root.
- Update `FEATURE_PARITY.md` and README wording so the operator-facing support claim matches the tested surface exactly.
- Ensure test output/logs emit machine-parseable scenario identifiers for success and failure cases.

Acceptance:
- There is deterministic coverage for mounting a named subvolume and a named snapshot via the public mount path.
- README and `FEATURE_PARITY.md` no longer overclaim relative to the tested operator surface.
- Coverage artifacts make it clear whether support is internal-only or operator-visible.

title: Refine soak campaigns with stop conditions and flake root-cause classification
description:
Supplemental refinement for bd-rchk0.5.9. Long-running soak and canary campaigns are only useful if they have deterministic stop conditions, bounded resource budgets, and honest flake classification. This bead prevents endurance runs from becoming vague pass/fail anecdotes or silently hiding product failures as environmental noise.

Scope: define campaign stop conditions, heartbeat schema, resource-budget handling, flake/product/host classification, stale-baseline quarantine, and follow-up creation rules for recurring failures. Campaigns must remain bounded enough for CI/RCH smoke while preserving enough detail for nightly/manual lanes.

Required proof before close:
1. Unit tests cover campaign profile parsing, duration and iteration bounds, heartbeat cadence, failure-threshold evaluation, stop-condition precedence, resource-budget exhaustion, stale baseline rejection, and artifact aggregation.
2. Unit tests cover classification for product regression, host capability skip, infrastructure error, timeout, resource exhaustion, known quarantined flake, new recurring flake, and inconclusive result.
3. E2E smoke runs a bounded campaign using representative workload rows and emits heartbeat, final summary, raw logs, and machine-readable artifacts.
4. Logs include campaign id, profile, git SHA, host/kernel/FUSE capability, workload ids, seeds, start/end time, heartbeat summaries, resource usage, stop reason, classification, artifact paths, cleanup status, and reproduction command.
5. Recurring flakes and product failures require a linked follow-up bead or an explicit quarantine entry with owner, expiry, user-risk rationale, reproduction data, and release-gate impact.
close_reason: Completed: soak/canary manifest now validates stop-condition precedence, bounded resource budgets, stale-baseline handling, host/infrastructure/timeout/product/root-cause classifications, known-vs-new recurring flake quarantine metadata, release-gate impact, and E2E dry-run checks

title: [FUZZ-TARGET] add ffs-fuse path-encoding mount-path harness
description:
Apply /testing-fuzzing to the raw path/name boundary in `ffs-fuse`. Build a harness that constructs `OsStr`/raw-byte names with `std::os::unix::ffi::OsStrExt`, then drives `lookup`, `mkdir`, `create`, `rename`, `symlink`, `readlink`, and `readdir` through the existing test doubles and mounted-path helper surfaces. The goal is to freeze behavior for non-UTF8 names, separator-like bytes, empty names, overlong names, and path-encoding corner cases before they become mounted-path regressions.

Primary targets:
- `crates/ffs-fuse/src/lib.rs:1970-2027` mkdir/rename dispatch
- `crates/ffs-fuse/src/lib.rs:2244-2445` lookup/readdir/symlink entry points
- current mounted-path helpers in `crates/ffs-harness/tests/fuse_e2e.rs` that already exercise `OsStrExt` and raw-byte name behavior

Mutation classes:
- non-UTF8 path bytes
- embedded slash / NUL-adjacent byte sequences where constructible
- empty, dot, dotdot, PATH_MAX-adjacent, and rename target/source combinations
- readlink/symlink targets that preserve raw bytes across mount-path lookup and readdir

Acceptance:
- new fuzz target added under `fuzz/fuzz_targets/` and registered in `fuzz/Cargo.toml`
- corpus seeded from current fscrypt-nokey raw-name and rename/readlink mounted-path cases
- malformed path bytes never panic and always classify to stable errno/error outcomes

title: Refine remediation catalog with data-safety severity and dead-end prevention
description:
Supplemental refinement for bd-rchk0.5.12. A remediation catalog can still fail users if entries say what happened but not whether their data is safe, whether mutation occurred, what command to run next, or who owns the unresolved state. This bead adds data-safety severity and dead-end prevention as hard catalog requirements.

Scope: define remediation entries around user decision-making: data-safety impact, mutation status, confidence state, immediate next command, safe retry policy, escalation path, owning bead/subsystem, docs target, and release-gate effect. Every fail/skip/warn/error state should either have a concrete action or an explicit non-goal rationale.

Required proof before close:
1. Unit/schema tests reject remediation rows missing data-safety severity, mutation status, operator action, safe retry policy, owning bead/subsystem, docs target, reproduction command, artifact requirements, or release-gate effect.
2. Unit tests cover product failure, host capability skip, unsupported operation, stale artifact, security refusal, unsafe repair refusal, low-confidence repair, noisy performance, missing proof lane, inconclusive oracle conflict, and pass-with-experimental-caveat entries.
3. E2E smoke feeds fixture outcomes through the catalog and proves JSON, CLI/TUI text, proof-bundle summary, and release-gate output all contain the same remediation id and no dead-end fail/skip/warn state.
4. Logs include outcome code, remediation id, data-safety severity, mutation status, action command or explicit non-goal, owner/bead, source artifact hash, docs target, output paths, and reproduction command.
5. Release gates must fail closed if any user-visible failure, skip, security refusal, unsupported feature, stale evidence, or unsafe repair state lacks a remediation entry with concrete next action or explicit non-goal rationale.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: FEATURE_PARITY mapping: This covers 'btrfs transaction parity' (currently ❌). Maps to transaction.c. When complete: flip to ✅ in FEATURE_PARITY.md.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: BTRFS TRANSACTION MODEL: btrfs transactions group multiple COW tree updates into an atomic unit. Key concepts: (1) transaction handle (acquire on begin, release on end), (2) delayed items (buffered in memory until transaction commit), (3) transaction commit writes all dirty trees atomically. Our MVCC layer maps naturally onto this: MVCC transaction ≈ btrfs transaction.

comments.author: MossyKnoll
comments.text: Progress update (2026-02-13): implemented  in  with MVCC-backed begin/commit/abort, staged tree-root metadata records, delayed-ref flush before commit, allocation/free bookkeeping, and structured tracing. Updated  row for btrfs transaction parity to 🟡 with remaining gap note. title: [HIGH] open_fs_btrfs_walk_root_tree smoke assertions are stale against default-root mount semantics
description:
# Review finding

The `build_btrfs_image()` smoke suite is failing early today on missing default-root resolution, but the test body behind `open_fs_btrfs_walk_root_tree` is also stale against the current mount contract. It still asserts that the walked root tree contains exactly one item with `objectid=256` and an 8-byte dummy payload.

## Evidence
- Failing suite today: `tests::open_fs_btrfs_walk_root_tree` is in the red set from `cargo test --workspace --all-targets`, but it currently aborts before its assertions because `OpenFs::from_device(..., OpenOptions::default())` cannot resolve a default-root `ROOT_ITEM`.
- Test assertions: `crates/ffs-core/src/lib.rs:22736-22748` expect `items.len() == 1`, `items[0].key.objectid == 256`, `item_type == 132`, and dummy payload bytes.
- Current selection logic opens btrfs under `BtrfsMountSelection::DefaultRoot` when no selector is supplied: `crates/ffs-core/src/lib.rs:2395-2428`. Any fixture fix that adds the missing default-root `ROOT_ITEM` for objectid 5 or otherwise models current semantics will likely invalidate the old single-item/objectid-256 expectation unless the test explicitly opens a non-default selection.

## Why this is high severity
This is a likely second workspace-red failure masked by the earlier `NotFound` abort. If the fixture is repaired without aligning this test, the gate will still stay red.

## Repro
Current gate: `rch exec -- env CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/tmp/rch_target_frankenfs_cod cargo test --workspace --all-targets`

## Expected fix direction
Either:
- make the smoke test open an explicit non-default/root-tree-focused selection and keep the current objectid-256 assertions, or
- update the fixture and assertions to reflect the default-root mount contract and the resulting walked root-tree inventory.
Do not stop at making `OpenFs::from_device()` succeed; prove the post-open root-tree assertions still match the intended contract.
close_reason: Completed: open_fs_btrfs_walk_root_tree now asserts the parseable default-root contract and passes in the targeted btrfs smoke suite

title: Refine deterministic fuzz smoke with seed provenance minimization and quarantine policy
description:
Supplemental refinement for bd-rchk7.4. A deterministic fuzz smoke gate is useful only if seed provenance, minimization, timeout handling, and quarantine rules are explicit. Otherwise the smoke gate can become flaky, silently drop hard seeds, or accept error-class drift without a reproducible follow-up.

Scope: define a seed manifest with source, provenance, parser surface, expected classification, minimization status, timeout budget, resource budget, quarantine state, owning bead, and corpus checksum. The smoke gate should be bounded but strict about panic, hang, timeout, and unexpected classification drift.

Required proof before close:
1. Unit/schema tests reject seed rows missing seed id, source/provenance, target parser, corpus checksum, expected classification, timeout budget, resource budget, minimization status, quarantine status, and owning bead for quarantined seeds.
2. Unit tests cover panic classification, timeout classification, resource-cap classification, expected error drift, minimized seed replay, non-minimized seed follow-up requirement, duplicate seed ids, stale corpus checksum, and quarantine expiry.
3. E2E smoke runs a fixed seed subset, emits deterministic pass/fail/skip/error artifacts, and proves every failure has a minimized replay command or a follow-up/quarantine entry with owner and expiry.
4. Logs include corpus version, seed id, seed hash, provenance, parser target, expected classification, observed classification, duration, resource counters where available, minimization status, quarantine id, artifact paths, and reproduction command.
5. Release gates and corpus docs cannot count fuzz smoke as healthy if any high-risk parser seed is missing provenance, silently quarantined, timed out without owner, or changed classification without a linked bead.

title: External journal: compute journal segments from external device geometry
description:
When replaying an external journal, we need to compute JournalSegment entries. For an internal journal, segments come from the journal inode's extent tree. For an external journal, the entire device is the journal:
- JBD2 superblock occupies block 0.
- Journal blocks span from block s_first_log_block to block s_maxlen.
- Each journal block = one filesystem block (block size from JBD2 superblock).

Implement a function external_journal_segments(jbd2_sb: &Jbd2Superblock) -> Vec<JournalSegment> that produces the segment list for an external journal device.

ACCEPTANCE: Function correctly computes segments. Test with a synthetic JBD2 superblock.
close_reason: Added Jbd2Superblock::external_journal_segment() that computes JournalSegment from first_log_block and max_len. Commit 7a8ede4.

3. E2E/harness smoke runs a bounded calibration subset and emits per-case JSON plus aggregate confidence summary without requiring the long campaign by default.
4. Logs include corpus id, seed, original image hash, corrupted image hash or manifest hash, repair-symbol budget, decoder parameters, expected outcome, observed outcome, confidence value, threshold decision, ledger row ids, cleanup status, artifact paths, and reproduction command.
5. Any threshold promoted to release gates must cite this calibration artifact; thresholds without enough evidence remain experimental and link to a measurement bead.

title: add ffs-core fuzz target for ext4 orphan-recovery mount path
description:
Apply /testing-fuzzing to the ext4 orphan-recovery mount path in `crates/ffs-core/src/lib.rs`.

## Target surface
- `OpenFs::from_device(...)` on ext4 images that advertise orphan recovery work
- `maybe_recover_ext4_orphans()` at `crates/ffs-core/src/lib.rs:2803`
- `collect_ext4_orphan_list_lenient()` at `crates/ffs-core/src/lib.rs:2943`
- `clear_ext4_orphan_state()` at `crates/ffs-core/src/lib.rs:3000`

## Why this matters
This path walks attacker-controlled inode chains (`s_last_orphan` + inode `dtime` links), tolerates cycles/out-of-range heads, mutates allocator state, and clears superblock state on success. It is classic fuzz material: bounded traversal, corrupt pointers, unreadable inodes, and stateful mutation in one mount-time recovery path.

## Scope
- Add a dedicated `fuzz` target that feeds adversarial ext4-like images into `OpenFs::from_device` / recovery helpers.
- Seed from existing orphan-recovery unit fixtures and mutate:
  - `s_last_orphan`
  - orphan flag bits
  - inode table pointers
  - `dtime` links (cycles, out-of-range, self-loop, deep chain)
  - extent/inode metadata for linked orphans
- Assert:
  - no panic / no hang
  - traversal remains bounded
  - deterministic outcome for identical input
  - if recovery returns stats, `processed() <= scanned` and counts remain internally consistent
  - clearing orphan state never writes past image bounds

## Acceptance
- New fuzz target registered in `fuzz/Cargo.toml`
- Seed corpus checked in
- Crashers minimized and promoted to regression tests
close_reason: Covered by fuzz/fuzz_targets/fuzz_openfs_journal_replay.rs (29KB umbrella harness): exercises OpenFs::open_with_options with Ext4JournalReplayMode, ExternalJournalInfo, Ext4FastCommitReplayEvidence, EXT4_ORPHAN_FS, EXT4_LAST_ORPHAN_OFFSET, JOURNAL_FAST_COMMIT_BLOCK, WalRecoveryReport — covers internal/external journal, fast-commit, orphan cleanup, and MVCC WAL replay through the real mount/open boundary as bd-p8c4q's acceptance requires.

title: Reject unsorted btrfs tree item keys
description: parse_leaf_items and parse_internal_items validate btrfs node capacity and payload bounds but do not reject item/key_ptr tables whose BtrfsKey order is not strictly increasing. Btrfs tree nodes require ordered keys; accepting unsorted or duplicate keys can poison tree walks and lookup/range logic built on the parser. Add strict key-order validation for leaf and internal node parsers plus regression coverage.
close_reason: Completed: btrfs leaf/internal item parsers now reject non-strictly-increasing keys with regression coverage.

title: Refine ambition evidence matrix with stale-reference and consumer-contract checks
description:
Supplemental refinement for bd-rchk0.5.10.1. The ambition evidence matrix must be an executable control plane, not a report that can drift. This bead requires stale-reference checks and consumer-contract tests so release gates, proof bundles, remediation catalogs, and README status updates all consume the same matrix rows.

Scope: define matrix consumer contracts, stale-reference validation, versioned vocabulary, required artifact links, and downgrade semantics for missing evidence. Every row should explain how it affects a user-facing claim and which consumer is responsible for enforcing that effect.

Required proof before close:
1. Unit/schema tests reject matrix rows with missing bead id, missing user-risk mapping, stale bead reference, unknown consumer name, missing release-claim effect, missing artifact path, missing log-field declaration, or unsupported status value.
2. Unit tests cover consumer-contract validation for proof bundle ingestion, release-gate evaluation, remediation catalog generation, README/FEATURE_PARITY status rendering, and follow-up bead creation.
3. E2E/script smoke builds a matrix report from real tracker rows, verifies referenced beads exist, emits consumer-specific summaries, and fails closed on an injected stale bead id or missing artifact link.
4. Logs include matrix version, source bead ids, consumer versions, stale-reference checks, missing-field diagnostics, downgrade decisions, generated artifact paths, and reproduction command.
5. The matrix can express validated, partial, blocked, stale, not-applicable, and intentionally deferred states without letting any P1 user-risk claim appear stronger than the underlying evidence.
close_reason: Completed: added stale-reference validation, consumer contracts, downgrade decisions, artifact fail-closed checks, E2E coverage, and focused RCH validation.

title: External journal: wire replay through external device ByteDevice
description:
Wire the external journal replay path:
1. Open the external journal image as a FileByteDevice.
2. Read journal blocks from the external device.
3. Feed them to replay_jbd2_segments (existing function) with the DATA device as the write target.
4. After successful replay, clear needs_recovery on the data device.

The key insight: journal blocks are READ from the external device, but replayed blocks are WRITTEN to the data device. The existing replay infrastructure accepts a BlockDevice for writes — just pass the data device's block adapter.

DEPENDS ON: Journal segment computation (bd-vdk6).

ACCEPTANCE: A test with a synthetic data FS + dirty external journal verifies that replay writes land on the data device and needs_recovery is cleared.
close_reason: Already implemented by concurrent agent: ExternalJournalReplayAdapter reads journal blocks from external device, writes replayed blocks to data device. UUID/block-size validation, JBD2 replay, superblock re-read after replay. Test open_fs_replays_external_journal_transaction passes.

title: Refine workload corpus with user-risk and proof-consumer coverage matrix
description:
Supplemental refinement for bd-rchk0.5.7.1. The workload corpus needs to be more than a bag of scenarios: each entry should explain what user risk it represents, which proof systems consume it, and what evidence a future closeout must produce. This prevents realistic workloads from becoming disconnected fixtures that do not support release claims.

Scope: define a coverage matrix for corpus entries that maps scenario id to README/runtime claim, filesystem flavor, operation class, user-risk category, required host capability, proof consumers, unit-test obligations, E2E/fuzz/soak obligations, expected logs, expected artifacts, and explicit non-applicability rationale.

Required proof before close:
1. Unit/schema tests reject corpus rows missing stable id, user-risk category, filesystem scope, capability tag, proof-consumer list, required log fields, required artifact fields, and explicit unsupported/non-goal rationale where applicable.
2. Unit tests cover duplicate scenario ids, stale proof-consumer names, orphaned high-risk categories, invalid capability tags, and rows that claim user-visible coverage without an E2E or long-campaign proof lane.
3. E2E/script smoke consumes representative rows across at least core parser, mounted-path, repair, and long-campaign consumers or emits structured host skips for unavailable lanes.
4. Logs include matrix version, row ids, selected scenarios, consumer names, capability decisions, generated artifact paths, pass/fail/skip/error state, and reproduction command.
5. Release gates and proof bundles can summarize corpus coverage by user risk and fail closed when a P1/P2 risk row has no active proof consumer.
close_reason: Completed: added workload corpus coverage matrix and validation gates

title: [FUZZ-TARGET] add ffs-fuse ioctl argument harness
description:
Apply /testing-fuzzing to the `ffs-fuse` ioctl dispatch boundary. Build a fuzz harness around `dispatch_ioctl(...)` / `dispatch_ioctl_for_testing(...)` that mutates `cmd`, `in_data`, `out_size`, `fh`, `caller_pid`, and read-only/writeable mount options while using the existing recorder/test doubles. The goal is to stress the argument parsers and ioctl response encoding without needing a live mount.

Primary targets:
- `crates/ffs-fuse/src/lib.rs:1213-1353` FIEMAP parsing/encoding helpers
- `crates/ffs-fuse/src/lib.rs:1241-1300` move_ext / u32 / flag parsers
- `crates/ffs-fuse/src/lib.rs:1536-1833` ioctl dispatch for FIEMAP, GET/SETFLAGS, GET/SETVERSION, fscrypt, move_ext, fslabel, and btrfs ioctl cases

Mutation classes:
- short/oversized ioctl buffers
- inconsistent `out_size` vs payload sizes
- garbage command numbers / incompatible flag bits
- malformed fscrypt `_EX`, move_ext, FIEMAP, and fslabel argument layouts

Acceptance:
- new fuzz target added under `fuzz/fuzz_targets/` and registered in `fuzz/Cargo.toml`
- harness proves no panic across malformed ioctl argument shapes
- any crashers become regression tests next to the current unit coverage

This leaves create-vs-replace errno parity unproven on the public mount surface.

Scope:
- Reuse or extend the mounted-path xattr helper to issue flagged `setxattr` calls on a real btrfs FUSE mount.
- Assert `XATTR_CREATE` rejects existing names with `EEXIST` while preserving the original value.
- Assert `XATTR_REPLACE` rejects missing names with the stable missing-xattr errno (`ENODATA`/`ENOATTR`) and does not create the attribute as a side effect.
- Cover at least one regular-file path and keep the assertions independent of ext4-specific behavior.

Acceptance:
- btrfs mounted-path tests prove create/replace errno semantics and no-side-effect guarantees.
- Any transport-level skips are explicit and distinguishable from backend regressions.

title: Refine mounted repair conformance with read-only mutation boundaries and rw refusal side effects
description:
Supplemental refinement for bd-rchk7.3. Mounted repair policy fixtures should prove the exact mutation boundary: default read-only scrub detects only, explicit read-only repair may mutate only the controlled image/ledger path when allowed, and read-write background repair must refuse without partial client-visible side effects until serialization is proven.

Scope: define mounted repair conformance scenarios that check image hashes, ledger rows, symbol refresh, mount options, CLI exit status, visible files, no host-path mutation, and reopen behavior for detection-only, read-only repair, read-write refusal, missing ledger, stale ledger, and host capability skip cases.

Required proof before close:
1. Unit/schema tests reject mounted repair fixture rows missing mount mode, background repair flag state, ledger path policy, expected mutation scope, image hash expectation, visible namespace expectation, ledger expectation, and cleanup policy.
2. Unit tests cover default detection-only, explicit read-only repair allowed, missing ledger refusal, stale ledger refusal, read-write repair refusal, unsupported flavor refusal, host capability skip, and cleanup failure classification.
3. Mounted E2E smoke uses the permissioned lane where available and proves read-write refusal leaves file size, directory entries, xattrs, ledger rows, and image hash in the expected state.
4. Logs include scenario id, CLI flags, mount options, image hash before/after, ledger rows before/after, repair classification, expected mutation scope, observed mutation scope, host paths touched, cleanup status, artifact paths, and reproduction command.
5. Release gates cannot promote mounted automatic repair readiness unless detection-only, read-only repair, and read-write refusal side-effect fixtures are all represented with fresh evidence.
