title: Refine operator repair workflow with dry-run safety and rollback preflight
description:
Supplemental refinement for bd-rchk0.5.8. The operator recovery drill should prove that automatic repair is safe to use for real, not just that a repair algorithm can mutate a fixture. The most important user-facing invariant is that no irreversible image mutation happens until preflight, dry-run evidence, backup/snapshot guidance, confidence threshold, and rollback/refusal policy all agree.

Scope: specify a dry-run-first repair workflow with explicit mutation preconditions, rollback preflight, backup or copy-on-write image guidance, confidence display, refusal modes, post-repair verification, and artifact-backed operator messaging. Keep local/default behavior non-destructive while making opt-in mutation paths fully logged.

Required proof before close:
1. Unit tests cover repair-plan validation, mutation-precondition evaluation, missing-backup refusal, low-confidence refusal, unsupported-image refusal, dry-run rendering, rollback-plan rendering, verification summary rendering, and structured diagnostic output.
2. Unit tests prove mutation commands cannot run when preflight is stale, artifact schema is stale, image hash changed after dry-run, rollback path is unavailable, or operator confirmation does not match the planned image hash.
3. E2E scripts cover detection-only, dry-run-success, refused-unsafe-repair, stale-preflight refusal, and one opt-in mutating repair where the lane supports it.
4. Logs include exact commands, image paths and hashes, corruption manifest, repair confidence, dry-run plan, mutation preconditions, operator-facing warning text, rollback target, verification result, cleanup status, and reproduction command.
5. README/operator docs can truthfully distinguish detection-only, dry-run, validated mutation, experimental mutation, and unsupported cases without weakening the automatic-repair goal.

title: Refine bd-rchk0.5.1.1 with invariant oracle soundness calibration
description:
Supplemental refinement for bd-rchk0.5.1.1. The invariant oracle needs soundness calibration so it does not become a second implementation that can silently disagree with FrankenFS or emit convincing false alarms. This bead focuses on proving the oracle itself is trustworthy.

Required proof before close:
1. Unit tests cover oracle-state construction, reducer/shrinker determinism, legal/illegal operation classification, unsupported operation reporting, false-positive guard fixtures, intentionally failing fixtures, and model version compatibility.
2. Calibration tests run the same trace through at least one real FrankenFS core or harness surface and the oracle, then classify mismatches as model_bug, production_bug, unsupported_surface, fixture_bug, or harness_bug.
3. E2E/integration smoke emits a minimized failing trace from a seeded fixture and proves replay is deterministic from the saved artifact without access to hidden process state.
4. Logs include model version, trace id, seed, operation index, pre-state hash, post-state hash, expected invariant id, observed invariant result, classification, shrink steps, artifact paths, and reproduction command.
5. Public proof-bundle consumers must reject oracle artifacts with unknown model version, missing classification, missing replay command, or non-minimized failures lacking a follow-up bead.
close_reason: Completed: invariant oracle report validation now carries model-versioned soundness metadata, state hashes, expected-vs-observed invariant results, classification checks, shrink evidence, non-minimized follow-up requirements, and --report consumer validation. title: ffs-core write_inode holds alloc lock across block_dev.sync
description: In crates/ffs-core/src/lib.rs (around line 5230), the Ext4 write path acquires the alloc_mutex lock to pass alloc.geo and alloc.groups to ffs_inode::write_inode, but fails to drop it before calling block_dev.sync(cx)?. This holds a critical subsystem lock across a potentially slow device sync operation, causing unnecessary contention and triggering clippy's significant_drop_tightening lint. The lock should be explicitly dropped immediately after write_inode returns.
close_reason: Scoped the alloc lock inside persist_ext4_inode_for_testing so write_inode completes before flush_mvcc_to_device; verified with rch cargo test -p ffs-core write_compressed_uncompressed_fallback_preserves_sparse_tail -- --nocapture and rch cargo check -p ffs-core --all-targets.

title: Cap btrfs decompression target size before allocation
description: Btrfs regular compressed extent reads cap disk_num_bytes and ram_bytes before decompression, but inline compressed extents pass ram_bytes directly into btrfs_decompress, whose zlib/LZO paths reserve output capacity before decoding. A malformed inline extent with huge ram_bytes can trigger oversized allocation before validation. Add a shared 128MiB decompression target cap at the decompressor boundary, reuse the constant at existing regular-extent guards, and add regression coverage that oversized targets are rejected before allocation.
close_reason: Capped btrfs decompression targets at the decompressor boundary before output allocation, reused the 128MiB limit for regular compressed extent guards, and added an oversized-target regression; fmt/check/clippy/focused test/UBS passed.

title: Investigate and fix ext4 bitmap CRC32C mismatch against real mkfs.ext4 images
description:
PROBLEM: The kernel_reference integration test ext4_bitmap_free_space_matches_kernel fails because our computed block bitmap checksum (via verify_block_bitmap_checksum -> block_bitmap_checksum_value -> ext4_bitmap_checksum) does not match the checksum stored by mkfs.ext4 1.47.2 on real images.

EVIDENCE: On a fresh 8MB ext4 image (mkfs.ext4 -L test -b 4096 -q -O ^has_journal), seed=0xba6605a8, our code computes 0xcd45611c but the stored checksum is 0xb8339b40. The ext4_chksum bridge function (!crc32c_append(!raw, data)) is verified correct for known test vectors (b'hello' produces the expected result). The GDT field offsets (bg_block_bitmap_csum_lo at 0x18, bg_block_bitmap_csum_hi at 0x38) match the kernel struct. The csum_seed derivation (from s_checksum_seed when CSUM_SEED is set) is verified correct.

POSSIBLE ROOT CAUSES:
(a) The crc32c crate 0.6.8 may have a hardware-acceleration edge case on this CPU (the test passes for small inputs but may diverge on 4096-byte inputs).
(b) There may be a subtle difference in how the kernel chains CRC32C for bitmap checksums vs how we do it (e.g., the kernel might zero some bits in the bitmap before checksumming, or exclude the last N bytes).
(c) The group descriptor checksum field offset may differ between mkfs.ext4 versions.
(d) There's a SECOND bitmap checksum function (compute_bitmap_checksum at line 1743) that uses csum_seed^group (XOR) instead of chaining — this function is wrong but verify_block_bitmap_checksum doesn't use it. However, it may have been copy-pasted from or to somewhere unexpected.

INVESTIGATION PLAN:
1. Add a test that creates a fresh ext4 image with mkfs.ext4, reads the raw bitmap bytes, and computes our CRC step-by-step, printing intermediate values.
2. Cross-validate by computing the same CRC using a second Rust CRC32C implementation (e.g., crc32c-hw or manual software CRC).
3. If the crate is correct, hexdump the raw GDT bytes and verify our parser reads the stored checksum from the right offset.
4. Check if mkfs.ext4 zeroes any bitmap bits before checksumming (e.g., unused tail bits beyond blocks_per_group).
5. Check the kernel source for ext4_block_bitmap_csum_set() to see if it modifies the bitmap before checksumming.

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- ext4_bitmap_free_space_matches_kernel test passes on a fresh mkfs.ext4 image.
- The root cause is documented in a code comment.
- If the crc32c crate has a bug, switch to an alternative or add a workaround.
- The wrong compute_bitmap_checksum function (XOR-based) is either removed or clearly marked as deprecated.
close_reason: Root cause: (1) group_number was chained into bitmap CRC but kernel doesn't do this, (2) checksum length was full block instead of clusters/inodes_per_group/8. Fixed in commits 20d65f4 and 7f4d6eb. Kernel reference test now passes.

title: Succinct structures for free-space bitmaps (Graveyard Entry 7.1)
description:
# Succinct structures for free-space bitmaps (Graveyard Entry 7.1)

## GOAL
Replace traditional free-space bitmaps with succinct data structures (rank/select bitmaps) that support O(1) rank and select queries while using near-optimal space.

## BACKGROUND
From Alien CS Graveyard 7.1: Succinct bitmaps (Jacobson 1989, Clark 1996) store bitmaps in n + o(n) bits while supporting:
- rank(i): count set bits in positions [0, i) — O(1)
- select(k): find position of k-th set bit — O(1)

For free-space bitmaps, this enables:
- O(1) "how many free blocks in range?" (rank)
- O(1) "find k-th free block" (select) — replaces linear scan for allocation

## DESIGN
- Two-level rank structure: superblocks (every 2^16 bits) + blocks (every 2^9 bits)
- Select via rank binary search or Vigna's select structure
- Space overhead: ~6.25% over raw bitmap (vs 0% for plain bitmap, but queries are O(n))
- Integration: replace ffs-ext4 block bitmap and inode bitmap

## APPLICABILITY
- Optimization for large filesystems where bitmap scans are expensive
- Most impactful when block groups are large or filesystem is >90% full
- Could also accelerate mballoc buddy allocator queries

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] Succinct bitmap with O(1) rank and select
- [ ] Space overhead < 10% over raw bitmap
- [ ] Benchmark: allocation scan time vs plain bitmap
- [ ] Drop-in replacement for block/inode bitmaps
acceptance_criteria: Succinct bitmap with O(1) rank and select operations. Space overhead below 10% over raw bitmap. Two-level rank structure: superblocks (every 2^16 bits) plus blocks (every 2^9 bits). Drop-in replacement for block and inode bitmaps via BlockBitmap trait. Benchmark: allocation scan time vs plain bitmap at 50%, 80%, 95% fullness. Benchmark: rank/select latency vs linear scan. Unit tests: rank correctness for all-zeros, all-ones, random patterns. Unit tests: select correctness for sparse and dense bitmaps. Unit tests: round-trip serialization/deserialization. Property test: rank(select(k)) == k for all valid k.

title: [HIGH] build_btrfs_readdir_image fixture leaves are missing tree_block_csum stamping
description:
# Review finding

`build_btrfs_readdir_image()` builds synthetic root-tree and fs-tree leaves for the readdir suite, but it never stamps `tree_block_csum` on either leaf.

## Evidence
- Builder: `crates/ffs-core/src/lib.rs:23165-23311`
- It writes the root-tree leaf at `root_tree_logical = 0x4000` and the fs-tree leaf at `fs_tree_logical = 0x20000`.
- Unlike the fsops fixtures, this builder never invokes the existing checksum helper (`stamp_btrfs_test_tree_block_crc32c`), and it switches to `nodesize = 16384`, so both synthetic tree blocks must be stamped consistently with the parser’s current checksum expectations.
- The workspace rerun still fails all readdir-specific consumers with `Format("invalid field: tree_block_csum (CRC32C checksum mismatch)")`:
  - `walk_btrfs_dir_entry_items_includes_dir_index_items`
  - `btrfs_readdir_mixed_types`
  - `btrfs_readdir_empty_directory_returns_dot_dotdot`
  - `btrfs_readdir_sorted_by_index`
  - `btrfs_readdir_offset_pagination`
  - `btrfs_readdir_special_characters_in_names`

## Why this is high severity
This is a second stale synthetic-fixture family independent of the minimal open/root-tree fixture. It blocks all of the new btrfs directory-iteration regression coverage.

## Repro
`rch exec -- env CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/tmp/rch_target_frankenfs_cod cargo test --workspace --all-targets`

## Expected fix direction
Stamp checksums for both generated leaves after all items/payloads are finalized, with nodesize-aware coverage for the 16 KiB fs-tree case.
close_reason: Fixed: build_btrfs_readdir_image at crates/ffs-core/src/lib.rs:23274 now stamps CRC32C on both root-tree leaf and fs-tree leaf at lines 23421-23424 with nodesize-aware slicing. All cited readdir consumers (walk_btrfs_dir_entry_items_includes_dir_index_items, btrfs_readdir_* family) pass under rch workspace run (17 readdir tests green).

title: JBD2 descriptor/revoke block checksum omits 4-byte tail instead of zeroing it
description: In crates/ffs-journal/src/lib.rs, verify_jbd2_block_checksum handles JBD2_BLOCKTYPE_DESCRIPTOR and JBD2_BLOCKTYPE_REVOKE by computing crc32c over &block[..bs - 4]. However, Linux jbd2_descriptor_block_csum_verify temporarily zeroes the 4-byte tail checksum field and then computes the checksum over the *entire* block size (j->j_blocksize). By slicing off the last 4 bytes instead of hashing 4 zero bytes, the rust implementation produces a different CRC32C value and will incorrectly fail validation for valid JBD2 descriptor blocks.
close_reason: Descriptor/revoke checksum verification now hashes the full block with a zeroed 4-byte tail, matching Linux jbd2 semantics; added regression coverage and passed targeted rch fmt/test/check/clippy.

title: [PERF] btrfs_ino_lookup scans entire fs-tree on every call — O(N) per ioctl with no caching
description:
# Performance concern from modes-of-reasoning pass

## Finding

`OpenFs::btrfs_resolve_inode_path` at crates/ffs-core/src/lib.rs:14447+ (added in f70dcca for bd-caex2) calls `self.walk_btrfs_fs_tree(cx)?` unconditionally on every invocation, walking every leaf entry in the subvolume fs-tree to build the `parent_by_child: BTreeMap` index. That means each BTRFS_IOC_INO_LOOKUP ioctl costs O(N) where N = total fs-tree items (inodes + dir entries + refs + xattrs + extents).

For a typical ext4/btrfs image the cost is negligible. For a large image (millions of inodes / tens of millions of items), a single ioctl can scan multi-megabyte indexes and iterate hundreds of thousands of entries. A caller issuing K ioctls imposes O(K*N) work — a slow query amplifier if a tool (e.g., `btrfs subvolume list`) iterates many inodes.

## Evidence

- Entry point: crates/ffs-core/src/lib.rs:14439 `fn btrfs_resolve_inode_path`
- Full-tree scan: `let entries = self.walk_btrfs_fs_tree(cx)?`
- Index built per call: BTreeMap<u64, (u64, Vec<u8>)> with one entry per INODE_REF item
- No cache, no early-termination (we build the full index before walking the chain even though we only need back-refs along the path)

## Fix directions (choose at implementation time)

1. **Early-exit walk.** Replace `walk_btrfs_fs_tree → build full index → walk` with a demand-driven search: at each step, search the fs-tree for items with key (objectid, INODE_REF, *) and stop at the first match. Each hop is an O(log N) btree lookup; total cost O(depth * log N).
2. **Per-subvolume cache** on `OpenFs` keyed on the fs-tree root generation. Invalidated on subvolume changes. Sub-μs per lookup after warmup.
3. **Batched multi-inode resolution.** If the caller is resolving multiple inodes in one request (e.g., a future `list_paths`), build the index once and amortize.

For V1, (1) is the simplest and keeps the code path stateless. (2) is the right long-term answer once we know the access pattern.

## Acceptance

- A synthetic image with 10K inodes completes a sequence of 100 INO_LOOKUP ioctls in bounded time (wall-clock cap, e.g., < 1s).
- Microbenchmark in `benches/` documents the per-ioctl cost.
- Unit tests from bd-caex2 continue to pass without regression.
close_reason: writable demand-driven walk + read-only cached index

title: e2compr: implement single-indirect block pointer read-modify-write
description:
Implement write support for indirect block pointers (i_block[12] -> pointer block -> data). This is the first level of indirect addressing and covers logical blocks 12 through 12+ptrs_per_block-1 (for 4K blocks: 12 through 1035).

STEPS:
1. When logical_block >= 12 and < 12 + ptrs_per_block:
   a. Read i_block[12]. If 0, allocate a new block, zero-fill it, write i_block[12].
   b. Read the indirect block.
   c. Write the target pointer at the correct 4-byte offset within it.
   d. Write the indirect block back to disk.
2. For freeing: when all pointers in the indirect block become 0, free the indirect block itself and set i_block[12] = 0.
3. Update set_indirect_block_ptr to dispatch to this path when lb >= 12 && lb < 12 + ptrs_per_block.

ACCEPTANCE: Compressed write works for files up to 4MB (1024 clusters * 4 blocks * 4K = 16MB with shift=2).
close_reason: Added write_block_ptr() with single-indirect allocation + read-modify-write. All callers updated. Max file size for e2compr writes extended from 48KB to ~4MB. 490 tests pass. Commit 18cd3d7.

title: Expand inode checksum metamorphic relations beyond field-content invariance
description:
The current inode-checksum metamorphic harness in crates/ffs-core/tests/metamorphic.rs (added in 785bb74) covers exactly one relation: the checksum is computed after zeroing the on-disk checksum bytes, so any prior garbage in those bytes does not affect the new checksum.

That is a useful but narrow property. The checksum function has several other behavioral contracts that protect against collision attacks and silent corruption, none of which are currently exercised:

- MR-INO: writing identical inode bytes to two different inode numbers must produce different on-disk checksums (so a per-inode forgery cannot be replayed at a different inode slot).
- MR-SEED: identical inode bytes + identical inode number with a different filesystem checksum seed must produce different checksums (so cross-filesystem tampering is detectable).
- MR-GEN: changing only the generation field flips the checksum (verifies generation participates in the digest, the standard kernel anti-replay mechanism for NFS handles).
- MR-DETECT: flipping any single bit outside the checksum field bytes must invalidate verify_inode_checksum (proves the digest covers the full inode body, not just a prefix).

Each of these maps directly onto a class of real-world ext4 vulnerabilities. Add them as proptest cases alongside the existing inode_checksum_field_content_invariance test.
close_reason: Added MR-INO, MR-SEED, MR-GEN, MR-DETECT proptests covering inode-number sensitivity, seed sensitivity, generation sensitivity, and single-bit-flip detection. All 5 metamorphic.rs cases pass (cargo test -p ffs-core --test metamorphic), clippy clean. sha=99e8455

title: Track: Foundations (ffs-types + ffs-error)

All other crates use these types (no local duplicates).

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text:
Why this track is first-class: filesystem bugs love unit confusion (bytes vs blocks) and inconsistent error semantics (silent truncation, wrong errno).

North star:
- ffs-types: strong newtypes + checked arithmetic so invariants are enforced at compile time.
- ffs-error: a stable user-facing error set + errno mapping for FUSE.

If this track is sloppy, every other track becomes untestable and unsafe.

title: ffs-core: Integrate ffs-journal replay into ext4 mount path + crash-recovery conformance
description:
Goal: make journal replay observable to users by wiring the new ffs-journal engine into the ext4 open/mount pipeline and proving behavior with deterministic crash-recovery tests.

Scope:
- Integrate replay_jbd2 invocation into ffs-core ext4 open flow behind compatibility-mode guard.
- Define journal-region discovery from ext4 superblock metadata (journal inode/device pointers) with explicit unsupported-mode errors.
- Surface replay metrics (ReplayStats) through engine diagnostics/logging path for auditable mount decisions.
- Add unit tests for integration glue (invocation conditions, error mapping, no-op behavior when journal absent).
- Add harness-level crash/recovery test flow that simulates committed and uncommitted journal transactions and verifies post-mount state.
- Add detailed structured logging lines for replay decisions and outcomes (scanned, committed, revoked, skipped).

Acceptance:
- Mount/open of ext4 fixture with journal transactions replays committed updates and ignores uncommitted/revoked updates.
- cargo test -p ffs-core and cargo test -p ffs-harness -- --nocapture include replay-path coverage with deterministic assertions.
- Logs contain machine-parseable replay summary fields suitable for CI diagnostics.

title: Refine oracle evidence with cross-oracle disagreement arbitration
description:
Supplemental refinement for the invariant and mounted differential oracle refinements. FrankenFS will have multiple truth sources: an executable invariant model, kernel differential runs, repair confidence artifacts, crash replay survivors, and explicit unsupported-scope records. The plan needs an arbitration contract for cases where those sources disagree, otherwise teams can cherry-pick the friendliest oracle.

Scope: define disagreement categories, owner routing, follow-up bead requirements, release-gate impact, and logs for cross-oracle conflicts. Arbitration should not decide filesystem truth by majority vote; it should preserve evidence, classify likely source of error, and fail closed for user-facing claims until the conflict is resolved or explicitly scoped out.

Required proof before close:
1. Unit tests cover disagreement categories: model_bug, kernel_baseline_issue, FrankenFS_product_bug, harness_bug, fixture_bug, unsupported_scope, host_capability_gap, repair_oracle_gap, and inconclusive_conflict.
2. Unit tests cover conflict inputs from invariant traces, mounted differential artifacts, repair confidence artifacts, crash replay survivor artifacts, and release-gate status rows, including stale or missing oracle evidence.
3. E2E/script smoke feeds fixture conflicts into the arbitrator and verifies output includes classification, controlling artifacts, blocked public claim, owning bead/remediation id, and reproduction commands.
4. Logs include arbitration id, source oracle ids, artifact hashes, normalized observation summary, selected classification, confidence/rationale, release-gate impact, follow-up bead id or non-goal reason, output path, and reproduction command.
5. Public readiness gates must fail closed for unresolved cross-oracle conflicts that affect mounted writes, mutating repair, writeback-cache, background scrub mutation, or data integrity claims.
close_reason: Completed cross-oracle arbitration validator, category/unit coverage, fixture E2E smoke, structured output checks, fail-closed readiness coverage, and RCH check/clippy validation.

title: Refine mounted crash recovery with checkpointed survivor oracle and partial-artifact preservation
description:
Supplemental refinement for bd-rchk0.3.3. Mounted crash/unmount/reopen tests need a checkpointed survivor oracle and partial-artifact preservation, otherwise a killed process can leave ambiguous evidence about what should have survived and which operation was last durably accepted.

Scope: define checkpoint artifacts for each mounted lifecycle scenario: pre-operation image hash, operation trace, fsync/fsyncdir checkpoints, crash/unmount point, expected survivor set, observed survivor set, recovery command, and partial log preservation. Use safe harness-level process control and temporary images only.

Required proof before close:
1. Unit/schema tests reject lifecycle scenarios missing checkpoint id, image hash, operation trace, fsync/fsyncdir boundary, crash/unmount point, expected survivor set, recovery command, cleanup policy, and partial-artifact policy.
2. Unit tests cover clean unmount, forced unmount when safely available, process termination during staged write, process termination after fsync, fsyncdir boundary, reopen after write, recovery classifier output, partial-log preservation, and unsafe process-control refusal.
3. Mounted E2E smoke emits checkpointed artifacts for at least clean unmount, reopen after fsync, and one safe termination/refusal scenario in the permissioned lane or a structured host skip.
4. Logs include checkpoint id, operation id, mount options, image hash before/after, fsync/fsyncdir calls, crash/unmount point, recovery command, expected/observed survivors, partial artifact paths, cleanup status, and reproduction command.
5. Experimental mounted write caveats cannot be relaxed unless lifecycle tests preserve enough partial artifacts to classify failures as product, harness, host limitation, or unsupported scope without rerunning immediately.
